title: Cooperative Coevolution versus Monolithic Evolutionary Search for Semi-Supervised Tabular Classification
comment: Accepted to be presented during the Genetic and Evolutionary Computation Conference 2026. July 13--17, 2026. San José, Costa Rica
summary: This paper studies semi-supervised tabular classification in the extreme low-label regime using lightweight base learners. The paper proposes a cooperative coevolutionary method (CC-SSL) that evolves (i) two feature-subset views and (ii) a pseudo-labeling policy, and compares it to a matched monolithic evolutionary baseline (EA-SSL) and three lightweight SSL baselines. Experiments on 25 OpenML datasets with labeled fractions {1%,5%,10%} evaluate test MacroF1 and accuracy, together with evolutionary and pseudo-label diagnostics. CC-SSL and EA-SSL achieve higher median test MacroF1 than the lightweight baselines, with the largest separations at 1% labeled data. Most CC-SSL vs. EA-SSL comparisons are statistical draws on final test performance. EA-SSL shows higher best-so-far fitness and higher diversity during search, while time-to-target is comparable and generations-to-target favors EA-SSL in several multiclass settings. Pseudo-label volume, ProbeDrop, and validation optimism show no significant differences between CC-SSL and EA-SSL under the shared protocol.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new method, CC-SSL, to improve semi-supervised tabular classification in low-label scenarios using lightweight learners. It benchmarks against comparable evolutionary and SSL methods, showing improved performance on 25 datasets.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of achieving robust semi-supervised classification performance in scenarios with extremely sparse labeled data using lightweight models.
AI.method: The proposed method, CC-SSL, leverages cooperative coevolution by evolving feature-subset views and pseudo-labeling strategies and comparing performance against baseline SSL methods and monolithic evolutionary algorithms.
AI.result: Results indicate that while CC-SSL and EA-SSL outperform lightweight SSL methods in median test MacroF1, CC-SSL and EA-SSL are generally comparable on final test performance. EA-SSL exhibits slightly better search efficacy and diversity in multiclass tasks.
AI.conclusion: CC-SSL provides an effective framework for semi-supervised tabular classification with minimal labeled data, though its performance is broadly equivalent to EA-SSL in final outcomes. Further investigation of these techniques could refine their utility in low-label scenarios.

title: Breaking Validity-Induced Boundaries to Expand Algorithm Search Space: A Two-Stage AST-Based Operator for LLM-Driven Automated Heuristic Evolution
summary:
Large Language Model (LLM) based automated heuristic design (AHD) has shown great potential in discovering efficient heuristics. Most existing LLM-AHD frameworks use semantic evolutionary operators that rely entirely on the LLM's pre-trained knowledge. These one-stage methods strictly require the generated code to be valid during the operation and often rely on a ``thought-code'' representation. We argue that this end-to-end generation fundamentally limits the exploration ability within the algorithm search space.
  In this paper, we propose a two-stage, structure-based evolutionary operator for LLM-AHD. In the first stage, our approach directly performs crossover and mutation on the Abstract Syntax Trees (ASTs) of the heuristic code, intentionally generating diverse but often invalid structural variants. In the second stage, the LLM is employed to repair these invalid heuristics into executable, high-quality code. Depending on the underlying framework, either the raw invalid variants or the repaired heuristics are integrated into the population to preserve potential structural patterns. We demonstrate that the proposed operator can significantly enhance the search ability of state-of-the-art LLM-AHD algorithms, such as EoH-S. Experimental results on the Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) and the Online Bin Packing Problem (OBP) show that our method effectively improves both optimization performance and convergence speed.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a two-stage evolutionary operator for LLM-based heuristic design, improving optimization efficiency and convergence speed in tasks like TSP and OBP.
AI.motivation: Existing one-stage LLM-AHD frameworks are constrained by their reliance on pre-trained knowledge and strict requirements for valid code generation, limiting exploration in the algorithm search space.
AI.method: The proposed method uses a two-stage approach: (1) structural crossover and mutation on ASTs to create diverse structural variants, and (2) LLM-based repair to convert invalid heuristics into executable code.
AI.result: The new approach enhances the search capabilities of state-of-the-art LLM-AHD models, improving performance and convergence on optimization tasks such as TSP and OBP.
AI.conclusion: Introducing a two-stage, structure-based operator unlocks untapped exploration potential, leading to superior results in automated heuristic design compared to traditional one-stage methods.

title: Fuzzy Encoding-Decoding to Improve Spiking Q-Learning Performance in Autonomous Driving
summary: This paper develops an end-to-end fuzzy encoder-decoder architecture for enhancing vision-based multi-modal deep spiking Q-networks in autonomous driving. The method addresses two core limitations of spiking reinforcement learning: information loss stemming from the conversion of dense visual inputs into sparse spike trains, and the limited representational capacity of spike-based value functions, which often yields weakly discriminative Q-value estimates. The encoder introduces trainable fuzzy membership functions to generate expressive, population-based spike representations, and the decoder uses a lightweight neural decoder to reconstruct continuous Q-values from spiking outputs. Experiments on the HighwayEnv benchmark show that the proposed architecture substantially improves decision-making accuracy and closes the performance gap between spiking and non-spiking multi-modal Q-networks. The results highlight the potential of this framework for efficient and real-time autonomous driving with spiking neural networks.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a fuzzy encoder-decoder model that enhances vision-based deep spiking Q-networks for autonomous driving, improving decision accuracy and performance.
AI.motivation: To address issues in spiking reinforcement learning, including information loss from sparse spike train conversions and weakly discriminative Q-value estimates.
AI.method: Developing an encoder-decoder system: the encoder uses fuzzy membership functions to create better spike representations; the decoder reconstructs Q-values from spiking outputs.
AI.result: The architecture significantly improves decision-making accuracy on the HighwayEnv benchmark, narrowing the gap between spiking and non-spiking Q-networks.
AI.conclusion: This framework enhances real-time, efficient decision-making in autonomous driving using spiking neural networks.

title: Full Feature Spiking Neural Network Simulation on Micro-Controllers for Neuromorphic Applications at the Edge
summary: Microcontroller units (MCU), which have an order of magnitude lower Size, Weight and Power (SWaP) than standard computers, makes them suitable for applications at the edge. Neuromorphic computing, which can realize low SWaP, relies on Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs). Until now, software based simulations of SNNs required GPU-based workstations, application classified core processors such as the ARM Cortex-A53, or specialized hardware like Intel's Loihi. In the present work, we demonstrate that the SNN simulator CARLsim can run its full feature set on a MCU RP2350 with 8 MB memory. We accomplished this by utilizing IEEE 16-bit float point numbers, which reduced memory requirements without loss of function. We were able to run the Synfire4 benchmark which comprises 1200 neurons. The accuracy was 97.5% compared to the standard single precision numbers. Furthermore, we show that CARLsim runs a Synfire4 benchmark scaled-down to 186 neurons on a MCU in real-time at only 20 mW. Compared to the smallest application class ARM processor used by Raspberry in their Pi Zero 2 W, our MCU implementation is five times more energy efficient for the SNN itself, and an order of magnitude better when compared to the complete SoC (MCU/CPU + Board).

AI.tldr: The paper demonstrates the ability to run a full-featured Spiking Neural Network (SNN) simulator, CARLsim, on a low-power MCU, achieving notable energy efficiency and retaining high accuracy.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to run SNN simulators on devices with lower Size, Weight, and Power (SWaP), facilitating neuromorphic computing applications at the edge.
AI.method: The authors reduced memory requirements using IEEE 16-bit floating point numbers and tested the SNN simulator CARLsim on an RP2350 MCU for its energy efficiency and accuracy.
AI.result: The simulation achieved 97.5% accuracy compared to standard precision, could handle a benchmark of 1200 neurons, and achieved real-time functionality for a smaller benchmark at just 20 mW, outperforming conventional ARM processors in energy efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Running SNNs on an MCU is feasible, with significant reductions in energy consumption and comparable performance to traditional processors, demonstrating the potential for energy-efficient neuromorphic computing at the edge.

title: Spike-driven Large Language Model
summary: Current Large Language Models (LLMs) are primarily based on large-scale dense matrix multiplications. Inspired by the brain's information processing mechanism, we explore the fundamental question: how to effectively integrate the brain's spiking-driven characteristics into LLM inference. Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) possess spike-driven characteristics, and some works have attempted to combine SNNs with Transformers. However, achieving spike-driven LLMs with billions of parameters, relying solely on sparse additions, remains a challenge in the SNN field. To address the issues of limited representational capacity and sparsity in existing spike encoding schemes at the LLM level, we propose SDLLM, a spike-driven large language model that eliminates dense matrix multiplications through sparse addition operations. Specifically, we use the plug-and-play gamma-SQP two-step spike encoding method to ensure that the quantization process aligns with the model's semantic space, mitigating representation degradation caused by binary spikes. Furthermore, we introduce bidirectional encoding under symmetric quantization and membrane potential clipping mechanisms, leading to spike trains with no or low firing counts dominating, significantly reducing the model's spike firing rate, while halving the number of time steps. Experimental results show that SDLLM not only significantly reduces inference costs but also achieves state-of-the-art task performance under the spike-based paradigm. For example, compared to previous spike-based LLMs, SDLLM reduces energy consumption by 7x and improves accuracy by 4.2%. Our model provides inspiration for the architecture design of the next generation of event-driven neuromorphic chips.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SDLLM, a novel spike-driven large language model that replaces dense matrix multiplications with sparse addition operations, resulting in performance gains and reduced energy consumption.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to integrate spiking-driven characteristics of the brain into large language model inference, overcoming limitations in representational capacity and sparsity in current spike encoding schemes.
AI.method: They propose SDLLM with a gamma-SQP two-step spike encoding method, bidirectional encoding, symmetric quantization, and membrane potential clipping mechanisms to optimize sparse spike-driven computation while reducing resource consumption.
AI.result: SDLLM reduces energy consumption by 7x, improves accuracy by 4.2%, and demonstrates state-of-the-art performance in spike-based language model tasks.
AI.conclusion: SDLLM achieves efficient and high-performing spike-based computation, suggesting a roadmap for innovation in event-driven neuromorphic hardware design.

title: Forge-UGC: FX optimization and register-graph engine for universal graph compiler
summary: We present Forge-UGC (FX Optimization and Register-Graph Engine for Universal Graph Compilation), a four-phase compiler for transformer deployment on heterogeneous accelerator hardware, validated on Intel AI Boost NPU. Existing frameworks such as OpenVINO and ONNX Runtime often use opaque compilation pipelines, limited pass-level visibility, and weak buffer management, which can lead to higher compilation cost and runtime overhead. Forge-UGC addresses this with a hardware-agnostic design that separates graph capture, optimization, intermediate representation lowering, and backend scheduling. Phase 1 captures graphs with torch.export at the ATen operator level, supporting modern transformer components such as rotary position embeddings, grouped-query attention, and SwiGLU without manual decomposition. Phase 2 applies six optimization passes: dead code elimination, common subexpression elimination, constant folding, attention fusion, operator fusion, and layout optimization, reducing graph node count by 14.2 to 21.9%. Phase 3 lowers the optimized graph into a typed intermediate representation with explicit virtual register assignments. Phase 4 performs liveness analysis, linear-scan buffer allocation, reducing peak buffer count by 30 to 48%, and device-affinity scheduling, reducing NPU-CPU transitions by 42 to 65%. Across six model families ranging from 125M to 8B parameters, evaluated on WikiText-103 and GLUE, Forge-UGC delivers 6.9 to 9.2x faster compilation than OpenVINO and ONNX Runtime, 18.2 to 35.7% lower inference latency, and 30.2 to 40.9% lower energy per inference. Fidelity is preserved, with max absolute logit differences below 2.1e-5 and KL divergence below 8.4e-9. We also introduce Fusion Gain Ratio, Compilation Efficiency Index, and per-pass execution profiling for systematic evaluation of NPU compilation pipelines.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Forge-UGC, a compiler designed to optimize transformer deployment on heterogeneous hardware, achieving faster compilation, lower latency, and reduced energy consumption compared to existing frameworks.
AI.motivation: Existing frameworks for deploying transformers on hardware like OpenVINO and ONNX Runtime have limitations in visibility, buffer management, and result in high compilation and runtime overhead. The authors aim to create a more efficient, hardware-agnostic compiler.
AI.method: Forge-UGC employs a four-phase process, including graph capture, optimization with six dedicated passes, intermediate representation lowering, and backend scheduling involving liveness analysis and buffer allocation.
AI.result: Forge-UGC demonstrates substantial performance improvements, delivering 6.9-9.2x faster compilation, 18.2-35.7% lower inference latency, and 30.2-40.9% lower energy usage while maintaining computational fidelity.
AI.conclusion: Forge-UGC is an effective and efficient compiler for transformer models, significantly outperforming existing solutions while introducing systematic evaluation metrics for compilation pipelines.

title: CoLLM: A Unified Framework for Co-execution of LLMs Federated Fine-tuning and Inference
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly adopted in edge intelligence to power domain-specific applications and personalized services, the quality and efficiency of the LLM post-training phase-including fine-tuning and inference, have become critical due to constrained resources. Although recent advances in federated parameter-efficient fine-tuning (FL PEFT) and low-latency inference have improved individual task performance, fine-tuning and inference are still handled as isolated workloads, which overlooks their interdependence and results in redundant deployments and delayed improvement in inference quality. To address these limitations, we introduce a new co-execution framework and instantiate it with CoLLM, a system that unifies FL PEFT and inference on shared edge replicas and model parameters. CoLLM addresses key challenges at both replica and cluster levels through: (1) an intra-replica model sharing mechanism that enables real-time model parameter reuse via unmerged inference and shadow adapter strategies; and (2) a two-timescale inter-replica coordination algorithm that adaptively balances fine-tuning and inference workloads to jointly optimize long-term model quality gains and short-term inference efficiency. Extensive evaluation across diverse LLMs and real-world traces show that CoLLM consistently outperforms state-of-the-art LLM systems, achieving up to 3x higher goodput, demonstrating its effectiveness in enabling seamless LLM post-training for edge intelligence.

AI.tldr: CoLLM introduces a unified framework for post-training large language models (LLMs) on edge devices, improving efficiency by combining fine-tuning and inference while addressing resource constraints.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to overcome inefficiencies in current separate handling of LLM fine-tuning and inference during post-training, which results in redundant deployments and delayed inference improvements.
AI.method: The authors propose CoLLM, a system using model sharing mechanisms and adaptive workload balancing strategies on edge replicas to unify federated parameter-efficient fine-tuning (FL PEFT) and inference.
AI.result: CoLLM demonstrates up to 3x higher goodput compared to existing systems, showcasing enhanced model quality and inference efficiency based on extensive tests with diverse LLMs and real-world scenarios.
AI.conclusion: CoLLM effectively addresses post-training inefficiencies for LLMs in edge intelligence, providing an integrated solution that boosts both model quality and inference speed.

title: Shift schema drift left: policy-aware compile-time contracts for typed JVM and Spark pipelines
comment: 7 pages, 2 figures, 1 table. Mechanism artifact paper with reproducible benchmarks. Code at https://github.com/vim89/compile-time-data-contracts
summary: Schema drift in data pipelines is often caught only when a job touches real data. Typed-Dataset layers close part of this gap but require wholesale adoption; table-level enforcement systems close another part but operate at write time against a stored schema. We present a small Scala 3 framework that occupies the seam: it proves producer-to-contract structural compatibility under explicit policies at compile time, derives Spark schemas from the same contract types, and re-checks the actual DataFrame schema at the sink boundary before write. The artifact fuses the compile-time witness with a policy-aware runtime comparator that adds a nested-collection-optionality check Spark's built-in comparators omit and implements structural subset semantics for backward- and forward-compatible field sets. Evaluation covers compile-time proofs, runtime policy tests, builder-path end-to-end tests, and reproducible benchmarks on two environments. This is a narrow, honest mechanism artifact; the broader claim that compile-time structural contracts deliver measurable productivity or reliability in practice is stated as motivation and left for future work.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a Scala 3 framework that ensures schema compatibility in data pipelines through compile-time checks and runtime validations.
AI.motivation: To address schema drift issues in data pipelines, which are often detected too late, the authors aim to enhance productivity and reliability by employing structural contracts during compile time.
AI.method: The framework uses compile-time structural compatibility proofs for schema validation, runtime comparators for policy-aware schema checking, and automatic derivation of Spark schemas from contract types.
AI.result: The artifact integrates these features to catch schema issues earlier and more robustly, demonstrated through compile-time proofs, runtime tests, end-to-end evaluations, and benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively addresses schema drift at both compile-time and runtime, though broader claims about its impact on productivity are reserved for future investigations.

title: HybridGen: Efficient LLM Generative Inference via CPU-GPU Hybrid Computing
summary: As modern LLMs support thousands to millions of tokens, KV caches grow to hundreds of gigabytes, stressing memory capacity and bandwidth. Existing solutions, such as KV cache pruning and offloading, alleviate these but underutilize hardware by relying solely on either GPU or CPU for attention computing, and considering yet limited CPU local memory for KV cache storage. We propose HybridGen, an efficient hybrid attention framework for long-context LLM inference. HybridGen enables CPU-GPU collaborative attention on systems with expanded tiered memory (e.g., CXL memory), addressing three key challenges: (1) multi-dimensional attention dependencies, (2) intensifying CPU-GPU load imbalance with longer sequences, and (3) NUMA penalty of tiered memories. HybridGen tackles these by introducing attention logit parallelism, a feedback-driven scheduler, and semantic-aware KV cache mapping. Experiments with three LLM models with eleven different sizes on three GPU platforms with a CXL-expanded memory show that HybridGen outperforms six state-of-the-art KV cache management methods by 1.41x--3.2x on average while maintaining superior accuracy.

AI.tldr: The authors present HybridGen, a CPU-GPU collaborative framework to optimize memory usage for long-context LLM inference, achieving substantial speed improvements while retaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: The motivation arises from the inefficiencies and hardware underutilization in managing KV cache storage for long-context LLM inference, especially as these caches grow to hundreds of gigabytes and stress memory capacity and bandwidth.
AI.method: HybridGen incorporates CPU-GPU collaborative attention with expanded tiered memory like CXL memory. It introduces attention logit parallelism, a feedback-driven scheduler, and semantic-aware KV cache mapping to balance workloads and resolve memory access challenges.
AI.result: HybridGen demonstrated 1.41x–3.2x speed improvement compared to six state-of-the-art KV cache management methods across various LLM sizes and GPU platforms, while maintaining accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively enhances memory utilization and computational efficiency for long-context LLMs, setting a new benchmark in KV cache management techniques.

title: BrainMem: Brain-Inspired Evolving Memory for Embodied Agent Task Planning
summary: Embodied task planning requires agents to execute long-horizon, goal-directed actions in complex 3D environments, where success depends on both immediate perception and accumulated experience across tasks. However, most existing LLM-based planners are stateless and reactive, operating without persistent memory and therefore repeating errors and struggling with spatial or temporal dependencies. We propose BrainMem(Brain-Inspired Evolving Memory), a training-free hierarchical memory system that equips embodied agents with working, episodic, and semantic memory inspired by human cognition. BrainMem continuously transforms interaction histories into structured knowledge graphs and distilled symbolic guidelines, enabling planners to retrieve, reason over, and adapt behaviors from past experience without any model fine-tuning or additional training. This plug-and-play design integrates seamlessly with arbitrary multi-modal LLMs and greatly reduces reliance on task-specific prompt engineering. Extensive experiments on four representative benchmarks, including EB-ALFRED, EB-Navigation, EB-Manipulation, and EB-Habitat, demonstrate that BrainMem significantly enhances task success rates across diverse models and difficulty subsets, with the largest gains observed on long-horizon and spatially complex tasks. These results highlight evolving memory as a promising and scalable mechanism for generalizable embodied intelligence.

AI.tldr: The paper presents BrainMem, a hierarchical memory system inspired by human cognition, addressing the limitations of stateless planners in embodied task planning.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of existing stateless, reactive LLM-based planners that fail to learn from past experiences and struggle with complex spatial and temporal dependencies.
AI.method: Proposes BrainMem, a training-free hierarchical memory system with working, episodic, and semantic memory that transforms interaction histories into structured knowledge and symbolic guidelines for improved task planning.
AI.result: BrainMem substantially improves task success rates across benchmarks, especially for long-horizon and spatially complex tasks, without requiring additional training or prompt engineering.
AI.conclusion: Evolving memory systems like BrainMem pave the way for scalable, generalizable intelligence in embodied agents, offering a plug-and-play solution that integrates effectively with multi-modal LLMs.

title: MLE-Toolbox: An Open-Source Toolbox for Comprehensive EEG and MEG Data Analysis
summary: MLE-Toolbox is a comprehensive open-source MATLAB toolbox for end-to-end analysis of magnetoencephalography (MEG) and electroencephalography (EEG) data. Inspired by widely used neuroimaging platforms such as Brainstorm and FieldTrip, it integrates the full analysis pipeline within a unified and user-friendly graphical interface (GUI), covering raw data import, preprocessing, source localization, functional connectivity, oscillatory analysis, and machine learning-based classification. The toolbox includes automated artifact rejection methods, including independent component analysis (ICA), signal-space projection (SSP), and signal-space separation (SSS); multiple source localization approaches, including minimum norm estimation (MNE), dynamic statistical parametric mapping (dSPM), standardized low-resolution brain electromagnetic tomography (sLORETA), and beamforming; multi-atlas parcellation with anatomical visualization; spectral power analysis with frequency-band brain mapping; phase-amplitude coupling (PAC); graph-theoretic brain network analysis; and integrated machine learning and deep learning classifiers. MLE-Toolbox also provides native interoperability with Brainstorm, FieldTrip, EEGLAB, and FreeSurfer, allowing researchers to build on established workflows while benefiting from additional automation, interactive visualization, and one-click academic report generation. Freely available for non-commercial use, MLE-Toolbox is designed to lower the barrier to rigorous, reproducible MEG/EEG research.

AI.tldr: MLE-Toolbox is an open-source MATLAB tool for complete MEG/EEG data analysis, integrating preprocessing, source localization, functional analysis, and machine learning into a unified GUI.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the need for an integrated, user-friendly tool for end-to-end analysis of MEG/EEG data to lower technical barriers and improve research reproducibility.
AI.method: The toolbox integrates capabilities like artifact rejection, source localization, connectivity analysis, and machine learning, with support for external neuroimaging platforms (e.g., Brainstorm, FieldTrip).
AI.result: MLE-Toolbox successfully consolidates comprehensive analysis workflows, supporting automation, visualization, and academic report generation, while interoperating with existing tools.
AI.conclusion: MLE-Toolbox simplifies MEG/EEG analysis, promotes reproducibility, and supports both beginner and advanced users with its broad functionality and intuitive design.

title: Ethics of Care for Software Engineering
comment: 2 pages, accepted at ICSE 2026 "Future of Software Engineering"
summary: Software engineering researchers repeatedly argue that the impact of their research on industrial practice, while desired and intended, is rarely achieved. We believe that a possible explanation of this phenomenon is the opposition of "caring about" and "caring for", based on the ethics of care. Indeed, while software engineering is collaborative and hence builds on interpersonal relations, researchers tend to care about "industrial impact" and "practitioners" in abstract terms, but rarely care for specific individuals working in specific contexts facing specific challenges. In this position paper, we advocate for the adoption of ethics of care in software engineering and discuss the implications of this adoption for researchers and conference organizers.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a gap between software engineering research and industrial practice due to the lack of focus on specific individuals and contexts, advocating for an ethics-of-care approach.
AI.motivation: To address the persistent issue of limited real-world impact of software engineering research on industrial practices.
AI.method: Analysis based on the ethics of care and a position advocating for a shift from abstract 'caring about' to concrete 'caring for' specific individuals and contexts.
AI.result: Implications and recommendations for researchers and conference organizers are discussed to bridge the gap between research and practice.
AI.conclusion: Software engineering research should adopt an ethics-of-care approach to ensure meaningful industrial impact by focusing on the needs of individuals and specific challenges in real-world contexts.

title: Fairness Constraints in High-Dimensional Generalized Linear Models
summary: Machine learning models often inherit biases from historical data, raising critical concerns about fairness and accountability. Conventional fairness interventions typically require access to sensitive attributes like gender or race, but privacy and legal restrictions frequently limit their use. To address this challenge, we propose a framework that infers sensitive attributes from auxiliary features and integrates fairness constraints into model training. Our approach mitigates bias while preserving predictive accuracy, offering a practical solution for fairness-aware learning. Empirical evaluations validate its effectiveness, contributing to the advancement of more equitable algorithmic decision-making.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a framework that infers sensitive attributes and incorporates fairness constraints in training machine learning models without explicitly requiring sensitive data.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the inherent biases in machine learning models caused by historical data while respecting privacy and legal restrictions on sensitive attributes.
AI.method: The paper proposes to infer sensitive attributes using auxiliary feature data and integrate fairness constraints directly into the model training process.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations demonstrate the approach effectively reduces bias without compromising predictive accuracy.
AI.conclusion: This framework offers a practical solution for fairness-aware machine learning, advancing equitable and accountable algorithmic decision-making processes.

title: A High-Accuracy Optical Music Recognition Method Based on Bottleneck Residual Convolutions
summary: Optical Music Recognition (OMR) aims to convert printed or handwritten music score images into editable symbolic representations. This paper presents an end-to-end OMR framework that combines residual bottleneck convolutions with bidirectional gated recurrent unit (BiGRU)-based sequence modeling. A convolutional neural network with ResNet-v2-style residual bottleneck blocks and multi-scale dilated convolutions is used to extract features that encode both fine-grained symbol details and global staff-line structures. The extracted feature sequences are then fed into a BiGRU network to model temporal dependencies among musical symbols. The model is trained using the Connectionist Temporal Classification loss, enabling end-to-end prediction without explicit alignment annotations. Experimental results on the Camera-PrIMuS and PrIMuS datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework. On Camera-PrIMuS, the proposed method achieves a sequence error rate (SeER) of $7.52\%$ and a symbol error rate (SyER) of $0.45\%$, with pitch, type, and note accuracies of $99.33\%$, $99.60\%$, and $99.28\%$, respectively. The average training time is 1.74~s per epoch, demonstrating high computational efficiency while maintaining strong recognition performance. On PrIMuS, the method achieves a SeER of $8.11\%$ and a SyER of $0.49\%$, with pitch, type, and note accuracies of $99.27\%$, $99.58\%$, and $99.21\%$, respectively. A fine-grained error analysis further confirms the effectiveness of the proposed model.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an end-to-end Optical Music Recognition (OMR) framework combining residual bottleneck convolutions and BiGRU for feature extraction and temporal modeling, achieving high accuracy and efficiency on music score datasets.
AI.motivation: To improve Optical Music Recognition systems by designing an end-to-end model that can efficiently and accurately process both fine details and global structures in music scores.
AI.method: The proposed framework employs ResNet-v2-style residual bottleneck convolutions with multi-scale dilated convolutions for feature extraction and a BiGRU network for temporal sequence modeling. Training uses Connectionist Temporal Classification loss to predict directly without alignment annotations.
AI.result: The model achieves high performance with a SeER of 7.52% and SyER of 0.45% on Camera-PrIMuS and a SeER of 8.11% and SyER of 0.49% on PrIMuS. It also shows high pitch, type, and note accuracy above 99% across datasets, with efficient training times.
AI.conclusion: The proposed OMR framework is effective and computationally efficient, capable of accurately recognizing musical scores with strong results on standard benchmarks.

title: BASIS: Balanced Activation Sketching with Invariant Scalars for "Ghost Backpropagation"
summary: The activation memory required for exact backpropagation scales linearly with network depth, context length, and feature dimensionality, forming an O(L * BN ) spatial bottleneck (where B is the sequence-batch cardinality and N is the feature dimension). This constraint historically throttles the scaling of deep neural networks. While randomized automatic differentiation attempts to mitigate this, it historically suffers from catastrophic variance. In this paper, we introduce BASIS (Balanced Activation Sketching with Invariant Scalars), an efficient backpropagation algorithm that fully decouples activation memory from the batch and sequence dimensions. BASIS propagates the exact error signal (dX) to preserve flawless gradient flow, but computes the weight updates (dW) using massively compressed rank-R tensors. To solve the foundational instability of sketched gradients, we propose two novel mechanisms: Balanced Hashing, which strictly eliminates off-diagonal collision variance, and Invariant Scalars, a principled bias-variance tradeoff that deterministically preserves the exact continuous energy norm of the spatial geometry. Theoretically, BASIS reduces activation memory to O(L * RN ) and heavily decreases the backward pass matrix-multiplication footprint. Empirically, training a GPT architecture for 50,000 steps validates our theoretical guarantees: at R = 32, BASIS achieves parity with (and marginally outperforms) exact backpropagation validation loss (6.575 vs. 6.616), acting as an implicit regularizer. Remarkably, the stabilized magnitude trajectory allows the model to converge smoothly even under extreme spatial compression (R = 1), proving the extreme robustness of the estimator. The code is available at https://github.com/VladimerKhasia/basis

AI.tldr: The paper introduces BASIS, a backpropagation algorithm that decreases activation memory requirements and improves scalability in deep neural networks through efficient tensor compression and stabilization techniques.
AI.motivation: The activation memory requirements of exact backpropagation scale unfavorably with network depth, context length, and feature dimensions, creating a spatial bottleneck that limits the scalability of deep neural networks. Previous mitigation attempts using randomized differentiation struggled with high variance.
AI.method: The authors propose BASIS, featuring two key innovations: Balanced Hashing to remove off-diagonal collision variance and Invariant Scalars to preserve the energy norm. BASIS computes exact error signals (dX) while compressing weight updates (dW) with rank-R tensors to reduce memory demand and computation cost.
AI.result: BASIS reduces activation memory to O(L * RN) without compromising model performance. Empirical results on a GPT model show validation loss parity and robustness, even under extreme tensor compression (R=1). The model converges smoothly and exhibits implicit regularization in these scenarios.
AI.conclusion: BASIS successfully overcomes backpropagation memory limitations through novel tensor compression and stabilization strategies, enabling efficient and scalable training of deep neural networks. It achieves competitive performance and robustness while significantly reducing memory and computational demands.

title: Multimodal Claim Extraction for Fact-Checking
summary: Automated Fact-Checking (AFC) relies on claim extraction as a first step, yet existing methods largely overlook the multimodal nature of today's misinformation. Social media posts often combine short, informal text with images such as memes, screenshots, and photos, creating challenges that differ from both text-only claim extraction and well-studied multimodal tasks like image captioning or visual question answering. In this work, we present the first benchmark for multimodal claim extraction from social media, consisting of posts containing text and one or more images, annotated with gold-standard claims derived from real-world fact-checkers. We evaluate state-of-the-art multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) under a three-part evaluation framework (semantic alignment, faithfulness, and decontextualization) and find that baseline MLLMs struggle to model rhetorical intent and contextual cues. To address this, we introduce MICE, an intent-aware framework which shows improvements in intent-critical cases.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the first benchmark for multimodal claim extraction from social media, evaluates current LLMs, and proposes a new intent-aware framework called MICE.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the gap in automated claim extraction methods that largely ignore the multimodal nature of misinformation in social media posts, which combine text and images.
AI.method: The authors created a benchmark dataset annotated with claims from fact-checkers, tested current multimodal LLMs using a three-part evaluation framework, and developed an intent-focused framework named MICE.
AI.result: State-of-the-art MLLMs struggle with rhetorical intent and context, but the proposed MICE framework shows improvements in these intent-critical scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal claim extraction is a challenging task for current models, but intent-aware approaches like MICE offer a promising way forward.

title: Governing the Agentic Enterprise: A Governance Maturity Model for Managing AI Agent Sprawl in Business Operations
summary: The rapid adoption of agentic AI in enterprise business operations--autonomous systems capable of planning, reasoning, and executing multi-step workflows--has created an urgent governance crisis. Organizations face uncontrolled agent sprawl: the proliferation of redundant, ungoverned, and conflicting AI agents across business functions. Industry surveys report that only 21% of enterprises have mature governance models for autonomous agents, while 40% of agentic AI projects are projected to fail by 2027 due to inadequate governance and risk controls. Despite growing acknowledgment of this challenge, academic literature lacks a formal, empirically validated governance maturity model connecting governance capability to measurable business outcomes. This paper introduces the Agentic AI Governance Maturity Model (AAGMM), a five-level framework spanning 12 governance domains, grounded in NIST AI RMF and ISO/IEC 42001 standards. We additionally propose a novel taxonomy of agent sprawl patterns--functional duplication, shadow agents, orphaned agents, permission creep, and unmonitored delegation chains--each linked to quantifiable business cost models. The framework is validated through 750 simulation runs across five enterprise scenarios and five governance maturity levels, measuring business outcomes including cost containment, risk incident rates, operational efficiency, and decision quality. Results demonstrate statistically significant differences (p < 0.001, large effect sizes d > 2.0) between all governance maturity levels, with Level 4-5 organizations achieving 94.3% lower sprawl indices, 96.4% fewer risk incidents, and 32.6% higher effective task completion rates compared to Level 1. The AAGMM provides practitioners with an actionable roadmap for governing autonomous AI agents while maximizing business returns.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the governance crisis caused by agentic AI proliferation in businesses, introduces the Agentic AI Governance Maturity Model (AAGMM), and validates it through simulations to improve outcomes.
AI.motivation: The paper seeks to address the challenge of uncontrolled agent sprawl in businesses due to the lack of mature governance models for autonomous AI systems, which risks project failures and inefficiencies.
AI.method: The authors developed the Agentic AI Governance Maturity Model (AAGMM) grounded in NIST AI RMF and ISO/IEC 42001 standards and validated it via 750 simulation runs across five enterprise scenarios.
AI.result: Simulations show that organizations at higher governance maturity levels (4-5) experienced significantly less agent sprawl, fewer risk incidents, and higher task completion rates compared to those at lower levels.
AI.conclusion: The AAGMM offers businesses a structured framework to govern autonomous AI agents effectively, reducing risks and increasing operational efficiency and decision-making quality.

title: Gradient-Free Continual Learning in Spiking Neural Networks via Inter-Spike Interval Regularization
summary: Continual learning, the ability to acquire new tasks sequentially without forgetting prior knowledge, is essential for deploying neural networks in dynamic real-world environments, from nuclear digital twin monitoring to grid-edge fault detection. Existing synaptic importance methods, such as Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC) and Synaptic Intelligence (SI), rely on gradient computation, making them incompatible with neuromorphic hardware that lacks backpropagation support. We propose ISI-CV, the first gradient-free synaptic importance metric for SNN continual learning, derived from the Coefficient of Variation (CV) of Inter-Spike Intervals (ISIs). Neurons that fire regularly (low CV) encode stable, task-relevant features and are protected from overwriting; neurons with irregular firing are permitted to adapt freely. ISI-CV requires only spike time counters and integer arithmetic, all of which are native to every neuromorphic chip. We evaluate on four benchmarks of increasing difficulty: Split-MNIST, Permuted-MNIST, Split-FashionMNIST, and Split-N-MNIST using real Dynamic Vision Sensor (DVS) event data. Across three seeds, ISI-CV achieves zero forgetting (AF = 0.000 +/- 0.000) on Split-MNIST and Split-FashionMNIST, near-zero forgetting on Permuted-MNIST (AF = 0.001 +/- 0.000), and the highest accuracy with the lowest forgetting on real neuromorphic DVS data (AA = 0.820 +/- 0.012, AF = 0.221 +/- 0.014). On N-MNIST, gradient-based methods produce unreliable importance estimates and perform worse than no regularization; ISI-CV avoids this failure by design.

AI.tldr: The authors propose ISI-CV, a gradient-free synaptic importance metric enabling Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) to perform continual learning on neuromorphic hardware, achieving high accuracy and minimal forgetting across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the challenge of enabling continual learning in neural networks for dynamic real-world environments, especially where neuromorphic hardware lacks support for gradient-dependent techniques.
AI.method: The authors introduce ISI-CV, a novel metric based on the Coefficient of Variation (CV) of Inter-Spike Intervals (ISIs). Neurons with stable firing patterns are protected from overwriting, while irregular neurons are free to adapt.
AI.result: ISI-CV achieves strong performance in continual learning on benchmarks such as Split-MNIST, Split-FashionMNIST, Permuted-MNIST, and real Dynamic Vision Sensor (DVS) data, significantly reducing forgetting compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: ISI-CV is a robust and hardware-compatible method that overcomes the limitations of gradient-based approaches, making it highly effective for neuromorphic hardware applications in continual learning scenarios.

title: EquivFusion: Unifying Hardware Equivalence Checking from Algorithms to Netlists via MLIR
summary: Ensuring functional consistency between high-level algorithmic models and low-level hardware implementations is a critical challenge, particularly as modern design flows increasingly span heterogeneous abstractions--from deep learning frameworks to hardware netlists. In this paper, we present EquivFusion, an end-to-end equivalence checking tool tailored for multi-modal circuit designs. Unlike traditional flows that rely on siloed tools or ad-hoc translation, EquivFusion leverages a verification-oriented MLIR lowering pipeline to unify diverse entry points, including PyTorch, C/C++, Chisel, Verilog, and gate-level netlists, into a common intermediate representation. This architecture enables automated, pairwise equivalence checking across diverse abstraction levels by rigorously translating designs into standard formal verification formats, i.e., SMT-LIB, BTOR2, AIGER. We demonstrate EquivFusion's feasibility to bridge the semantic gap between software specifications and hardware realizations, showcasing its effectiveness in facilitating "shift-left" formal verification for datapath-intensive hardware designs.

AI.tldr: EquivFusion is an equivalence checking tool designed for verifying consistency in multi-modal circuit designs, bridging high-level models and low-level hardware implementations using an MLIR-based pipeline.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the challenge of ensuring functional consistency between algorithmic models and hardware implementations, given the increasing heterogeneity of modern design flows.
AI.method: EquivFusion uses a verification-oriented MLIR lowering pipeline to convert diverse input formats, such as PyTorch, C/C++, Chisel, Verilog, and gate-level netlists, into a unified intermediate representation for formal equivalence checking.
AI.result: The study demonstrates EquivFusion's ability to perform automated pairwise equivalence checking across abstraction levels, converting designs into standardized formats like SMT-LIB, BTOR2, and AIGER.
AI.conclusion: EquivFusion offers a robust solution for bridging software specifications and hardware implementations, particularly enabling early-stage verification for datapath-intensive hardware designs.

title: Scene-Aware Latency Estimation for Microservices via Multi-Scale Graph Fusion
summary: Cloud-Native microservice architectures have become prevalent owing to their inherent flexibility and scalability properties. To satisfy service quality guarantees, cloud providers must implement efficient proactive autoscaling algorithms. However, effective proactive scaling critically depends on accurately estimating end-to-end latency under given resource quotas, which remains highly challenging. Existing methods struggle with the multi-hierarchical nature and dynamic operational contexts of microservice systems. They primarily employ single-scale modeling that fails to capture inherent organizational structures and lacks adaptability to varying workload types. To address these limitations, we propose MSGAF, a Multi-Scale Graph Adaptive Fusion framework with Scene-Aware Learning for microservice latency estimation. Our approach constructs hierarchical graph representations through learnable aggregation-based coarsening, capturing system behaviors across microscopic, mesoscopic, and macroscopic levels. The framework comprises three components: a system state encoding module transforming heterogeneous monitoring data into unified representations, a multi-scale graph adaptive fusion module leveraging graph attention networks for hierarchical feature extraction, and a scene-aware learning module employing specialized expert networks with dynamic weight allocation for context-specific estimation. Additionally, we design and implement a comprehensive non-intrusive monitoring system for real-time data collection. Extensive experiments on benchmark microservice applications demonstrate that MSGAF significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods across diverse operational scenarios, providing substantial improvements for cloud-native performance optimization.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the challenges in proactive autoscaling for cloud-native microservice architectures by introducing MSGAF, a framework for multi-scale graph-based latency estimation, surpassing existing methods.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to improve proactive autoscaling efficiency by developing accurate latency estimation methods for microservices, overcoming limitations in current techniques.
AI.method: The method involves creating MSGAF, which builds hierarchical graph representations, integrates adaptive fusion, and employs scene-aware learning for effective latency predictions across diverse workloads.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrated MSGAF's superior latency estimation performance compared to state-of-the-art methods across benchmark microservice systems.
AI.conclusion: MSGAF enhances cloud-native performance optimization by addressing organizational complexities and dynamic contexts in microservice environments, improving proactive scaling.

title: Partitioning Unstructured Sparse Tensor Algebra for Load-Balanced Parallel Execution
summary: Sparse tensor algebra is challenging to efficiently parallelize due to the irregular, data-dependent, and potentially skewed structure of sparse computation. We propose the first partitioning algorithm that provably load balances the computation of any sparse tensor algebra expression across parallel execution units. Our algorithm generalizes parallel merging algorithms to any number of operands, and to multi-dimensional, hierarchical sparse data structures. We implement our algorithm within an existing sparse tensor algebra compilation framework to automatically generate parallel sparse tensor algebra kernels that target multi-core CPUs and GPUs. We show that our generated code is competitive with hand-implemented parallelization strategies used by vendor libraries like Intel MKL and NVIDIA cuSPARSE (geo-means of $0.73$--$3.4\times$) and \textsc{Taco} (geo-means of $1.0$--$2.4\times$), and significantly outperforms general-purpose strategies for sparse tensor expressions where specialized algorithms have not been developed (geo-means of $2.0$--$6.4\times$).

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a partitioning algorithm for efficient parallelization of sparse tensor algebra, outperforming existing strategies and libraries.
AI.motivation: Sparse tensor algebra is hard to parallelize due to irregular, data-dependent structure and skewness, necessitating a robust solution.
AI.method: A partitioning algorithm generalizing parallel merging for multi-dimensional sparse data structures is implemented within an existing framework to auto-generate parallel kernels targeting CPUs and GPUs.
AI.result: Generated code shows 0.73–3.4× competitiveness with Intel MKL and NVIDIA cuSPARSE and 1.0–2.4× over Taco, with 2.0–6.4× improvement over unspecialized general-purpose methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed algorithm efficiently load balances parallel sparse tensor computations and consistently outperforms specialized and general-purpose strategies.

title: BranchBench: Aligning Database Branching with Agentic Demands
summary:
Branchable databases are evolving from developer tools to infrastructure for agentic workloads characterized by speculative mutations and non-linear state exploration. Traditional RDBMS mechanisms such as nested transactions do not provide the persistent isolation and concurrent branch management required by autonomous agents, and recent "zero-copy" designs make different trade-offs whose impact on agentic workloads remains unclear.
  To clarify this space, we present BranchBench, a benchmark for evaluating branchable relational DBMSes under agentic exploration. We characterize five representative workloads-agentic software engineering, failure reproduction, data curation, MCTS, and simulation-and design parameterized macrobenchmarks that execute branch-mutate-evaluate loops to reflect these workloads, along with microbenchmarks that isolate branch lifecycle costs. We evaluate state of the art systems including Neon, DoltgreSQL, Tiger Data, Xata, and PostgreSQL baselines, and find a fundamental tension: systems optimized for fast branching suffer up to 5-4000x slower reads as branches deepen, while systems optimized for fast data operations incur 25-1500x higher branch creation and switching latency. Further, no current system supports the representative workloads at scale. These results highlight the need for branch-native DBMSes designed specifically for agentic exploration.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces BranchBench, a benchmark for assessing branchable relational DBMSes in agentic exploration workloads. Current DBMS systems exhibit trade-offs—fast branching leads to slower reads as branches deepen, while systems optimized for data operations show high branch latency.
AI.motivation: Existing RDBMS mechanisms are inadequate for agentic workloads that involve speculative mutations and non-linear exploration. Recent solutions have trade-offs whose impacts on such workloads remain unclear.
AI.method: The researchers developed BranchBench, including parameterized macrobenchmarks and microbenchmarks to evaluate branch lifecycle costs across five workload types. They tested various DBMS systems such as Neon, DoltgreSQL, and others.
AI.result: Evaluation showed that systems optimized for fast branching lead to slower reads as branches deepen. Conversely, systems designed for fast data operations experience significantly higher latency in branch creation and switching.
AI.conclusion: There is a need for branch-native DBMSes capable of scaling effectively for agentic exploration workloads, as current systems fail to support these workloads efficiently.

title: Interdisciplinary Workshop on Mechanical Intelligence: Summary Report
summary: This report provides a summary of the outcomes of the Interdisciplinary Workshop on Mechanical Intelligence held in 2024. Mechanical Intelligence (MI) represents the phenomenon that novel structural features of material/biological/robotic systems can encode intelligence through responsiveness, adaptivity, memory, and learning in the mechanical structure itself. This is in contrast to computational intelligence, wherein the intelligence functions occur through electrical signaling and computer code. The two-day workshop was held at NSF headquarters on May 30-31 and included 38 invited academic researcher participants, and 8 program officers from the NSF. The workshop was structured around active small and large group discussions in groups of 4-5 and 9-10 with the goal of addressing topical questions on MI. Working groups entered notes into shared presentation slides for each discussion session and presented their outcomes in a final presentation on the last day. Here we summarize the overall outcomes of the workshop.

AI.tldr: The abstract summarizes a workshop on Mechanical Intelligence (MI) held in 2024, focusing on intelligence encoded in mechanical structures instead of electrical/computational systems.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to explore a novel paradigm where material, biological, or robotic systems exhibit intelligence via mechanical features like responsiveness, adaptivity, and learning, distinct from computational methods.
AI.method: The workshop featured 38 academic researchers, 8 NSF program officers, group discussions (small and large), and collaborative outcome documentation in presentation slides.
AI.result: The report offers a consolidated summary of discussions, addressing key topical questions related to MI.
AI.conclusion: This workshop represents a foundational step toward understanding and researching Mechanical Intelligence, potentially broadening horizons of intelligence beyond computational approaches.

title: Causality as a Minimum Energy Principle
comment: Published in IEEE Engineering in Medicine and Biology Society Annual Conference (EMBC) 2026
summary: Classical causal models, such as Granger causality and structural equation modeling, are largely restricted to acyclic interactions and struggle to represent cyclic and higher-order dynamics in complex networks. We introduce a causal framework grounded in a variational principle, interpreting causality as directional energy flow from high- to low-energy states along network connections. Using Hodge theory, network flows are decomposed into dissipative components and a persistent harmonic component that captures stable cyclic interactions. Applied to resting-state fMRI connectivity, our variational framework reveals robust cyclic causal patterns that are not detected by conventional causal models, highlighting the value of variational principles for causality.

AI.tldr: Classical causal models have limitations in representing cyclic and complex interactions in networks. The paper introduces a variational causal framework using Hodge theory to uncover cyclic patterns in networks, demonstrated with resting-state fMRI data.
AI.motivation: Classical models struggle with cyclic and higher-order dynamics, especially in complex networks. This paper aims to address these limitations by proposing a framework to reveal causality involving cycles.
AI.method: The proposed causal framework is grounded in a variational principle, where causality is interpreted as directional energy flow. Hodge theory is used to decompose network flows into dissipative and harmonic components to capture cyclic causal interactions.
AI.result: The variational framework successfully detects cyclic causal patterns in resting-state fMRI connectivity, which conventional causal models fail to identify.
AI.conclusion: Variational principles, combined with Hodge theory, provide a powerful approach to uncovering complex cyclic causal interactions in networks, offering insights beyond classical causal modeling.

title: Results-Actionability Gap: Understanding How Practitioners Evaluate LLM Products in the Wild
summary: How do product teams evaluate LLM-powered products? As organizations integrate large language models (LLMs) into digital products, their unpredictable nature makes traditional evaluation approaches inadequate, yet little is known about how practitioners navigate this challenge. Through interviews with nineteen practitioners across diverse sectors, we identify ten evaluation practices spanning informal 'vibe checks' to organizational meta-work. Beyond confirming four documented challenges, we introduce a novel fifth we call the results-actionability gap, in which practitioners gather evaluation data but cannot translate findings into concrete improvements. Drawing on patterns from successful teams, we contribute strategies to bridge this gap, supporting practitioners' formalization journey from ad-hoc interpretive practices (e.g., vibe checks) toward systematic evaluation. Our analysis suggests these interpretive practices are necessary adaptations to LLM characteristics rather than methodological failures. For HCI researchers, this presents a research opportunity to support practitioners in systematizing emerging practices rather than developing new evaluation frameworks.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how product teams evaluate large language model (LLM)-powered products, highlighting challenges and strategies to systematize evaluation practices.
AI.motivation: Organizations need effective ways to evaluate LLMs in products due to their unpredictable nature and limitations of traditional evaluation methods.
AI.method: The study interviewed 19 practitioners from various sectors to identify evaluation practices and challenges.
AI.result: Identifies ten evaluation practices, introduces the 'results-actionability gap,' and offers strategies to systematize informal practices like 'vibe checks.'
AI.conclusion: Interpretive practices are adaptive responses to LLM characteristics, not failures, and researchers should support practitioners in formalizing these methodologies rather than creating new ones.

title: A Mechanism Study of Delayed Loss Spikes in Batch-Normalized Linear Models
summary: Delayed loss spikes have been reported in neural-network training, but existing theory mainly explains earlier non-monotone behavior caused by overly large fixed learning rates. We study one stylized hypothesis: normalization can postpone instability by gradually increasing the effective learning rate during otherwise stable descent. To test this hypothesis at theorem level, we analyze batch-normalized linear models. Our flagship result concerns whitened square-loss linear regression, where we derive explicit no-rising-edge and delayed-onset conditions, bound the waiting time to directional onset, and show that the rising edge self-stabilizes within finitely many iterations. Combined with a square-loss decomposition, this yields a concrete delayed-spike mechanism in the whitened regime. For logistic regression, under highly restrictive active-margin assumptions, we prove only a supporting finite-horizon directional precursor in a knife-edge regime, with an optional appendix-only loss lower bound under an extra non-degeneracy condition. The paper should therefore be read as a stylized mechanism study rather than a general explanation of neural-network loss spikes. Within that scope, the results isolate one concrete delayed-instability pathway induced by batch normalization.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how batch normalization can delay instability in deep learning loss landscapes and provides theoretical insights using linear models.
AI.motivation: To provide a theoretical understanding of delayed loss instabilities observed in neural-network training, which existing theories fail to fully explain.
AI.method: The authors analyze stylized models (linear regression and logistic regression) with normalization, deriving conditions and mechanisms for delayed instability.
AI.result: Explicit conditions and bounds are derived for delayed-onset behavior in linear regression, with supporting results for logistic regression under restrictive assumptions.
AI.conclusion: The work highlights a concrete pathway through which batch normalization can delay instability, contributing to understanding loss spikes but remains a stylized case study.

title: From Inheritance to Saturation: Disentangling the Evolution of Visual Redundancy for Architecture-Aware MLLM Inference Acceleration
comment: 16 pages, 14 figures, plus appendix, accepted at ACL 2026
summary: High-resolution Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) face prohibitive computational costs during inference due to the explosion of visual tokens. Existing acceleration strategies, such as token pruning or layer sparsity, suffer from severe "backbone dependency", performing well on Vicuna or Mistral architectures (e.g., LLaVA) but causing significant performance degradation when transferred to architectures like Qwen. To address this, we leverage truncated matrix entropy to uncover a universal three-stage inference lifecycle, decoupling visual redundancy into universal Intrinsic Visual Redundancy (IVR) and architecture-dependent Secondary Saturation Redundancy (SSR). Guided by this insight, we propose HalfV, a framework that first mitigates IVR via a unified pruning strategy and then adaptively handles SSR based on its specific manifestation. Experiments demonstrate that HalfV achieves superior efficiency-performance trade-offs across diverse backbones. Notably, on Qwen25-VL, it retains 96.8\% performance at a 4.1$\times$ FLOPs speedup, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art baselines. Our code is available at https://github.com/civilizwa/HalfV.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the computational inefficiencies of High-resolution Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) by introducing HalfV, a framework that efficiently mitigates visual redundancy across different architectures while maintaining high performance.
AI.motivation: The computational cost of inference for MLLMs is challenging due to the explosion of visual tokens, and existing acceleration strategies are highly dependent on specific architectures, limiting their generalizability.
AI.method: The authors used truncated matrix entropy to identify a universal three-stage inference lifecycle for MLLMs, separating visual redundancy into Intrinsic Visual Redundancy (IVR) and architecture-dependent Secondary Saturation Redundancy (SSR). They proposed the HalfV framework to reduce IVR via unified pruning and adaptively manage SSR.
AI.result: HalfV achieved a 4.1× speedup in FLOPs on the Qwen25-VL model while retaining 96.8% of its performance, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art methods in efficiency-performance trade-offs across multiple architectures.
AI.conclusion: The proposed HalfV framework effectively mitigates visual redundancy in MLLMs, delivering superior efficiency and generalizability compared to existing methods across various backbone architectures.

title: UniMamba: A Unified Spatial-Temporal Modeling Framework with State-Space and Attention Integration
summary: Multivariate time series forecasting is fundamental to numerous domains such as energy, finance, and environmental monitoring, where complex temporal dependencies and cross-variable interactions pose enduring challenges. Existing Transformer-based methods capture temporal correlations through attention mechanisms but suffer from quadratic computational cost, while state-space models like Mamba achieve efficient long-context modeling yet lack explicit temporal pattern recognition. Therefore we introduce UniMamba, a unified spatial-temporal forecasting framework that integrates efficient state-space dynamics with attention-based dependency learning. UniMamba employs a Mamba Variate-Channel Encoding Layer enhanced with FFT-Laplace Transform and TCN to capture global temporal dependencies, and a Spatial Temporal Attention Layer to jointly model inter-variate correlations and temporal evolution. A Feedforward Temporal Dynamics Layer further fuses continuous and discrete contexts for accurate forecasting. Comprehensive experiments on eight public benchmark datasets demonstrate that UniMamba consistently outperforms state-of-the-art forecasting models in both forecasting accuracy and computational efficiency, establishing a scalable and robust solution for long-sequence multivariate time-series prediction.

AI.tldr: UniMamba proposes a unified framework integrating state-space dynamics and attention mechanisms for multivariate time series forecasting, achieving superior accuracy and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Challenges in forecasting multivariate time series due to complex temporal dependencies and cross-variable interactions, and limitations of existing methods in balancing accuracy with computational efficiency.
AI.method: Combines state-space dynamics with attention mechanisms, utilizing FFT-Laplace Transform, Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCN), and Spatial Temporal Attention Layer to capture dependencies. Implements a Feedforward Temporal Dynamics Layer for context fusion.
AI.result: UniMamba consistently outperformed state-of-the-art models in accuracy and computational efficiency across eight public datasets.
AI.conclusion: UniMamba provides a scalable and robust solution for long-sequence multivariate time-series forecasting, bridging gaps in current methods.

title: Cross-Family Speculative Decoding for Polish Language Models on Apple~Silicon: An Empirical Evaluation of Bielik~11B with UAG-Extended MLX-LM
summary: Speculative decoding accelerates LLM inference by using a small draft model to propose k candidate tokens for a target model to verify. While effective for same-tokenizer pairs on high-bandwidth GPUs, its applicability to cross-family pairs with mismatched tokenizers and consumer-grade unified memory remains underexplored. We extend the MLX-LM framework with Universal Assisted Generation (UAG) to enable cross-tokenizer speculative decoding on Apple Silicon. We evaluate Bielik 11B-Instruct (Mistral-based) as the target model, paired with three draft models: Bielik 1.5B (Qwen-based with custom tokenizer), Qwen2.5-1.5B, and Llama 3.2-1B. Experiments on three Polish-language datasets (Wikipedia, pl_alpaca, synthetic) use draft lengths k in {2, 4, 6} to compare naive and context-aware token translation. Results show: (1) context-aware translation consistently improves acceptance rates across all configurations; (2) the Polish-specialized Bielik 1.5B achieves lower acceptance than general-purpose Qwen2.5 and Llama 3.2 drafters; (3) throughput on Apple Silicon is content-dependent, reaching 1.7x speedup for structured text but failing for varied instructions; and (4) verification cost on unified memory does not amortize as theory predicts because both models are memory-bandwidth bound, making sequential drafting expensive relative to batched verification. We propose a hardware-aware speedup formula and characterize conditions for cross-family speculative decoding on Apple Silicon. This is the first systematic evaluation of cross-family speculative decoding for Polish LLMs and the first empirical study of UAG-based decoding on unified memory architectures.

AI.tldr: This study explores cross-tokenizer speculative decoding for large language models (LLMs) on Apple Silicon, evaluating its effectiveness using a new Universal Assisted Generation (UAG) framework.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to enhance the efficiency of speculative decoding for LLM inference in cross-family, cross-tokenizer contexts, especially on consumer-grade hardware like Apple Silicon.
AI.method: The authors extend the MLX-LM framework with Universal Assisted Generation (UAG) and test it with a Polish LLM (target) and three draft models under various tokenization contexts. Naive and context-aware token translations are compared, and hardware constraints are analyzed.
AI.result: Key findings include: (1) context-aware token translation improves acceptance rates, (2) specialized models have lower acceptance than general-purpose drafters, (3) Apple Silicon throughput is content-sensitive, and (4) verification costs are unexpectedly high due to memory bandwidth limitations.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights challenges and proposes a hardware-aware speedup formula for cross-tokenizer speculative decoding on Apple Silicon, identifying contexts where it is effective and offering insights for future LLM deployment on such architectures.

title: Semantic Consensus: Process-Aware Conflict Detection and Resolution for Enterprise Multi-Agent LLM Systems
summary: Multi-agent large language model (LLM) systems are rapidly emerging as the dominant architecture for enterprise AI automation, yet production deployments exhibit failure rates between 41% and 86.7%, with nearly 79% of failures originating from specification and coordination issues rather than model capability limitations. This paper identifies Semantic Intent Divergence--the phenomenon whereby cooperating LLM agents develop inconsistent interpretations of shared objectives due to siloed context and absent process models--as a primary yet formally unaddressed root cause of multi-agent failure in enterprise settings. We propose the Semantic Consensus Framework (SCF), a process-aware middleware comprising six components: a Process Context Layer for shared operational semantics, a Semantic Intent Graph for formal intent representation, a Conflict Detection Engine for real-time identification of contradictory, contention-based, and causally invalid intent combinations, a Consensus Resolution Protocol using a policy--authority--temporal hierarchy, a Drift Monitor for detecting gradual semantic divergence, and a Process-Aware Governance Integration layer for organizational policy enforcement. Evaluation across 600 runs spanning three multi-agent frameworks (AutoGen, CrewAI, LangGraph) and four enterprise scenarios demonstrates that SCF is the only approach to achieve 100% workflow completion--compared to 25.1% for the next-best baseline--while detecting 65.2% of semantic conflicts with 27.9% precision and providing complete governance audit trails. The framework is protocol-agnostic and compatible with MCP and A2A communication standards.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the Semantic Consensus Framework (SCF) to address Semantic Intent Divergence in multi-agent large language model systems for AI automation, achieving superior workflow completion, semantic conflict detection, and auditing capabilities.
AI.motivation: The paper is motivated by the high failure rates (41%-86.7%) in enterprise multi-agent LLM systems caused primarily by specification and coordination issues, particularly the phenomenon of Semantic Intent Divergence.
AI.method: The authors propose the Semantic Consensus Framework (SCF), a middleware system with six components designed to formalize intent representation, detect conflicts, resolve disagreements, monitor semantic drift, and integrate governance policies.
AI.result: SCF achieved 100% workflow success in evaluations compared to 25.1% for the next-best system, detected 65.2% of semantic conflicts with 27.9% precision, and offered complete governance audit trails.
AI.conclusion: SCF proves to be a robust solution for addressing coordination challenges in multi-agent LLM systems, significantly improving reliability, governance, and conflict resolution in enterprise AI workflows.

title: Impact of leaky dynamics on predictive path integration accuracy in recurrent neural networks
summary: Experimental evidence indicates that intrinsic temporal dynamics operating across multiple time scales are closely associated with the emergence of periodic spatial activity of increasing complexity. However, how information encoded in grid-like firing patterns for path integration is processed across these intrinsic time scales remains unclear. To address this question, we introduce adaptive time scales through a leak term in recurrent neural networks (RNNs), forming leaky RNNs discretized from the continuous attractors of firing rate models. Our results demonstrate that leaky RNNs substantially enhance the emergence of well-defined and highly regular hexagonal firing patterns. Compared with vanilla RNNs lacking a leak term, the trained leaky RNNs produce more accurate position estimates while generating reliable grid-cell-like representations. Furthermore, under identical noise conditions, leaky RNNs consistently exhibit more stable dynamics and better-defined grid structures. The learned dynamics also give rise to stable torus attractors with a clear central hole, supporting robust and regular grid-like activity. Overall, the dynamic leak acts as a low-pass filtering mechanism that protects recurrent neural circuitry from noise, stabilizes network dynamics, and improves path-integration accuracy in recurrent neural networks.

AI.tldr: This study introduces leaky RNNs to model grid-cell-like activity and shows that including a leak term improves path integration, stability, and noise resilience.
AI.motivation: To understand how temporal dynamics across multiple scales influence grid-cell-like firing patterns and path integration.
AI.method: Incorporated leaky time-scale dynamics into recurrent neural networks (leaky RNNs) and analyzed their effects on grid-cell-like activity compared to vanilla RNNs.
AI.result: Leaky RNNs generate more accurate position estimates, stable dynamics, and regular hexagonal firing patterns under noise conditions.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic leak acts as a low-pass filter, enhancing path-integration accuracy, stabilizing dynamics, and improving noise robustness in recurrent neural networks.

title: E2AFS: Energy-Efficient Approximate Floating Point Square Rooter for Error Tolerant Computing
summary: Floating-point square-root computation is a power- and delay-critical operation in edge-AI, signal-processing, and embedded systems. Conventional implementations typically rely on multipliers or iterative pipelines, resulting in increased hardware complexity, switching activity, and energy consumption. This work presents E2AFS, a lightweight and fully multiplier-free floating-point square-root architecture optimized for energy-efficient computation. By reducing logic depth and minimizing switching activity, the proposed design achieves substantial improvements in hardware efficiency and performance. FPGA implementation on an Artix-7 device demonstrates that E2AFS achieves the lowest dynamic power (7.63 mW), the shortest critical-path delay (4.639 ns), and the minimum power-delay product (35.39 pJ) compared to existing ESAS and CWAHA architectures. Error evaluation using multiple accuracy metrics, together with graphical analysis, shows that E2AFS closely approximates the exact square-root function with consistently low deviation. Application-level validation in Sobel edge detection and K-means color quantization further confirms its suitability for low-power real-time edge and embedded platforms.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces E2AFS, a lightweight multiplier-free architecture for efficient floating-point square-root computation, demonstrating superior power efficiency and performance compared to traditional designs.
AI.motivation: To address the power and delay inefficiencies in conventional floating-point square-root computation for edge and embedded systems, which rely on hardware-intensive approaches.
AI.method: Developed a multiplier-free design optimized to reduce logic depth and switching activity, implemented on an FPGA for benchmarking against existing architectures.
AI.result: E2AFS achieves the lowest dynamic power (7.63 mW), shortest critical-path delay (4.639 ns), and best power-delay product (35.39 pJ), with high accuracy compared to current architectures.
AI.conclusion: E2AFS is validated as an energy-efficient and accurate solution for edge-AI and embedded applications, making it suitable for real-time tasks like Sobel edge detection and K-means color quantization.

title: Spot-and-Scoot: Peeking Into Spot Instance Availability
