Specifically, we introduce a Topology Extractor to capture the global topological representation of the point cloud, which is integrated into local geometry features and enables robust category-level structural representation. Simultaneously, we propose a Mamba-based Global Semantic Aggregator that injects semantics priors into keypoints to enhance their expressiveness and leverages multiple TwinMamba blocks to model long-range dependencies for more effective global feature aggregation. Extensive experiments on three benchmark datasets (REAL275, CAMERA25, and HouseCat6D) demonstrate that TSM-Pose outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the challenge of robust generalization in category-level object pose estimation by introducing TSM-Pose, utilizing topology-aware learning and semantic aggregation.
AI.motivation: Current methods struggle to generalize to unseen instances due to weak feature extraction and semantic keypoint modeling deficiencies.
AI.method: The proposed framework integrates a Topology Extractor to capture structural representations and a Mamba-based Global Semantic Aggregator for enhanced keypoint expressiveness and global feature aggregation.
AI.result: Empirical validations show TSM-Pose achieves superior performance over state-of-the-art techniques across multiple benchmark datasets.
AI.conclusion: TSM-Pose effectively addresses generalization challenges through innovative modeling of topology and semantics in object pose estimation.

title: Decomposing the Depth Profile of Fine-Tuning
comment: 25 pages incl. 13 appendix pages. 1 figure, 19 tables
summary: Fine-tuning adapts pretrained networks to new objectives. Whether the resulting depth profile of representational change reflects an intrinsic property of the model or the magnitude of gradient flow has not been tested directly. We measure this profile across 240 fine-tuning runs spanning 15 models in four architecture families (encoder and decoder transformers, a state-space model, and an RNN) at scales from 125M to 6.9B parameters. Representational change concentrates in output-proximal layers in every standard-training run except one. We apply a per-layer control that equalizes $\|ΔW\|/\|W\|$ across layers after each optimizer step. Under this control, the profile persists in some conditions and collapses in others. At 125M--350M, sequential-block architectures (BERT, OPT, GPT-2) retain the slope across tested objectives while parallel-block architectures (Pythia, CodeGen) retain it only for causal-language-modeling objectives. This architectural distinction narrows at 1.3B--1.4B, where both block types show positive equal-step slopes for CausalLM. Under standard training, profile shape is described by two additional axes: steepness tracks a training-free objective distance at initialization, and profile width is dominated by architecture. We treat the locality gradient, the depthwise slope of representational change, as a composite phenomenon whose components are scale-dependent.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the depth profile of representational change during fine-tuning across various models and architectures, revealing dependencies on both architecture types and scale.
AI.motivation: The study aims to understand how representational adjustments during fine-tuning are distributed across network layers and whether this is governed by intrinsic properties of models or external factors like gradient flow.
AI.method: The authors analyzed 240 fine-tuning runs across 15 models of four architecture families, applying a controlled experiment to measure layer-wise representational changes under standard and modified training conditions.
AI.result: Representational changes were predominantly concentrated in output-proximal layers across all standard-training runs, with variations observed depending on architecture type, scale, and training objectives.
AI.conclusion: Fine-tuning depth profiles are influenced by architecture, parameter scale, and training dynamics, suggesting representational change is a multifaceted phenomenon tied to both intrinsic and extrinsic factors.

title: Cat-DPO: Category-Adaptive Safety Alignment
summary: Aligning large language models with human preferences must balance two competing goals: responding helpfully to legitimate requests and reliably refusing harmful ones. Most preference-based safety alignment methods collapse safety into a single scalar that is applied uniformly to every preference pair. The result is a model that looks safe on average but stays relatively unsafe on a minority of harm categories. We cast safety alignment as a per-category constrained optimization problem and derive Cat-DPO, a direct-preference-optimization algorithm with a separate adaptive safety margin for each harm category. The margin tightens when the model still produces unsafe responses on a category and relaxes once the model catches up, so the training signal tracks each category's current difficulty rather than averaging under one global rate. Across two LLM backbones and six preference-learning baselines, Cat-DPO iimproves aggregate helpfulness and harmlessness and compresses per-category safety variance and the best-to-worst gap, offering a drop-in per-category refinement of direct preference safety alignment.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Cat-DPO, a method for safer alignment of large language models by optimizing safety on a per-category basis rather than collapsing safety into a single measure.
AI.motivation: Ensuring large language models align with human preferences while balancing helpfulness and harmlessness, especially addressing safety variance in harm categories.
AI.method: The authors implement a per-category constrained optimization strategy called Cat-DPO, using adaptive safety margins that adjust based on a model’s performance for each harm category.
AI.result: Cat-DPO achieves better overall helpfulness and harmlessness, reduces variance in safety across harm categories, and narrows the performance gap from best-to-worst harmfulness categories.
AI.conclusion: This method effectively refines safety alignment in large language models on a granular, per-category level and integrates seamlessly with existing preference-learning approaches.

title: Evolutionary Negative Module Pruning for Better LoRA Merging
summary: Merging multiple Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) experts into a single backbone is a promising approach for efficient multi-task deployment. While existing methods strive to alleviate interference via weight interpolation or subspace alignment, they rest upon the implicit assumption that all LoRA matrices contribute constructively to the merged model. In this paper, we uncover a critical bottleneck in current merging paradigms: the existence of $\textit{negative modules}$ -- specific LoRA layers that inherently degrade global performance upon merging. We propose $\textbf{E}$volutionary $\textbf{N}$egative $\textbf{M}$odule $\textbf{P}$runing ($\textbf{ENMP}$), a plug-and-play LoRA pruning method to locate and exclude these detrimental modules prior to merging. By leveraging an evolutionary search strategy, ENMP effectively navigates the discrete, non-differentiable landscape of module selection to identify optimal pruning configurations. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that ENMP consistently boosts the performance of existing merging algorithms, achieving a new state-of-the-art across both language and vision domains. Code is available at https://github.com/CaoAnda/ENMP-LoRAMerging.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies a key bottleneck in merging Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) layers due to detrimental 'negative modules' and introduces ENMP, a pruning method to eliminate them, improving performance in multi-task deployment.
AI.motivation: The motivation of the paper arises from the need to improve the performance of models when merging multiple LoRA experts. Current approaches do not account for specific modules that degrade performance, indicating a gap in the merging process.
AI.method: The method proposed, ENMP, is a LoRA pruning technique that uses an evolutionary search strategy to identify and exclude "negative modules" before merging, improving global performance.
AI.result: ENMP demonstrates consistent performance improvements across language and vision tasks, surpassing state-of-the-art results in LoRA merging.
AI.conclusion: ENMP provides a robust, plug-and-play solution to enhance the merging of LoRA experts by pruning degrading modules, making it a valuable tool for efficient multi-task model deployment.

title: Training-inference input alignment outweighs framework choice in longitudinal retinal image prediction
summary: Quantitative prediction of future retinal appearance from longitudinal imaging would support clinical decisions in progressive macular disease that currently rely on qualitative comparison or scalar progression scores. Recent methods have moved toward increasing generative complexity, but whether this complexity is necessary for slowly progressing retinal disease is unclear. We tested this through a controlled comparison of five conditioning configurations sharing one architecture and training dataset, spanning standard conditional diffusion, inference-aligned stochastic training, and deterministic regression. In our evaluation, aligning the training and inference input distributions produced large gains (delta-SSIM +0.082, SSIM +0.086, both p < 0.001), while the choice among aligned frameworks did not significantly affect any primary metric. Task-entropy and posterior-concentration analyses, replicated on two fundus autofluorescence (FAF) platforms, provided a mechanistic account: the predictable component of inter-visit change is small relative to time-invariant acquisition variability, leaving stochastic sampling with little width to exploit. Guided by these findings, we developed TRU (Temporal Retinal U-Net), a deterministic direct-regression model with continuous time-delta conditioning and multi-scale history aggregation. We evaluated TRU on 28,902 eyes across three imaging platforms: a mixed-disease Optos FAF cohort (9,942 eyes), zero-shot transfer to Stargardt macular dystrophy on Optos (288 eyes) and Heidelberg Spectralis (125 eyes), and a boundary evaluation on Cirrus en-face fundus images from a glaucoma cohort (18,547 eyes). TRU matched or exceeded delta-SSIM, SSIM, and PSNR in every FAF cohort against three state-of-the-art benchmarks, and its advantage grew monotonically with available history length.

AI.tldr: The study examined predictive models for progressive macular disease and proposed a deterministic regression model (TRU) that achieved superior performance across various retinal imaging platforms.
AI.motivation: To enhance clinical decisions in progressive macular diseases by quantitatively predicting future retinal appearance using longitudinal imaging.
AI.method: The study compared five conditioning configurations (including diffusion models and deterministic regression) using a shared architecture and dataset, followed by analyses to understand task entropy and posterior concentration. It also introduced TRU, a deterministic direct-regression model incorporating continuous time-delta conditioning and multi-scale history aggregation.
AI.result: Training aligned with inference inputs showed substantial gains in predictive metrics like SSIM. TRU performed consistently well across 28,902 eyes from three imaging datasets and outperformed state-of-the-art benchmarks, especially as imaging history length increased.
AI.conclusion: Generative complexity may be unnecessary for slowly progressing retinal diseases; deterministic models like TRU can outperform existing benchmarks by focusing on history aggregation and aligned input frameworks.

title: Do LLM-derived graph priors improve multi-agent coordination?
summary: Multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) is crucial for AI systems that operate collaboratively in distributed and adversarial settings, particularly in multi-domain operations (MDO). A central challenge in cooperative MARL is determining how agents should coordinate: existing approaches must either hand-specify graph topology, rely on proximity-based heuristics, or learn structure entirely from environment interaction; all of which are brittle, semantically uninformed, or data-intensive. We investigate whether large language models (LLMs) can generate useful coordination graph priors for MARL by using minimal natural language descriptions of agent observations to infer latent coordination patterns. These priors are integrated into MARL algorithms via graph convolutional layers within a graph neural network (GNN)-based pipeline, and evaluated on four cooperative scenarios from the Multi-Agent Particle Environment (MPE) benchmark against baselines spanning the full spectrum of coordination modeling, from independent learners to state-of-the-art graph-based methods. We further ablate across five compact open-source LLMs to assess the sensitivity of prior quality to model choice. Our results provide the first quantitative evidence that LLM-derived graph priors can enhance coordination and adaptability in dynamic multi-agent environments, and demonstrate that models as small as 1.5B parameters are sufficient for effective prior generation.

AI.tldr: The study explores using large language models (LLMs) to generate coordination graph priors for multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) scenarios, demonstrating that even compact LLMs significantly enhance performance in cooperative environments.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses key challenges in multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), where existing coordination methods are either rigid, overly simplistic, or resource-intensive. The authors are motivated by the potential of language-driven methods to provide more versatile and efficient ways to model agent coordination.
AI.method: Using large language models (LLMs), the authors generate graph priors based on natural language descriptions of agent observations. These priors are fed into graph convolutional layers in graph neural network (GNN)-enabled MARL algorithms and evaluated on benchmark scenarios.
AI.result: The experiments on four Multi-Agent Particle Environment (MPE) scenarios show that LLM-derived graph priors improve coordination and adaptability in MARL settings. The study demonstrates effective performance using LLMs as small as 1.5 billion parameters.
AI.conclusion: The findings confirm that LLMs can effectively generate semantically rich coordination priors, enhancing the robustness and efficiency of MARL algorithms while reducing dependence on pre-specified or learned graph structures.

title: RoTRAG: Rule of Thumb Reasoning for Conversation Harm Detection with Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary: Detecting harmful content in multi turn dialogue requires reasoning over the full conversational context rather than isolated utterances. However, most existing methods rely mainly on models internal parametric knowledge, without explicit grounding in external normative principles. This often leads to inconsistent judgments in socially nuanced contexts, limited interpretability, and redundant reasoning across turns. To address this, we propose RoTRAG, a retrieval augmented framework that incorporates concise human written moral norms, called Rules of Thumb (RoTs), into LLM based harm assessment. For each turn, RoTRAG retrieves relevant RoTs from an external corpus and uses them as explicit normative evidence for turn level reasoning and final severity classification. To improve efficiency, we further introduce a lightweight binary routing classifier that decides whether a new turn requires retrieval grounded reasoning or can reuse existing context. Experiments on ProsocialDialog and Safety Reasoning Multi Turn Dialogue show that RoTRAG consistently improves both harm classification and severity estimation over competitive baselines, with an average relative gain of around 40% in F1 across benchmark datasets and an average relative reduction of 8.4% in distributional error, while reducing redundant computation without sacrificing performance.

AI.tldr: RoTRAG is a framework that enhances harm detection in multi-turn dialogues by incorporating external moral norms and optimizing reasoning efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing harm detection methods in dialogues often lack grounding in external normative principles, leading to inconsistent judgments, poor interpretability, and redundant reasoning.
AI.method: RoTRAG integrates human-written moral norms (Rules of Thumb) into harm detection by retrieving relevant rules for contextual reasoning and severity classification. A binary routing classifier optimizes computational efficiency by deciding the necessity of retrieval.
AI.result: RoTRAG outperforms competitive baselines in harm classification and severity estimation by around 40% in F1 scores and reduces distributional error by 8.4%. It also minimizes redundant computation.
AI.conclusion: The incorporation of external moral norms and the optimization of reasoning processes improve both the effectiveness and efficiency of harm detection in dialogues.

title: Contrastive Attribution in the Wild: An Interpretability Analysis of LLM Failures on Realistic Benchmarks
summary: Interpretability tools are increasingly used to analyze failures of Large Language Models (LLMs), yet prior work largely focuses on short prompts or toy settings, leaving their behavior on commonly used benchmarks underexplored. To address this gap, we study contrastive, LRP-based attribution as a practical tool for analyzing LLM failures in realistic settings. We formulate failure analysis as \textit{contrastive attribution}, attributing the logit difference between an incorrect output token and a correct alternative to input tokens and internal model states, and introduce an efficient extension that enables construction of cross-layer attribution graphs for long-context inputs. Using this framework, we conduct a systematic empirical study across benchmarks, comparing attribution patterns across datasets, model sizes, and training checkpoints. Our results show that this token-level contrastive attribution can yield informative signals in some failure cases, but is not universally applicable, highlighting both its utility and its limitations for realistic LLM failure analysis. Our code is available at: https://aka.ms/Debug-XAI.

AI.tldr: The paper studies LRP-based contrastive attribution methods to analyze failures of LLMs in benchmark scenarios, presenting a scalable cross-layer attribution framework.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap in understanding LLM failures in realistic benchmark settings, which previous work overlooks by focusing on short prompts or toy scenarios.
AI.method: Contrastive attribution, based on the logit difference between incorrect output tokens and correct ones, paired with an efficient extension for cross-layer graph constructions for long-context inputs.
AI.result: Empirical analysis shows that contrastive token-level attribution can provide useful signals for debugging LLM failures, but its efficacy is context-dependent and not universally applicable.
AI.conclusion: While useful in specific scenarios, the proposed method has limitations in general applicability for debugging LLM failures, emphasizing selective utility.

title: Self-Reasoning Agentic Framework for Narrative Product Grid-Collage Generation
summary: Narrative-driven product photography has become a prevalent paradigm in modern marketing, as coherent visual storytelling helps convey product value and establishes emotional engagement with consumers. However, existing image generation methods do not support structured narrative planning or cross-panel coordination, often resulting in weak storytelling and visual incoherence. In practice, narrative product photography is commonly presented as multi-grid collages, where multiple views or scenes jointly communicate a product narrative. To ensure visual consistency across grids and aesthetic harmony of the overall composition, we generate the collage as a single unified image rather than composing independently synthesized panels. We propose a self-reasoning agentic framework for narrative product grid collage generation. Given a product packshot and its name, the system first constructs a Product Narrative Framework that explicitly represents the product's identity, usage context, and situational environment, and translates it into complementary grids governed by a shared visual style. Constraint-aware prompts are then compiled and fed to a generation model that synthesizes the collage jointly. The generated output is evaluated on both content validity and photography quality, with explicit gates determining whether to proceed or refine. When evaluation fails, the system performs failure attribution and applies targeted refinement, enabling progressive improvement through iterative self-reflection. Experiments demonstrate that our framework consistently improves aesthetic quality, narrative richness, and visual coherence, compared to direct prompting baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework to generate narrative-driven product photography collages as unified images, improving visual coherence and storytelling quality.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of structured narrative planning and visual coherence in current image generation methods for marketing-oriented product photography.
AI.method: A self-reasoning framework uses a Product Narrative Framework to design complementary grid layouts for narrative collages, coupled with iterative refinement for improvement.
AI.result: Experiments show enhanced aesthetic quality, storytelling richness, and cross-panel visual consistency compared to baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed system is effective in generating visually consistent product photography collages that improve marketing communication.

title: Demystifying the unreasonable effectiveness of online alignment methods
summary: Iterative alignment methods based on purely greedy updates are remarkably effective in practice, yet existing theoretical guarantees of \(O(\log T)\) KL-regularized regret can seem pessimistic relative to their empirical performance. In this paper, we argue that this mismatch arises from the regret criterion itself: KL-regularized regret conflates the statistical cost of learning with the exploratory randomization induced by the softened training policy. To separate these effects, we study the traditional temperature-zero regret criterion, which evaluates only the top-ranked response at inference time. Under this decision-centric notion of performance, we prove that standard greedy online alignment methods, including online RLHF and online DPO, achieve constant \((O(1))\) cumulative regret. By isolating the cost of identifying the best response from the stochasticity induced by regularization, our results provide a sharper theoretical explanation for the practical superb efficiency of greedy alignment.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that standard greedy online alignment methods achieve constant regret under a decision-centric criterion, challenging existing pessimistic theoretical guarantees.
AI.motivation: To address the mismatch between empirical performance of greedy alignment methods and theoretical KL-regularized regret estimates.
AI.method: The authors evaluate alignment methods using a temperature-zero regret criterion, focusing on top-ranked inference responses.
AI.result: It is proven that greedy methods exhibit constant cumulative regret under decision-centric evaluation, separating statistical learning costs from regularization-induced exploratory randomization.
AI.conclusion: Greedy online alignment methods are theoretically justified as efficient, explaining their practical effectiveness.

title: Calibrated? Not for Everyone: How Sexual Orientation and Religious Markers Distort LLM Accuracy and Confidence in Medical QA
summary: Safe clinical deployment of Large Language Models (LLMs) requires not only high accuracy but also robust uncertainty calibration to ensure models defer to clinicians when appropriate. Our paper investigates how social descriptors of a patient (specifically sexual orientation and religious affiliation) distort these uncertainty signals and model accuracy. Evaluating nine general-purpose and biomedical LLMs on 2,364 medical questions and their counterfactual variants, we demonstrate that identity markers cause a "calibration crisis". "Homosexual" markers consistently trigger performance drops, and intersectional identities produce idiosyncratic, non-additive harms to calibration. Moreover, a clinician-validated case study in an open-ended generation setting confirms that these failures are not an artifact of the multiple-choice format. Our results demonstrate that the presence of social identity cues does not merely shift predictions; it affects the reliability of confidence signals, posing a significant risk to equitable care and safe deployment in confidence-based clinical workflows.

AI.tldr: The paper explores how patient identity markers like sexual orientation and religious affiliation impact the calibration and confidence of large language models (LLMs) in clinical applications, revealing biases that undermine safety and equity in medical settings.
AI.motivation: Large Language Models (LLMs) require precise confidence calibration to ensure they defer to clinical decision-makers when necessary. However, social identity descriptors might distort their performance, raising risks in equitable medical care.
AI.method: The study evaluates nine LLMs on 2,364 medical questions and corresponding counterfactual alternatives, analyzing the impact of patient identity markers such as sexual orientation and religion. Calibration and performance under these conditions are quantified, and a clinician-validated open-ended case study serves as an additional evaluation.
AI.result: Social identity cues, notably 'homosexual,' led to performance and calibration reliability issues. Intersectional identities introduced additional idiosyncratic biases, suggesting serious risks in deployment. Failures persisted across question types, including open-ended clinical cases.
AI.conclusion: Patient identity markers can distort LLMs' confidence and calibration, creating risks for inequitable and unsafe application in clinical workflows. Addressing these biases is critical for their practical deployment in healthcare.

title: When Vision-Language Models Judge Without Seeing: Exposing Informativeness Bias
summary: The reliability of VLM-as-a-Judge is critical for the automatic evaluation of vision-language models (VLMs). Despite recent progress, our analysis reveals that VLM-as-a-Judge often pays limited attention to the image when making decisions. Instead, they often blindly favor the more informative answer, even when they can recognize it conflicts with the image content. We call this problem informativeness bias, which significantly undermines judge reliability. To address it, we propose BIRCH (Balanced Informativeness and CoRrectness with a Truthful AnCHor), a judging paradigm that first corrects inconsistencies with the image content in candidate answers, and then compares the answers against this corrected version. This shifts the judge's focus from informativeness to image-grounded correctness. Experiments on multiple models and benchmarks show that BIRCH reduces informativeness bias by up to 17%, resulting in performance gains of up to 9.8%. Our work reveals an overlooked but fundamental flaw in current VLM-as-a-Judge systems and highlights the need for more principled designs.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a fundamental flaw called informativeness bias in vision-language model judges and proposes a paradigm called BIRCH to improve reliability by focusing on image-grounded correctness.
AI.motivation: The paper is motivated by the issue of informativeness bias in vision-language models, where judges prioritize informative answers over image content, undermining decision reliability.
AI.method: The proposed method, BIRCH, corrects inconsistencies in candidate answers with image content and then evaluates the answers based on this corrected version.
AI.result: BIRCH reduces informativeness bias by up to 17% and improves performance by up to 9.8% across various models and benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights the deficiency in current VLM-as-a-Judge systems caused by informativeness bias and demonstrates the effectiveness of the BIRCH paradigm for improving reliability.

title: Hyperspectral Unmixing Hierarchies
summary:
Unmixing reveals the spatial distribution and spectral details of different constituents, called endmembers, in a hyperspectral image. Because unmixing has limited ground truth requirements, can accommodate mixed pixels, and is closely tied to light propagation, it is a uniquely powerful tool for analyzing hyperspectral images. However, spectral variability inhibits unmixing performance, the proper way to determine the number of endmembers is ambiguous, and the clarity of the endmembers degrades as more are included. Hierarchical structure is a possible solution to all three problems.
  Here, hierarchical unmixing is defined by imposing a hierarchical abundance sum constraint on Deep Nonnegative Matrix Factorization. Binary Linear Unmixing Tactile Hierarchies (BLUTHs) solve the hierarchical unmixing problem with a simple network architecture. Sparsity modulation unmixing growth tailors the topology of a BLUTH to each scene. The structure imposed by BLUTHs allows endmembers with varying levels of spectral contrast to be revealed, mitigating the challenge of spectral variability.
  The performance of BLUTHs exceeds state-of-the-art unmixing algorithms on laboratory scenes, particularly with regard to abundance estimation, while their performance remains competitive on remote sensing scenes. In addition, ocean color unmixing by BLUTHs is demonstrated on hyperspectral scenes from the HYPSO and PACE satellites.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a hierarchical unmixing method for hyperspectral images, called BLUTHs, which outperforms state-of-the-art algorithms, particularly in abundance estimation.
AI.motivation: To address challenges in hyperspectral image unmixing, such as spectral variability, ambiguity in determining the number of endmembers, and degradation in endmember clarity when more are included.
AI.method: The proposed method uses Binary Linear Unmixing Tactile Hierarchies (BLUTHs), based on Deep Nonnegative Matrix Factorization, with a hierarchical abundance sum constraint and sparsity modulation unmixing growth tailored to each scene.
AI.result: BLUTHs demonstrate superior performance compared to other unmixing algorithms on laboratory scenes, show competitive results on remote sensing scenes, and succeed in ocean color unmixing in hyperspectral satellite data.
AI.conclusion: BLUTHs address spectral variability and improve unmixing performance, providing a versatile and effective tool for hyperspectral image analysis.

title: Guardrails in Logit Space: Safety Token Regularization for LLM Alignment
summary: Fine-tuning well-aligned large language models (LLMs) on new domains often degrades their safety alignment, even when using benign datasets. Existing safety alignment techniques primarily focus on pretraining, leaving fine-tuned models vulnerable to behavioral shifts. In this work, we introduce safety token regularization (STR), a lightweight method designed to preserve safety properties during fine-tuning. Our approach identifies salient tokens from rejection templates of well-aligned models and constrains their associated logits during training, preventing the loss of critical safety behaviors. Unlike reinforcement learning or preference optimization methods, STR requires minimal additional computation and seamlessly integrates with parameter-efficient fine-tuning techniques such as LoRA. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that our approach achieves safety performance on par with state-of-the-art methods, while preserving task-specific utility and requiring minimal implementation overhead. Furthermore, we show that safety token regularization enhances training stability and overall performance beyond safety considerations alone. This work offers a practical and readily deployable strategy for continual safety alignment in fine-tuned LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Safety Token Regularization (STR) to preserve safety alignment during fine-tuning of large language models.
AI.motivation: Fine-tuning large language models on new domains often compromises their safety alignment, and existing techniques inadequately address this issue during the fine-tuning stage.
AI.method: STR identifies salient safety-related tokens and constrains their logits during training to maintain safety properties, requiring minimal computational resources and integrating with techniques like LoRA.
AI.result: STR matches state-of-the-art safety performance while maintaining task utility, improves training stability, and demands minimal implementation effort.
AI.conclusion: STR is a practical, lightweight, and effective strategy for maintaining safety alignment in fine-tuned LLMs, enhancing both safety and training outcomes.

title: A Universal Avoidance Method for Diverse Multi-branch Generation
summary: Modern generative models still lack human-level creativity, particularly in multi-branch diversity. Prior approaches to address this problem often incur heavy computation or strong dependency on model architecture. Therefore, we introduce UAG(Universal Avoidance Generation), a model-agnostic and computationally efficient generation strategy that penalizes similarity among previously generated outputs. Thus, UAG can enhance multi-branch diversity across both diffusion and transformer models, with minimal additional computation. In experiments, our method achieves up to 1.9 times higher diversity, runs 4.4 times faster, and requires only 1/64 of the FLOPs compared to state-of-the-art methods. The full code is https://anonymous.4open.science/r/2026_ACL_Universal/.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces UAG, a lightweight and general-purpose strategy to increase diversity in generative models without heavy computational costs.
AI.motivation: Modern generative models lack human-level creativity, particularly in producing diverse outputs across multiple branches.
AI.method: Proposes Universal Avoidance Generation (UAG), a model-independent strategy that penalizes similarity across generated outputs to improve diversity. UAG is computationally efficient and does not require changes to model architecture.
AI.result: UAG enhances diversity up to 1.9 times more while being 4.4 times faster and using substantially lower computational resources (1/64 FLOPs) compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: UAG successfully balances diversity and efficiency, making it an advantageous approach for both diffusion and transformer generative models.

title: Prompt Optimization Enables Stable Algorithmic Collusion in LLM Agents
summary: LLM agents in markets present algorithmic collusion risks. While prior work shows LLM agents reach supracompetitive prices through tacit coordination, existing research focuses on hand-crafted prompts. The emerging paradigm of prompt optimization necessitates new methodologies for understanding autonomous agent behavior. We investigate whether prompt optimization leads to emergent collusive behaviors in market simulations. We propose a meta-learning loop where LLM agents participate in duopoly markets and an LLM meta-optimizer iteratively refines shared strategic guidance. Our experiments reveal that meta-prompt optimization enables agents to discover stable tacit collusion strategies with substantially improved coordination quality compared to baseline agents. These behaviors generalize to held-out test markets, indicating discovery of general coordination principles. Analysis of evolved prompts reveals systematic coordination mechanisms through stable shared strategies. Our findings call for further investigation into AI safety implications in autonomous multi-agent systems.

AI.tldr: This paper examines whether prompt optimization can drive emergent collusive behaviors in LLM agents in market simulations and shows that such optimization leads to advanced, generalizable tacit coordination strategies.
AI.motivation: The research addresses the gap in understanding how prompt optimization, an emerging paradigm, influences autonomous LLM agents' behavior, particularly their propensity for algorithmic collusion in market settings.
AI.method: The authors introduce a meta-learning framework where LLM agents interact in duopoly markets. An LLM-based meta-optimizer iteratively refines shared strategic prompts to examine emergent behaviors.
AI.result: Meta-prompt optimization facilitates stable tacit collusion among agents, yielding significantly better coordination than baseline models. These strategies generalize well across different market scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights the potential of optimized prompts to evoke systematic and generalizable collusive strategies among AI agents, raising important AI safety concerns in autonomous multi-agent systems.

title: UGD: An Unsupervised Geometric Distance for Evaluating Real-world Noisy Point Cloud Denoising
comment: to be published in IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
summary: Point cloud denoising is a fundamental and crucial challenge in real-world point cloud applications. Existing quantitative evaluation metrics for point cloud denoising methods are implemented in a supervised manner, which requires both the denoised point cloud and the corresponding ground-truth clean point cloud to compute a representative geometric distance. This requirement is highly problematic in real-world scenarios, where ground-truth clean point clouds are often unavailable. In this paper, we propose a simple yet effective unsupervised geometric distance (UGD) for real-world noisy point cloud denoising, calculated solely from noisy point clouds. The core idea of UGD is to learn a patch-wise prior model from a set of clean point clouds and then employ this prior model as the ground-truth to quantify the degradation by measuring the geometric variations of the denoised point cloud. To this end, we first learn a pristine Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) with extracted patch-wise quality-aware features from a set of pristine clean point clouds by a patch-wise feature extraction network, which serves as the ground-truth for the quantitative evaluation. Then, the UGD is defined as the weighted sum of distances between each patch of the denoised point cloud and the learned pristine GMM model in the patch space. To train the employed patch-wise feature extraction network, we propose a self-supervised training framework through multi-task learning, which includes pair-wise quality ranking, distortion classification, and distortion distribution prediction. Quantitative experiments with synthetic noise confirm that the proposed UGD achieves comparable performance to supervised full-reference metrics. Moreover, experimental results on real-world data demonstrate that the proposed UGD enables unsupervised evaluation of point cloud denoising methods based exclusively on noisy point clouds.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an unsupervised geometric distance (UGD) metric for evaluating point cloud denoising methods using only noisy point clouds, without needing ground-truth clean data.
AI.motivation: Point cloud applications struggle with denoising challenges, especially in the absence of ground-truth clean data for traditional supervised evaluation metrics.
AI.method: The UGD relies on learning a patch-wise prior model (using a Gaussian Mixture Model) from clean point clouds and measuring geometric variations against this model, combined with a self-supervised multi-task training framework.
AI.result: UGD demonstrated comparable performance to supervised metrics in synthetic noise datasets and successfully evaluated point cloud denoising using real-world noisy data.
AI.conclusion: UGD offers an effective unsupervised solution for quantitative evaluation in point cloud denoising, addressing the lack of ground-truth clean data.

title: Continual Safety Alignment via Gradient-Based Sample Selection
summary: Large language models require continuous adaptation to new tasks while preserving safety alignment. However, fine-tuning on even benign data often compromises safety behaviors, including refusal of harmful requests, truthfulness, and commonsense reasoning. We investigate which training samples cause alignment drift through a data-centric lens. Our empirical analysis shows samples contribute unequally: high-gradient samples cause greater safety degradation and drive models toward pretrained distributions, while moderate-gradient samples enable task learning with minimal alignment loss. We propose gradient-based sample selection that filters high-gradient samples during fine-tuning. Across multiple model families on continual domain tasks, our method substantially improves alignment preservation while maintaining competitive task performance, without requiring curated safe data or architectural modifications. Our method is robust across selection ratios, task orderings, and diverse attack benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies that certain training samples cause large language models to lose safety alignment during fine-tuning. It proposes a method to filter such harmful samples based on gradients, preserving alignment without compromising task performance.
AI.motivation: Large language models often lose safety alignment (e.g., refusal of harmful requests, truthfulness) during fine-tuning, even on benign data, which poses risks to their use. Understanding how training samples contribute to alignment drift is critical.
AI.method: The authors analyze the effect of training samples through a data-centric approach, highlighting that high-gradient samples are the culprits of safety degradation. They introduce a gradient-based sample selection method that filters these samples during fine-tuning.
AI.result: The proposed method significantly preserves safety alignment, ensures competitive task performance, and is effective without using curated safe data or altering model architectures. Robustness was demonstrated across model families, tasks, and attack benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Gradient-based sample selection is a promising approach to improve continual domain learning in language models, as it minimizes alignment loss and enhances safety without compromising performance or requiring extensive changes.

title: Align Documents to Questions: Question-Oriented Document Rewriting for Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) enhances the factuality of Large Language Models (LLMs) by incorporating retrieved documents and/or generated context. However, LLMs often exhibit a stylistic bias when presented with mixed contexts, favoring fluent but hallucinated generated content over factually grounded yet disorganized retrieved evidence. This phenomenon reveals that the utility of retrieved information is bottlenecked by its presentation. To bridge this gap, we propose QREAM, a style-controlled rewriter that aligns retrieved documents with a question-oriented style while preserving facts, better for LLM readers to utilize. Our framework consists of two stages: (1) QREAM-ICL, which uses stylistic seeds to guide iterative rewriting exploration; and (2) QREAM-FT, a lightweight student model distilled from denoised ICL outputs. QREAM-FT employs dual-criteria rejection sampling, filtering based on answer correctness and factual consistency to ensure high-quality supervision. QREAM seamlessly integrates into existing RAG pipelines as a plug-and-play module. Experiments demonstrate that QREAM consistently enhances advanced RAG pipelines, yielding up to 8% relative improvement with negligible latency overhead, effectively balancing question relevance with factual grounding.

AI.tldr: Researchers developed QREAM, a style-controlled rewriter that improves how retrieved documents are presented for LLMs within RAG pipelines. This enhances factuality while maintaining a question-oriented style.
AI.motivation: The motivation behind this paper is to address the stylistic bias in LLMs where these models favor fluently generated but hallucinated content over disorganized yet factually correct evidence, limiting the utility of retrieved documents.
AI.method: The method consists of two stages: QREAM-ICL for iterative rewriting guided by stylistic seeds, and QREAM-FT, which distills a lightweight student model using dual-criteria rejection sampling for high-quality supervision. QREAM is designed to integrate into RAG pipelines as a module.
AI.result: QREAM demonstrated up to 8% relative improvement in enhancing RAG pipelines' factual grounding and question relevance, with negligible latency overhead.
AI.conclusion: QREAM successfully balances question-oriented relevance and factual accuracy, proving as a valuable addition to RAG architectures by optimizing the presentation of retrieved documents for LLMs.

title: Adversarial Arena: Crowdsourcing Data Generation through Interactive Competition
summary: Post-training Large Language Models requires diverse, high-quality data which is rare and costly to obtain, especially in low resource domains and for multi-turn conversations. Common solutions are crowdsourcing or synthetic generation, but both often yield low-quality or low-diversity data. We introduce Adversarial Arena for building high quality conversational datasets by framing data generation as an adversarial task: attackers create prompts, and defenders generate responses. This interactive competition between multiple teams naturally produces diverse and complex data. We validated this approach by conducting a competition with 10 academic teams from top US and European universities, each building attacker or defender bots. The competition, focused on safety alignment of LLMs in cybersecurity, generated 19,683 multi-turn conversations. Fine-tuning an open-source model on this dataset produced an 18.47% improvement in secure code generation on CyberSecEval-Instruct and 29.42% improvement on CyberSecEval-MITRE.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Adversarial Arena, a novel approach to generating high-quality conversational datasets by framing data generation as an adversarial task between attackers (prompt creators) and defenders (response generators).
AI.motivation: Generating diverse, high-quality conversational data for training Large Language Models (LLMs) is challenging, particularly in low-resource domains or multi-turn conversations. Existing solutions like crowdsourcing or synthetic data often fall short in quality and diversity.
AI.method: The authors designed an interactive competition, called Adversarial Arena, involving 10 academic teams tasked with building attacker and defender bots. These teams engaged in adversarial data generation, focusing on safety alignment of LLMs in the cybersecurity domain.
AI.result: The competition generated 19,683 multi-turn conversations, and fine-tuning an open-source LLM on this dataset resulted in an 18.47% improvement in secure code generation on CyberSecEval-Instruct and a 29.42% improvement on CyberSecEval-MITRE.
AI.conclusion: The adversarial data generation method proves effective in creating high-quality, diverse conversational datasets, significantly enhancing the performance of LLMs in specialized tasks like cybersecurity.

title: DOSE: Data Selection for Multi-Modal LLMs via Off-the-Shelf Models
summary: High-quality and diverse multimodal data are essential for improving vision-language models (VLMs), yet existing datasets often contain noisy, redundant, and poorly aligned samples. To address these problems, data filtering is commonly used to enhance the efficiency and performance of multimodal learning, but it introduces extra computational cost because filtering models are usually trained on the same data they are meant to screen. To reduce this cost, we study DOSE, which explores whether off-the-shelf pretrained models that have never seen the target data can be used to select training samples for larger and stronger multimodal models without any task-specific training. Even without fine-tuning, these models can effectively assess text quality and image-text alignment to guide data selection. Based on this, we build a joint quality-alignment distribution and apply adaptive weighted sampling to select informative samples while maintaining long-tail diversity. This approach enhances data diversity, enabling models trained on DOSE-filtered data to match or surpass those trained on the full dataset on standard VQA and math benchmarks. Extensive experiments demonstrate its effectiveness, efficiency, and scalability.

AI.tldr: DOSE utilizes off-the-shelf pretrained models to efficiently filter multimodal data for training VLMs, reducing costs while maintaining performance.
AI.motivation: Existing datasets for VLMs are noisy, redundant, and misaligned, necessitating effective data filtering to improve model training without excessive computational expense.
AI.method: DOSE leverages pretrained models (without task-specific fine-tuning) to assess text quality and text-image alignment, applying adaptive weighted sampling based on a joint quality-alignment distribution.
AI.result: DOSE-filtered data maintains diversity and allows models to match or outperform those trained on unfiltered data across VQA and math benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: DOSE demonstrates that pretrained models can effectively filter multimodal data without additional training, combining efficiency and effectiveness in enhancing VLM performance.

title: Neuro-Symbolic Resolution of Recommendation Conflicts in Multimorbidity Clinical Guidelines
comment: Accepted by Proceedings of the 40th Annual AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence (Bridge Program on Logic & AI: Logical and Symbolic Reasoning in Language Models)
summary: Clinical guidelines, typically developed by independent specialty societies, inherently exhibit substantial fragmentation, redundancy, and logical contradiction. These inconsistencies, particularly when applied to patients with multimorbidity, not only cause cognitive dissonance for clinicians but also introduce catastrophic noise into AI systems, rendering the standard Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system fragile and prone to hallucination. To address this fundamental reliability crisis, we introduce a Neuro-Symbolic framework that automates the detection of recommendation redundancies and conflicts. Our pipeline employs a multi-agent system to translate unstructured clinical natural language into rigorous symbolic logic language, which is then verified by a Satisfiability (SAT) solver. By formulating a hierarchical taxonomy of logical rule interactions, we identify a critical category termed Local Conflict - a decision conflict arising from the intersection of comorbidities. Evaluating our system on a curated benchmark of 12 authoritative SGLT2 inhibitor guidelines, we reveal that 90.6% of conflicts are Local, a structural complexity that single-disease guidelines fail to address. While state-of-the-art LLMs fail in detecting these conflicts, our neuro-symbolic approach achieves an F1 score of 0.861. This work demonstrates that logical verification must precede retrieval, establishing a new technical standard for automated knowledge coordination in medical AI.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a neuro-symbolic framework to address inconsistencies and contradictions in clinical guidelines, improving the reliability of AI systems like Retrieval-Augmented Generation.
AI.motivation: Current clinical guidelines are fragmented, redundant, and contradictory, causing challenges for clinicians and AI systems, especially for multimorbidity patients. This leads to fragile AI outcomes.
AI.method: The framework uses a multi-agent system to convert natural language into symbolic logic, which is verified using a SAT solver. It identifies 'Local Conflicts' in guidelines using a hierarchical taxonomy.
AI.result: Tested on SGLT2 inhibitor guidelines, the system revealed 90.6% of conflicts are Local, a complexity single-disease guidelines ignore. It demonstrated an F1 score of 0.861, outperforming state-of-the-art LLMs.
AI.conclusion: Logical verification is crucial before retrieval to improve the reliability of medical AI systems, establishing a new standard for automated knowledge management in healthcare.

title: WebUncertainty: Dual-Level Uncertainty Driven Planning and Reasoning For Autonomous Web Agent
summary: Recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) have empowered autonomous web agents to execute natural language instructions directly on real-world webpages. However, existing agents often struggle with complex tasks involving dynamic interactions and long-horizon execution due to rigid planning strategies and hallucination-prone reasoning. To address these limitations, we propose WebUncertainty, a novel autonomous agent framework designed to tackle dual-level uncertainty in planning and reasoning. Specifically, we design a Task Uncertainty-Driven Adaptive Planning Mechanism that adaptively selects planning modes to navigate unknown environments. Furthermore, we introduce an Action Uncertainty-Driven Monte Carlo tree search (MCTS) Reasoning Mechanism. This mechanism incorporates the Confidence-induced Action Uncertainty (ConActU) strategy to quantify both aleatoric uncertainty (AU) and epistemic uncertainty (EU), thereby optimizing the search process and guiding robust decision-making. Experimental results on the WebArena and WebVoyager benchmarks demonstrate that WebUncertainty achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: WebUncertainty introduces a framework for autonomous web agents to address limitations in dynamic interaction and complex task execution using adaptive planning and uncertainty-driven reasoning.
AI.motivation: Existing autonomous web agents often struggle with dynamic interactions and long execution tasks due to rigid planning and hallucination-prone reasoning.
AI.method: WebUncertainty uses a Task Uncertainty-Driven Adaptive Planning and an Action Uncertainty-Driven MCTS. It quantifies uncertainties (aleatoric and epistemic) using the ConActU strategy to improve decisions.
AI.result: Experimental evaluations on WebArena and WebVoyager benchmarks show that the framework outperforms state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: WebUncertainty offers a robust mechanism for complex and uncertain web tasks, proving effective in enhancing planning and decision-making processes for autonomous agents.

title: Adverse-to-the-eXtreme Panoptic Segmentation: URVIS 2026 Study and Benchmark
summary: This paper presents the report of the URVIS 2026 challenge on adverse-to-extreme panoptic segmentation. As the first challenge of its kind, it attracted 17 registered participants and 47 submissions, with 4 teams reaching the final phase. The challenge is based on the MUSES dataset, a multi-sensor benchmark for panoptic segmentation in adverse-to-extreme weather, including RGB frame camera, LiDAR, radar, and event camera data. Weighted Panoptic Quality (wPQ) is designed and adopted as the official ranking metric for fair evaluation across weather conditions. In this report, we summarise the challenge setting and benchmark results, analyse the performance of the submitted methods, and discuss current progress and remaining challenges for robust multimodal panoptic segmentation. Link: https://urvis-workshop.github.io/challenge-Muses.html

AI.tldr: This paper reports on the URVIS 2026 challenge, focusing on panoptic segmentation in adverse-to-extreme weather conditions using the MUSES dataset and assessing performance with weighted Panoptic Quality (wPQ) as the ranking metric.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of multimodal panoptic segmentation under adverse-to-extreme weather conditions and foster advancements in robust segmentation approaches.
AI.method: Multisensor benchmark data (MUSES dataset) was leveraged, using RGB, LiDAR, radar, and event cameras. Performance was evaluated using Weighted Panoptic Quality (wPQ), promoting fairness across diverse weather scenarios.
AI.result: The challenge attracted 17 participants, resulting in 47 method submissions and 4 successfully reaching the final phase. The paper highlights benchmark results and provides insights into current methodologies.
AI.conclusion: The report underscores existing progress in multimodal segmentation under challenging conditions while identifying areas requiring further research to achieve robustness in adverse weather scenarios.

title: Revisiting Auxiliary Losses for Conditional Depth Routing: An Empirical Study
comment: 23 pages, 4 figures. Preprint. Controlled empirical study with 3-seed runs at 157.5M parameters; includes a negative result on oracle-style utility/rank supervision for conditional depth routing
summary:
Conditional depth execution routes a subset of tokens through a lightweight cheap FFN while the remainder execute the standard full FFN at each controlled layer. The central difficulty is gate training: the gate decision must propagate through many layers before it influences the language modeling (LM) loss, so the resulting gradients are weak and noisy. Auxiliary losses are commonly stacked to stabilise training, yet the interactions among them -- particularly between a predictive auxiliary and explicit score supervision -- have not been systematically compared under controlled conditions.
  We evaluate two gate designs under a 157.5M-parameter decoder-only model with controller-only training, 50% full-path budget, and 3-seed runs on a fineweb-edu subset. The MLP gate (G1) maps the current hidden state to a utility score; the JEPA-guided gate (G3) adds an action-conditional predictor that forecasts, in a low-dimensional latent space, the outcome of executing full vs. cheap per token, aligned against a fixed target head. Under the standard recipe with oracle-style utility regression and pairwise rank supervision (util/rank), G3 improves early-to-mid optimisation over G1 in 3/3 seeds (lower avg LM, faster threshold hits, ~10.3x lower grad norms), with 20k-step endpoint LM within a 0.005 heuristic reference.
  A key finding (ablation A3): jointly removing util/rank improves best/avg LM and threshold-hit speed in 3/3 seeds for both gates, and the early-to-mid advantage of G3 over G1 disappears. We trace this to an off-policy oracle label that assumes all subsequent layers execute full, whereas gated execution routes only a fraction through full -- making util/rank net-negative under the current recipe. Removing util/rank also cuts the training FLOPs proxy from ~1.53x to ~1.07x full-only (2.87h to 1.75h on a V100-32GB, ~39%). Conclusions are scoped to the studied regime.

AI.tldr: The paper explores conditional depth execution in neural networks, focusing on gate training challenges. It evaluates two gate designs under controlled conditions and finds that removing auxiliary losses improves training efficiency and performance.
AI.motivation: Improve efficiency in language modeling by selectively routing tokens through different processes, targeting better training mechanisms for gate decisions.
AI.method: Two gate designs (MLP gate and JEPA-guided gate) are benchmarked under a controlled decoder-only model setup, with experiments comparing auxiliary losses' effects.
AI.result: Removing auxiliary losses (util/rank) significantly improves LM performance, speeds threshold hits, and reduces computational costs (FLOPs proxy reduced by ~30%).
AI.conclusion: Removing auxiliary losses enhances optimization and efficiency for both gate designs, suggesting these losses are net-negative under current recipes.

title: Logical Computational Linguistics
summary:
In this book we promote logical computational linguistics as opposed to statistical computational linguistics. In particular, we provide a logical semantic interface. This book assembles more than twenty years of research work on type logical grammar, and adds new ideas and material.
  Chains of statistical dependencies of less than one hundred per cent confidence tend monotonically to zero. Chains of logical dependencies of any length maintain one hundred per cent confidence end to end.
  We aspire to enable perfect syntactic and semantic processing in life-critical NLP applications.

AI.tldr: The book advocates for logical computational linguistics over statistical approaches and introduces a logical semantic interface for syntactic and semantic parsing.
AI.motivation: The authors highlight flaws in statistical dependencies, emphasizing their lack of reliability over extended chains, and emphasize the need for high-confidence logical dependencies, especially in life-critical applications.
AI.method: The work builds on over two decades of research on type logical grammar, integrating a logical semantic interface for robust computation.
AI.result: Logical dependencies are shown to deliver consistent confidence rates even across extended lengths, unlike statistical ones.
AI.conclusion: Logical computational linguistics enables precise syntactic and semantic processing, making it suitable for high-stakes NLP scenarios.

title: Polysemantic Experts, Monosemantic Paths: Routing as Control in MoEs
summary: An LLM's residual stream is both state and instruction: it encodes the current context and determines the next transformation. We introduce a parameter-free decomposition for Mixture-of-Experts models that splits each layer's hidden state into a control signal that causally drives routing and an orthogonal content channel invisible to the router. Across six MoE architectures, we find that models preserve surface-level features (language, token identity, position) in the content channel, while the control signal encodes an abstract function that rotates from layer to layer. Because each routing decision is low-bandwidth, this hand-off forces compositional specialization across layers. While individual experts remain polysemantic, expert paths become monosemantic, clustering tokens by semantic function across languages and surface forms. The same token (e.g., ":") follows distinct trajectories depending on whether it serves as a type annotation, an introductory colon, or a time separator. Our decomposition identifies the source of this structure: clusters in the control subspace are substantially more monosemantic than those in the full representation. As a result, the natural unit of interpretability in MoEs is not the expert but the trajectory.

AI.tldr: An LLM's residual stream is shown to balance both context and instruction, with a new method illuminating compositional specialization and monosemantic pathways in Mixture-of-Experts models.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to understand how Mixture-of-Experts models encode and transfer information across layers, and why certain tokens exhibit various pathways based on semantic functions, a critical aspect for interpretability and optimization.
AI.method: They introduce a parameter-free decomposition for hidden states in MoE models, splitting them into a 'control signal' that drives routing and an 'orthogonal content channel,' invisible to the router, to analyze token trajectories and semantic functions.
AI.result: It was found that routing is low-bandwidth, enforcing compositional specialization across layers. Expert paths become monosemantic although individual experts themselves are polysemantic, and key semantic distinctions manifest in token trajectories rather than individual experts.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that the natural unit of interpretability in Mixture-of-Experts models is not the individual expert but the trajectory formed through routing decisions, which preserves structured, semantically modular computations.

title: DVAR: Adversarial Multi-Agent Debate for Video Authenticity Detection
summary: The rapid evolution of video generation technologies poses a significant challenge to media forensics, as conventional detection methods often fail to generalize beyond their training distributions. To address this, we propose DVAR (Debate-based Video Authenticity Reasoning), a training-free framework that reformulates video detection as a structured multi-agent forensic reasoning process. Moving beyond the paradigm of pattern matching, DVAR orchestrates a competition between a Generative Hypothesis Agent and a Natural Mechanism Agent. Through iterative rounds of cross-examination, these agents defend their respective explanations against abnormal evidence, driving a logical convergence where the truth emerges from rigorous stress-testing. To adjudicate these conflicting claims, we apply Occam's Razor through the Minimum Description Length (MDL) framework, defining an Explanatory Cost to quantify the "logical burden" of each reasoning path. Furthermore, we integrate GenVideoKB, a dynamic knowledge repository that provides high-level reasoning heuristics on generative boundaries and failure modes. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DVAR achieves competitive performance against supervised state-of-the-art methods while exhibiting superior generalization to unseen generative architectures. By transforming detection into a transparent debate, DVAR provides explicit, interpretable reasoning traces for robust video authenticity assessment.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a training-free framework, DVAR, for video authenticity detection using multi-agent reasoning and Occam's Razor principles, showing improved generalization and interpretability.
AI.motivation: Traditional methods for video authenticity detection struggle to generalize beyond their training distributions due to the rapid evolution of video generation techniques.
AI.method: DVAR operates as a multi-agent reasoning framework involving a Generative Hypothesis Agent and a Natural Mechanism Agent debating over video authenticity evidence. Using Occam's Razor via Minimum Description Length, an Explanatory Cost metric decides the logical burden of competing explanations. The system integrates GenVideoKB, a dynamic repository offering insights on generative boundaries and failure modes.
AI.result: DVAR achieves competitive performance with supervised state-of-the-art models while exhibiting superior generalization to generative architectures it has not been trained on.
AI.conclusion: DVAR demonstrates that reformulating video detection as a structured reasoning debate enhances generalization, offers transparency, and provides interpretable reasoning, making it robust for media forensics.

title: Fully Analog Resonant Recurrent Neural Network via Metacircuit
summary: Physical neural networks offer a transformative route to edge intelligence, providing superior inference speed and energy efficiency compared to conventional digital architectures. However, realizing scalable, end-to-end, fully analog recurrent neural networks for temporal information processing remains challenging due to the difficulty of faithfully mapping trained network models onto physical hardware. Here we present a fully analog resonant recurrent neural network (R$^2$NN) implemented via a metacircuit architecture composed of coupled electrical local resonators. A reformulated mechanical-electrical analogy establishes a direct mapping between the R$^2$NN model and metacircuit elements, enabling accurate physical implementation of trained neural network parameters. By integrating jointly trainable global resistive coupling and local resonances, which generate effective frequency-dependent negative resistances, the architecture shapes an impedance landscape that steers currents along frequency-selective pathways. This mechanism enables direct extraction of discriminative spectral features, facilitating real-time temporal classification of raw analog inputs while bypassing analog-to-digital conversion. We demonstrate the cross-domain versatility of this framework using integrated hardware for tactile perception, speech recognition, and condition monitoring. This work establishes a scalable, fully analog paradigm for intelligent temporal processing and paves the way for low-latency, resource-efficient physical neural hardware for edge intelligence.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a fully analog resonant recurrent neural network (R$^2$NN) design using coupled electrical local resonators for real-time temporal data classification, eliminating the need for analog-to-digital conversion and enhancing edge device efficiency.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address challenges in implementing scalable, fully analog recurrent neural networks for temporal information processing, bridging the gap between effective neural modeling and physical hardware execution.
AI.method: The study deploys a metacircuit architecture utilizing coupled electrical local resonators and a mechanical-electrical analogy for accurate parameter mapping. It includes jointly trainable resistive coupling with local resonance to guide frequency-selective currents for spectral feature extraction.
AI.result: The proposed framework demonstrates effective real-time analog classification across tactile perception, speech recognition, and condition monitoring applications, showcasing versatility and cross-domain functionality.
AI.conclusion: This research advances scalable analog neural networks as efficient temporal data processors for edge intelligence, offering low latency and energy efficiency while foregoing analog-to-digital conversion.

title: More Than Meets the Eye: Measuring the Semiotic Gap in Vision-Language Models via Semantic Anchorage
comment: 16 pages, 4 figures. Accepted to the Main Conference of ACL 2026
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) excel at photorealistic generation, yet often struggle to represent abstract meaning such as idiomatic interpretations of noun compounds. To study whether high visual fidelity interferes with idiomatic compositionality under visual abstraction, we introduce DIVA, a controlled benchmark that replaces high-fidelity visual detail with schematic iconicity by generating paired, sense-anchored visualizations for literal and idiomatic readings. We further propose Semantic Alignment Gap ($Δ$), an architecture-agnostic metric that quantifies divergence between literal and idiomatic visual grounding. We additionally introduce a directional signed bias $b(t)$ to separately measure the direction and strength of literal preference. Evaluating 8 recent VLMs, we reveal a consistent Literal Superiority Bias: model scale alone does not resolve literal preference, and increased visual fidelity is associated with weaker symbolic alignment, suggesting cognitive interference from hyper-realistic imagery. Our findings suggest that improving compositional understanding requires iconographic abstraction of visual input and anchoring interpretation and generation in intended meaning.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces DIVA, a benchmark to study how visual fidelity affects idiomatic meaning comprehension in Vision-Language Models (VLMs), revealing that higher visual fidelity weakens symbolic understanding.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether high visual fidelity in Vision-Language Models interferes with their ability to represent and understand idiomatic meanings via visual abstraction.
AI.method: The authors introduced DIVA, a controlled benchmark using schematic iconic visualizations for both literal and idiomatic readings, alongside a Semantic Alignment Gap metric and a signed bias measure to analyze VLMs' preferences and symbolic alignment.
AI.result: Eight state-of-the-art VLMs were studied, revealing a consistent Literal Superiority Bias, where increased visual fidelity leads to weaker symbolic alignment and a stronger literal preference.
AI.conclusion: To improve compositional understanding in VLMs, iconographic abstraction and intentional anchoring of interpretation and generation in the intended meaning are necessary.

title: On the Reliability of Computer Use Agents
summary: Computer-use agents have rapidly improved on real-world tasks such as web navigation, desktop automation, and software interaction, in some cases surpassing human performance. Yet even when the task and model are unchanged, an agent that succeeds once may fail on a repeated execution of the same task. This raises a fundamental question: if an agent can succeed at a task once, what prevents it from doing so reliably? In this work, we study the sources of unreliability in computer-use agents through three factors: stochasticity during execution, ambiguity in task specification, and variability in agent behavior. We analyze these factors on OSWorld using repeated executions of the same task together with paired statistical tests that capture task-level changes across settings. Our analysis shows that reliability depends on both how tasks are specified and how agent behavior varies across executions. These findings suggest the need to evaluate agents under repeated execution, to allow agents to resolve task ambiguity through interaction, and to favor strategies that remain stable across runs.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates why computer-use agents fail on repeated task executions despite initial success, identifying execution stochasticity, task ambiguity, and agent behavior variability as key factors.
AI.motivation: Understanding why state-of-the-art computer-use agents sometimes fail at tasks they successfully completed before, even under the same conditions.
AI.method: Conducted studies on OSWorld using repeated executions of tasks, paired with statistical tests analyzing task-level changes under different conditions and factors.
AI.result: Reliability of agents is influenced by task specification clarity and agent behavioral consistency across executions.
AI.conclusion: Agents must be evaluated for performance consistency across repetitions, address task ambiguity via interaction, and adopt stable strategies to improve reliability.

title: Inductive Convolution Nuclear Norm Minimization for Tensor Completion with Arbitrary Sampling
summary: The recently established Convolution Nuclear Norm Minimization (CNNM) addresses the problem of \textit{tensor completion with arbitrary sampling} (TCAS), which involves restoring a tensor from a subset of its entries sampled in an arbitrary manner. Despite its promising performance, the optimization procedure of CNNM needs performing Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) multiple times, which is computationally expensive and hard to parallelize. To address the issue, we reformulate the optimization objective of CNNM from the perspective of convolution eigenvectors. By introducing pre-learned convolution eigenvectors which are shared among different tensors, we propose a novel method called Inductive Convolution Nuclear Norm Minimization (ICNNM), which bypasses the SVD step so as to decrease significantly the computational time. In addition, due to the extra prior knowledge encoded in the pre-learned convolution eigenvectors, ICNNM also outperforms CNNM in terms of recovery performance. Extensive experiments on video completion, prediction and frame interpolation verify the superiority of ICNNM over CNNM and several other competing methods.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ICNNM, a novel tensor completion approach that bypasses computationally expensive SVD operations and delivers better recovery accuracy compared to CNNM.
AI.motivation: To overcome the high computational cost and limited parallelizability of the existing CNNM method for tensor completion.
AI.method: The authors reformulated the optimization objective of CNNM using convolution eigenvectors and introduced pre-learned convolution eigenvectors for their proposed ICNNM method.
AI.result: ICNNM significantly reduces computational time by eliminating the SVD step and achieves superior recovery performance in tensor completion tasks.
AI.conclusion: ICNNM is more computationally efficient and delivers better tensor recovery results, making it a valuable improvement over CNNM.

title: REALM: Reliable Expertise-Aware Language Model Fine-Tuning from Noisy Annotations
summary: Supervised fine-tuning of large language models relies on human-annotated data, yet annotation pipelines routinely involve multiple crowdworkers of heterogeneous expertise. Standard practice aggregates labels via majority vote or simple averaging, discarding annotator identity and causing the model to absorb the errors of unreliable annotators directly into its parameters. We propose REALM, a method that jointly learns the model parameters and a scalar expertise value for each annotator entirely unsupervised, requiring no supervision beyond annotator identity. The key idea is to model each observed label as a mixture between the model's prediction and a uniform random guess, weighted by the annotator's learned expertise. We extend REALM to a multi-task setting via a learned expertise matrix that captures per-annotator reliability across tasks. We evaluate on five question answering benchmarks, fine-tuning three sizes of Flan-T5 under simulated noisy annotations. The proposed algorithm consistently outperforms the naive noisy SFT in the large majority of single- and multi-task settings, across datasets, model sizes, and noise types, with accuracy improvements of up to $50\%$ in the most adversarial regime and gains that grow with model capacity.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces REALM, a method to improve fine-tuning of language models by learning annotator expertise alongside model parameters, outperforming standard techniques under noisy data.
AI.motivation: Standard fine-tuning practices neglect annotator identity and expertise, causing models to absorb errors from unreliable annotators.
AI.method: REALM models labels as a weighted mixture of the model’s prediction and a random guess, learning annotator expertise values and extending this to multi-task scenarios via an expertise matrix.
AI.result: REALM outperforms traditional noisy fine-tuning across models, tasks, datasets, and noise levels, showing up to 50% accuracy improvement in adversarial scenarios.
AI.conclusion: REALM effectively mitigates noise in annotations by incorporating annotator expertise, improving model performance, particularly under challenging conditions.

title: Still Between Us? Evaluating and Improving Voice Assistant Robustness to Third-Party Interruptions
summary: While recent Spoken Language Models (SLMs) have been actively deployed in real-world scenarios, they lack the capability to discern Third-Party Interruptions (TPI) from the primary user's ongoing flow, leaving them vulnerable to contextual failures. To bridge this gap, we introduce TPI-Train, a dataset of 88K instances designed with speaker-aware hard negatives to enforce acoustic cue prioritization for interruption handling, and TPI-Bench, a comprehensive evaluation framework designed to rigorously measure the interruption-handling strategy and precise speaker discrimination in deceptive contexts.
