AI.method: The authors designed PARM, which adapts reward models using pipeline-specific data and preference optimization to align predictions with pipeline execution outcomes.
AI.result: PARM was evaluated on four optimization benchmarks and a cross-domain experiment on GSM8K, showing consistent improvements in quality and stability over baselines.
AI.conclusion: PARM enhances reward guidance in multi-stage pipelines, offering valuable insights into multi-stage LLM reasoning and execution alignment.

title: PPEDCRF: Dynamic-CRF-Guided Selective Perturbation for Background-Based Location Privacy in Video Sequences
summary:
We propose PPEDCRF, a calibrated selective perturbation framework that protects \emph{background-based location privacy} in released video frames against gallery-based retrieval attackers. Even after GPS metadata are stripped, an adversary can geolocate a frame by matching its background visual cues to geo-tagged reference imagery; PPEDCRF mitigates this threat by estimating location-sensitive background regions with a dynamic conditional random field (DCRF), rescaling perturbation strength with a normalized control penalty (NCP), and injecting Gaussian noise only inside the inferred regions via a DP-style calibration rule.
  On a controlled paired-scene retrieval benchmark with eight attacker backbones and three noise seeds, PPEDCRF reduces ResNet18 Top-1 retrieval accuracy from 0.667 to $0.361\pm0.127$ at $σ_0=8$ while preserving $36.14\,$dB PSNR -- an ${\approx}6\,$dB quality advantage over global Gaussian noise. Transfer across the eight-backbone seed-averaged benchmark is broadly supportive (23 of 24 backbone-gallery cells show negative $Δ$), while appendix-scale confirmation identifies MixVPR as a remaining adverse-transfer exception. Matched-operating-point analysis shows that PPEDCRF and global Gaussian noise converge in Top-1 privacy at equal utility, so the practical benefit is spatially concentrated perturbation that preserves higher visual quality at any given noise scale rather than stronger matched-utility privacy. Code: https://github.com/mabo1215/PPEDCRF

AI.tldr: PPEDCRF is a framework designed to protect location privacy in video frames by identifying sensitive background areas and selectively perturbing them. It retains high visual quality while reducing retrieval accuracy of location from visual cues.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the privacy threat posed by the ability of attackers to geolocate video frames using background visual cues, even when GPS metadata is removed.
AI.method: PPEDCRF uses a dynamic conditional random field (DCRF) to detect location-sensitive regions, applies a normalized control penalty (NCP) for rescaling perturbation strength, and injects Gaussian noise selectively within those regions using a differential privacy-style calibration.
AI.result: In tests on paired-scene benchmarks, PPEDCRF substantially reduced retrieval accuracy of ResNet18 models and demonstrated better visual quality for perturbed images compared to globally-applied Gaussian noise. Transferability across attacker models was broadly validated.
AI.conclusion: PPEDCRF preserves privacy while maintaining visual quality by concentrating perturbations spatially. It does not outperform global noise in overall privacy at matched utility but is more efficient in maintaining image quality and localizing the perturbation impact.

title: Grokking of Diffusion Models: Case Study on Modular Addition
summary: Despite their empirical success, how diffusion models generalize remains poorly understood from a mechanistic perspective. We demonstrate that diffusion models trained with flow-matching objectives exhibit grokking--delayed generalization after overfitting--on modular addition, enabling controlled analysis of their internal computations. We study this phenomenon across two levels of data regime. In a single-image regime, mechanistic dissection reveals that the model implements modular addition by composing periodic representations of individual operands. In a diverse-image regime with high intraclass variability, we find that the model leverages its iterative sampling process to partition the task into an arithmetic computation phase followed by a visual denoising phase, separated by a critical timestep threshold. Our work provides the mechanistic decomposition of algorithmic learning in diffusion models, revealing how these models bridge continuous pixel-space generation and discrete symbolic reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how diffusion models trained using flow-matching objectives exhibit grokking and perform modular addition, with insights into their internal computation across different data regimes.
AI.motivation: To understand the mechanisms behind diffusion models' generalization and uncover how they merge continuous pixel-space generation with discrete reasoning.
AI.method: Investigating modular addition in diffusion models using flow-matching objectives, with mechanistic dissection in both single-image and diverse-image data regimes.
AI.result: The study reveals that in single-image settings, models implement modular addition via periodic representations of operands. In diverse-image regimes, they partition tasks into arithmetic and denoising phases with a critical timestep threshold.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion models functionally decompose tasks, tying algorithmic learning to their iterative sampling processes, bridging continuous and symbolic reasoning effectively.

title: Screen Before You Interpret: A Portable Validity Protocol for Benchmark-Based LLM Confidence Signals
comment: 25 pages, 6 figures, 8 tables, 2 appendices. Companion to arXiv:2604.15702
summary: LLM confidence signals are used for abstention, routing, and safety-critical decisions. No standard practice exists for checking whether a confidence signal carries item-level information before building on it. We transfer the validity screening principle from clinical personality assessment (PAI, MMPI-3) as a portable protocol for benchmark-based LLM confidence data. The protocol specifies three core indices (L, Fp, RBS), a structural indicator (TRIN), and an item-sensitivity statistic, computed from a single 2x2 contingency table. A three-tier classification system (Invalid, Indeterminate, Valid) draws on four clinical traditions. Validated on 20 frontier LLMs across 524 items, four models are classified Invalid, two Indeterminate. Valid-profile models show mean r = .18 (15/16 significant). Invalid-profile models show mean r = -.20 (d = 2.48). Cross-benchmark validation on 18 models using MMLU with verbalized confidence and on external data from Yang et al. (2024) confirms the screen transfers across benchmarks and probe formats. All data and code: https://github.com/synthiumjp/validity-scaling-llm

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a protocol for validating LLM confidence signals using techniques adapted from clinical assessments. It screens LLMs for confidence validity before applying them in critical areas.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of standard practices in validating LLM confidence signals, which are crucial for safety-critical applications and decision-making.
AI.method: The study applies a protocol adapted from clinical personality assessments to benchmark-based LLM confidence data. This involves core indices, a structural indicator, and an item-sensitivity test, validated across 20 LLMs and external datasets.
AI.result: Of the 20 tested LLMs, four were classified as Invalid and two as Indeterminate. Valid-profile models showed significant positive correlations, while Invalid ones showed negative correlations. Cross-benchmark validation confirmed the protocol's transferability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed validation protocol is effective in identifying LLMs with reliable confidence signals and can be applied across various benchmarks and formats, improving LLM utility in critical tasks.

title: One Pass for All: A Discrete Diffusion Model for Knowledge Graph Triple Set Prediction
summary: Knowledge Graphs (KGs) are composed of triples, and the goal of Knowledge Graph Completion (KGC) is to infer the missing factual triples. Traditional KGC tasks predict missing elements in a triple given one or two of its elements. As a more realistic task, the Triple Set Prediction (TSP) task aims to infer the set of missing triples conditioned only on the observed knowledge graph, without assuming any partial information about the missing triples. Existing TSP methods predict the set of missing triples in a triple-by-triple manner, falling short in capturing the dependencies among the predicted triples to ensure consistency. To address this issue, we propose a novel discrete diffusion model termed DiffTSP that treats TSP as a generative task. DiffTSP progressively adds noise to the KG through a discrete diffusion process, achieved by masking relational edges. The reverse process then gradually recovers the complete KG conditioned on the incomplete graph. To this end, we design a structure-aware denoising network that integrates a relational context encoder with a relational graph diffusion transformer for knowledge graph generation. DiffTSP can generate the complete set of triples in a one-pass manner while ensuring the dependencies among the predicted triples. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on three public datasets. Code: https://github.com/ADMIS-TONGJI/DiffTSP.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces DiffTSP, a novel discrete diffusion model for the Triple Set Prediction (TSP) task, designed to infer missing triples and ensure their dependencies in Knowledge Graphs. DiffTSP achieves state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for the TSP task predict triple-by-triple, failing to ensure consistency and dependency among predicted triples, which is essential for realistic knowledge graph completion.
AI.method: The proposed DiffTSP employs a discrete diffusion process that adds noise to the KG by masking relational edges and gradually recovers the complete graph using a structure-aware denoising network combining a relational context encoder and a relational graph diffusion transformer.
AI.result: DiffTSP demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on three public TSP datasets, showing improved results in generating complete, consistent triples.
AI.conclusion: DiffTSP effectively addresses the limitations of existing methods by generating complete triple sets in a one-pass manner, maintaining dependencies, and advancing the performance in Knowledge Graph Completion tasks.

title: LookasideVLN: Direction-Aware Aerial Vision-and-Language Navigation
summary: Aerial Vision-and-Language Navigation (Aerial VLN) enables unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) to follow natural language instructions and navigate complex urban environments. While recent advances have achieved progress through large-scale memory graphs and lookahead path planning, they remain limited by shallow instruction understanding and high computational cost. In particular, existing methods rely primarily on landmark descriptions, overlooking directional cues "a key source of spatial context in human navigation". In this work, we propose LookasideVLN, a new paradigm that exploits directional cues in natural language to achieve both more accurate spatial reasoning and greater computational efficiency. LookasideVLN comprises three core components: (1) an Egocentric Lookaside Graph (ELG) that dynamically encodes instruction-relevant landmarks and their directional relationships, (2) a Spatial Landmark Knowledge Base (SLKB) that provides lightweight memory retrieval from prior navigation experiences, and (3) a Lookaside MLLM Navigation Agent that aligns multimodal information from user instructions, visual observations, and landmark-direction information from ELG for path planning. Extensive experiments show that LookasideVLN significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art CityNavAgent, even with a single-level lookahead, demonstrating that leveraging directional cues is a powerful yet efficient strategy for Aerial VLN.

AI.tldr: Proposed LookasideVLN improves aerial navigation by UAVs through efficient and accurate use of directional cues in natural language.
AI.motivation: Existing Aerial VLN methods are limited by shallow language understanding of directional cues and high computational demand, impairing accurate UAV navigation.
AI.method: Introduces LookasideVLN with an Egocentric Lookaside Graph (ELG), Spatial Landmark Knowledge Base (SLKB), and Lookaside MLLM Navigation Agent to enhance spatial reasoning and efficiency.
AI.result: Achieves superior performance compared to the CityNavAgent with improved computational efficiency and accurate navigation.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating directional cues into Aerial VLN systems can yield substantial improvements in UAV navigation accuracy and cost-effectiveness.

title: SafeAnchor: Preventing Cumulative Safety Erosion in Continual Domain Adaptation of Large Language Models
summary:
Safety alignment in large language models is remarkably shallow: it is concentrated in the first few output tokens and reversible by fine-tuning on as few as 100 adversarial examples. This fragility becomes critical in real-world deployment, where models undergo sequential adaptation across domains such as medicine, law, and code, causing safety guardrails to erode cumulatively. Yet all existing safety-preserving methods target only single-task fine-tuning, leaving the multi-domain sequential setting entirely unaddressed.
  We introduce SafeAnchor, a framework that anchors safety in place throughout continual adaptation. SafeAnchor first identifies low-rank safety subspaces in LoRA parameter space via Fisher Information eigendecomposition, then constrains domain-specific gradient updates to the orthogonal complement of these subspaces, and finally monitors for residual safety drift with threshold-triggered corrective replay. Evaluated on Llama-2-7B-Chat and Mistral-7B-Instruct across a three-domain pipeline and eight benchmarks, SafeAnchor retains 93.2% of original safety alignment, outperforming all baselines by 18-42 points, while matching unconstrained fine-tuning to within 1.5 points on domain tasks.

AI.tldr: Safety alignment in large language models is fragile and deteriorates during continual adaptation across domains. The proposed SafeAnchor framework retains high safety alignment while preserving performance in domain-specific tasks.
AI.motivation: Current safety mechanisms in large language models falter under sequential domain adaptation, causing cumulative erosion of safety safeguards, which is a critical challenge for real-world deployments.
AI.method: The SafeAnchor framework identifies low-rank safety subspaces using Fisher Information eigendecomposition, restricts gradient updates to orthogonal spaces during domain adaptation, and applies corrective replay to monitor for safety drift.
AI.result: SafeAnchor achieved 93.2% retention of safety alignment, outperforming baselines by 18-42 points, while maintaining near-matching performance (within 1.5 points) on domain-specific tasks.
AI.conclusion: SafeAnchor effectively anchors safety parameters during continual adaptation across multiple domains, offering a significant improvement in preserving safety alignment without sacrificing task-specific performance.

title: Concurrent Criterion Validation of a Validity Screen for LLM Confidence Signals via Selective Prediction
comment: 11 pages, 4 figures, 2 tables. Companion to arXiv:2604.15702
summary: The validity screen (Cacioli, 2026d, 2026e) classifies LLM confidence signals as Valid, Indeterminate, or Invalid. We test whether these classifications predict selective prediction performance. Twenty frontier LLMs from seven families were evaluated on 524 items across six cognitive tracks. Valid models show mean Type 2 AUROC = .624 (SD = .048). Invalid models show mean AUROC = .357 (SD = .231). Cohen's d = 2.81, p = .002. The tiers order monotonically: Invalid (.357) < Indeterminate (.554) < Valid (.624). Split-half cross-validation yields median d = 1.77, P(d > 0) = 1.0 across 1,000 splits. The three-tier classification accounts for 47% of the variance in AUROC. DeepSeek-R1 drops from 85.3% accuracy at full coverage to 11.3% at 10% coverage. The screen predicts the criterion. For selective prediction, the screen matters.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a validity screen for classifying the confidence signals of large language models (LLMs) into Valid, Indeterminate, or Invalid, demonstrating its predictive capacity for selective prediction outcomes.
AI.motivation: To improve selective prediction outcomes by categorizing LLM confidence signals effectively.
AI.method: Evaluated 20 frontier LLMs from seven families on 524 items across six cognitive tracks to observe their classification performance based on validity screening.
AI.result: Valid models showed significantly better prediction performance (Type 2 AUROC = .624), while Invalid models performed poorly (AUROC = .357). Classification tiers explained 47% of AUROC variance, and results were consistent across cross-validation methods.
AI.conclusion: The three-tier validity classification is a statistically significant and useful tool for predicting selective prediction efficacy within LLMs.

title: Training and Agentic Inference Strategies for LLM-based Manim Animation Generation
summary: Generating programmatic animation using libraries such as Manim presents unique challenges for Large Language Models (LLMs), requiring spatial reasoning, temporal sequencing, and familiarity with domain-specific APIs that are underrepresented in general pre-training data. A systematic study of how training and inference strategies interact in this setting is lacking in current research. This study introduces ManimTrainer, a training pipeline that combines Supervised Fine-tuning (SFT) with Reinforcement Learning (RL) based Group Relative Policy Optimisation (GRPO) using a unified reward signal that fuses code and visual assessment signals, and ManimAgent, an inference pipeline featuring Renderer-in-the-loop (RITL) and API documentation-augmented RITL (RITL-DOC) strategies. Using these techniques, this study presents the first unified training and inference study for text-to-code-to-video transformation with Manim. It evaluates 17 open-source sub-30B LLMs across nine combinations of training and inference strategies using ManimBench. Results show that SFT generally improves code quality, while GRPO enhances visual outputs and increases the models' responsiveness to extrinsic signals during self-correction at inference time. The Qwen 3 Coder 30B model with GRPO and RITL-DOC achieved the highest overall performance, with a 94% Render Success Rate (RSR) and 85.7% Visual Similarity (VS) to reference videos, surpassing the baseline GPT-4.1 model by +3 percentage points in VS. Additionally, the analysis shows that the correlation between code and visual metrics strengthens with SFT and GRPO but weakens with inference-time enhancements, highlighting the complementary roles of training and agentic inference strategies in Manim animation generation.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces ManimTrainer and ManimAgent as pipelines to train and perform inference for generating Manim animations with Large Language Models (LLMs). The study evaluates training and inference strategies on 17 LLMs using ManimBench, showing improvements in code quality and visual output.
AI.motivation: Current LLMs struggle with spatial reasoning, temporal sequencing, and use of APIs for programmatic animation generation. There's a lack of systematic study on training and inference strategies to address these challenges.
AI.method: The paper used a training pipeline (ManimTrainer) combining Supervised Fine-tuning and RL-based Group Relative Policy Optimisation, along with an inference pipeline (ManimAgent) utilizing Renderer-in-the-loop and API documentation-augmented strategies.
AI.result: Among the evaluated models, Qwen 3 Coder 30B with GRPO and RITL-DOC achieved the best results, including a 94% Render Success Rate and 85.7% Visual Similarity to reference animations, outperforming GPT-4.1.
AI.conclusion: SFT improves code quality, while GRPO enhances visual outputs and responsiveness during inference. Training and inference strategies play complementary roles in achieving high-quality animations using Manim.

title: DreamShot: Personalized Storyboard Synthesis with Video Diffusion Prior
comment: Accepted by CVPR2026 as a Highlight paper
summary: Storyboard synthesis plays a crucial role in visual storytelling, aiming to generate coherent shot sequences that visually narrate cinematic events with consistent characters, scenes, and transitions. However, existing approaches are mostly adapted from text-to-image diffusion models, which struggle to maintain long-range temporal coherence, consistent character identities, and narrative flow across multiple shots. In this paper, we introduce DreamShot, a video generative model based storyboard framework that fully exploits powerful video diffusion priors for controllable multi-shot synthesis. DreamShot supports both Text-to-Shot and Reference-to-Shot generation, as well as story continuation conditioned on previous frames, enabling flexible and context-aware storyboard generation. By leveraging the spatial-temporal consistency inherent in video generative models, DreamShot produces visually and semantically coherent sequences with improved narrative fidelity and character continuity. Furthermore, DreamShot incorporates a multi-reference role conditioning module that accepts multiple character reference images and enforces identity alignment via a Role-Attention Consistency Loss, explicitly constraining attention between reference and generated roles. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DreamShot achieves superior scene coherence, role consistency, and generation efficiency compared to state-of-the-art text-to-image storyboard models, establishing a new direction toward controllable video model-driven visual storytelling.

AI.tldr: DreamShot enhances storyboard synthesis for visual storytelling by leveraging video generative models to achieve better scene coherence and consistent character roles compared to traditional text-to-image approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing storyboard synthesis methods struggle with maintaining long-range temporal coherence and narrative flow, prompting the need for a solution that ensures character and scene consistency in cinematic visual storytelling.
AI.method: DreamShot utilizes video generative models with spatial-temporal consistency, incorporating a multi-reference role conditioning module and Role-Attention Consistency Loss to maintain character identity and improve narrative fidelity.
AI.result: DreamShot demonstrates superior scene coherence, role consistency, and generation efficiency, outperforming state-of-the-art text-to-image storyboard synthesis models.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a novel and effective approach for controllable storyboard synthesis, paving the way for video model-driven visual storytelling.

title: CAPO: Counterfactual Credit Assignment in Sequential Cooperative Teams
summary: In cooperative teams where agents act in a fixed order and share a single team reward, it is hard to know how much each agent contributed, and harder still when agents are updated one at a time because data collected earlier no longer reflects the new policies. We introduce the Sequential Aristocrat Utility (SeqAU), the unique per-agent learning signal that maximizes the individual learnability of each agent's action, extending the classical framework of Wolpert and Tumer (2002) to this sequential setting. From SeqAU we derive CAPO (Counterfactual Advantage Policy Optimization), a critic-free policy-gradient algorithm. CAPO fits a per-agent reward decomposition from group rewards and computes the per-agent advantage in closed form plus a handful of forward passes through the current policy, requiring no extra environment calls beyond the initial batch. We give analytic bias and variance bounds and validate them on a controlled sequential bandit, where CAPO's advantage over standard baselines grows with the team size. The framework is general; multi-LLM pipelines are a natural deployment target.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SeqAU, a learning signal for sequential cooperative teams, and proposes CAPO, a critic-free policy-gradient algorithm that improves individual learning and addresses reward decomposition challenges.
AI.motivation: To address the difficulty of determining individual agent contributions in sequential cooperative teams operating under shared rewards, particularly when agent policies are updated incrementally.
AI.method: The authors propose SeqAU to maximize per-agent learnability in cooperative teams and derive CAPO, a policy-gradient algorithm that uses closed-form advantage computation and reward decomposition with no extra environment interactions.
AI.result: CAPO demonstrates significant improvements in performance compared to standard baselines, with advantages scaling positively relative to team size, as validated in a controlled sequential bandit.
AI.conclusion: The SeqAU framework and CAPO algorithm provide a scalable and effective solution for learning in sequential cooperative multi-agent scenarios, with applicability extending to multi-LLM pipelines.

title: Do LLMs Use Cultural Knowledge Without Being Told? A Multilingual Evaluation of Implicit Pragmatic Adaptation
summary: Many benchmarks show that large language models can answer direct questions about culture. We study a different question: do they also change how they speak when culture is only implied by the situation? We evaluate 60 culturally grounded conversational scenarios across five languages in three conditions: a neutral baseline (Prompt A), an explicit cultural instruction (Prompt B), and implicit situational cueing (Prompt C). We score responses on 12 pragmatic features covering deference to authority, individual-versus-group framing, and uncertainty management. We define Pragmatic Context Sensitivity (PCS) as the fraction of the Prompt A->B shift that reappears under Prompt A->C. Across four deployed LLMs and five languages (English, German, Hindi, Nepali, Urdu), the primary stable-only PCS mean is 0.196 (SD = 0.113), indicating that the models recover only about one-fifth of the pragmatic shift they can produce when instructed explicitly. Transfer is strongest for authority-related cues (0.299) and weakest for individual-versus-group framing (0.120). Uncertainty-related behaviour is mixed: hedging density exhibits negative explicit gaps in all five languages, suggesting that alignment training actively suppresses the target behaviour. Because Hindi and Urdu share core grammar yet index distinct cultural communities, we use them as a natural control; a paired analysis finds no reliable baseline difference (t = 0.96, p = 0.339, dz = 0.06), suggesting that models respond primarily to linguistic structure rather than to the cultural associations a language carries. We argue that multilingual cultural pragmatics is an explicit-versus-implicit deployment problem, not only a factual knowledge problem.

AI.tldr: The paper explores whether large language models adjust their conversational pragmatics based on implicit cultural cues, finding that they recover only about 20% of pragmatic shifts achievable via explicit instructions.
AI.motivation: To understand if large language models adapt their communication style implicitly in culturally grounded scenarios, beyond factual accuracy in answering direct questions.
AI.method: The study evaluates chat responses across 60 scenarios, 5 languages, and under 3 prompting conditions (neutral, explicit cultural instruction, and implicit situational cueing). Responses are analyzed on 12 pragmatic features.
AI.result: Across tested models and languages, the mean Pragmatic Context Sensitivity (PCS) is 0.196, indicating limited transfer from explicit instructions to implicit cueing. Authority-related cues show stronger PCS (0.299) while individual-group framing is weak (0.120).
AI.conclusion: Large language models exhibit limited ability to implicitly adapt to cultural pragmatics, highlighting the explicit vs implicit challenge in multilingual cultural understanding.

title: The implicated scientist: on the role of AI researchers in the development of weapons systems
comment: Presented as an oral talk and a poster at the AI for Peace workshop at ICLR 2026
summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) technologies are increasingly used in modern weapons systems. Notably, these systems have recently been involved in mass killings and destruction at scale. Furthermore, there is currently a strong interest and competition among powerful players to accelerate the proliferation of weapons with automated or AI-based components, a phenomenon known as AI arms race. This competition poses a risk of causing even more deaths and devastation in the future, as well as increased power and wealth inequality. In this work, we aim to shed light on the role of AI researchers as implicated subjects in the harms caused by weapons enabled by AI technologies. We investigate and discuss the specifics of this implication and explore ways to transfigure this position of implication into one of differentiated, long-distance solidarity with the victims of technologically fortified injustices.

AI.tldr: The paper discusses the ethical implications of AI researchers in the development and deployment of AI-powered weapon systems.
AI.motivation: To address the ethical responsibility of AI researchers amidst the rise of AI-augmented weapons and the associated arms race.
AI.method: The authors investigate the engagement of AI researchers in the creation of these technologies and propose strategies for ethical realignment.
AI.result: Identifies the involvement of AI researchers and the harms caused by AI weapons, suggesting transfiguration towards solidarity with victims.
AI.conclusion: Urges the AI community to reconsider their role and align their efforts with ensuring justice and minimizing harm caused by AI weaponry.

title: SciDraw-6K: A Multilingual Scientific Illustration Dataset Generated by Google Gemini
summary: We present SciDraw-6K, a curated dataset of 6,291 scientific illustrations synthesized by Google Gemini image-generation models, each paired with prompts in eleven languages (English, Simplified Chinese, Traditional Chinese, Japanese, Korean, German, French, Spanish, Brazilian Portuguese, Italian, and Russian). Images span eight broad scientific categories -- biomedical, chemistry, materials, electronics, environment, AI systems, physics, and a long "other" tail -- and are produced primarily by the gemini-2.5-flash-image and gemini-3-pro-image-preview model families. In contrast to general-purpose text-to-image corpora that dominate the literature, SciDraw-6K is purpose-built for the scientific illustration genre: schematic diagrams, mechanism figures, table-of-contents graphics, and conceptual posters. We describe the construction pipeline, report dataset statistics, and document its use as the substrate of sci-draw.com, a public scientific drawing service. The dataset is released to support multilingual text-to-image research, domain-adapted diffusion fine-tuning, and prompt-engineering studies for scientific visualization. Dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/SciDrawAI/SciDraw-6K Code: https://github.com/SciDrawAI/scidraw-6k

AI.tldr: SciDraw-6K is a dataset of 6,291 scientific illustrations synthesized using Google Gemini models, paired with prompts in 11 languages, across 8 scientific categories, designed for multilingual and scientific visualization research.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the lack of specialized datasets tailored for scientific illustration tasks in text-to-image generation, aiming to enable multilingual and domain-specific research in this area.
AI.method: The authors developed SciDraw-6K using Google Gemini image-generation models, providing annotated data for scientific illustrations paired with prompts in multiple languages. The dataset includes categorized scientific images and facilitates tasks like fine-tuning and prompt engineering.
AI.result: The resulting dataset contains high-quality, categorized, and multilingual scientific illustrations, complemented by a public drawing service (sci-draw.com).
AI.conclusion: SciDraw-6K offers a resource to advance research in scientific visualization, multilingual text-to-image generation, and specialized fine-tuning of diffusion models, enhancing tools for scientific communication.

title: MoE-nD: Per-Layer Mixture-of-Experts Routing for Multi-Axis KV Cache Compression
summary: KV cache memory is the dominant bottleneck for long-context LLM inference. Existing compression methods each act on a single axis of the four-dimensional KV tensor -- token eviction (sequence), quantization (precision), low-rank projection (head dimension), or cross-layer sharing -- but apply the same recipe to every layer. We show that this homogeneity leaves accuracy on the table: different layers respond very differently to each compression operation, and the optimal per-layer mix of eviction and quantization is far from uniform. We propose MoE-nD, a mixture-of-experts framework that routes each layer to its own (eviction-ratio, K-bits, V-bits) tuple under a global memory budget. An offline-calibrated greedy solver chooses the routing that minimizes predicted quality loss; at inference time, per-layer heterogeneous eviction and quantization are applied jointly through a single attention patch. On a 4-task subset of LongBench-v1 (16k inputs, n=50 per task, adapted reasoning-model protocol; see section Experiments), MoE-nD's hetero variant matches our uncompressed 1.9~GB baseline at 14x compression (136~MB) while every other compressed baseline we tested (1d, 2d_uniform, 2d) at comparable or smaller memory stays under 8/100. The gains hold on AIME reasoning benchmarks (+6 to +27 pts over the strongest per-layer-quantization baseline across eight configurations). Two null results -- MATH-500 and LongBench's TREC -- share a principled cause (short inputs, solver picks keep=1.0 on most layers), cleanly characterizing when per-layer eviction routing has headroom to help.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies bottlenecks in KV cache memory for long-context LLMs and introduces MoE-nD, a method offering per-layer compression strategies, achieving significant memory savings without quality loss.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to improve memory efficiency in long-context LLM inference by addressing non-uniform responses to current homogeneous compression methods.
AI.method: The authors propose MoE-nD, which applies a mixture-of-experts framework to determine optimal per-layer (eviction-ratio, K-bits, V-bits) under a global memory budget using an offline-calibrated solver.
AI.result: MoE-nD achieves up to 14x compression with minimal quality loss, outperforming baseline compression techniques on LongBench-v1 and AIME reasoning benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed heterogeneous per-layer compression approach is effective and highlights when per-layer routing contributes meaningfully to performance.

title: RePrompT: Recurrent Prompt Tuning for Integrating Structured EHR Encoders with Large Language Models
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown strong promise for mining Electronic Health Records (EHRs) by reasoning over longitudinal clinical information to capture context-rich patient trajectories. However, leveraging LLMs for structured EHRs (e.g., standardized diagnosis and medication codes) presents two key challenges. First, translating time-stamped EHR sequences into plain text can obscure both temporal structure and code identities, weakening the ability to capture code co-occurrence and longitudinal regularities. Second, unlike cohort-trained predictive models that learn a shared, task-aligned representation space across patients, LLMs are often applied in a case-isolated inference setting where each patient is processed independently without leveraging population-level patterns. To address these challenges, we introduce RePrompT, a time-aware LLM framework that integrates structured EHR encoders through prompt tuning, without modifying underlying architectures. Specifically, RePrompT recurrently incorporates latent states from prior visits to preserve longitudinal information, and injects population-level information through trainable prompt tokens derived from a cohort-trained, task-aligned EHR encoder. Experiments on MIMIC-III and MIMIC-IV demonstrate that RePrompT consistently outperforms both EHR-based and LLM-based baselines across multiple clinical prediction tasks.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces RePrompT, an LLM framework designed to better leverage structured EHRs for clinical prediction tasks. It addresses challenges of temporal structure loss and isolated inference by preserving longitudinal information and adding population-level context through prompt tuning.
AI.motivation: LLMs face limitations in processing structured EHRs due to loss of temporal and co-occurrence information, as well as lacking cohort-level insights in case-isolated inference settings.
AI.method: RePrompT enables time-aware LLMs by recurrently integrating latent states from previous visits and introducing prompt tokens derived from cohort-trained EHR encoders, without altering model architectures.
AI.result: RePrompT demonstrated superior performance against both EHR-based and LLM-based methods across various clinical prediction tasks on MIMIC-III and MIMIC-IV datasets.
AI.conclusion: Integrating structured EHR encoders and time-aware prompt tuning allows LLMs to better capture patient trajectories and population-level patterns, improving clinical predictions.

title: Learning from Less: Measuring the Effectiveness of RLVR in Low Data and Compute Regimes
summary: Fine-tuning Large Language Models (LLMs) typically relies on large quantities of high-quality annotated data, or questions with well-defined ground truth answers in the case of Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR). While previous work has explored the benefits to model reasoning capabilities by scaling both data and compute used for RLVR, these results lack applicability in many real-world settings where annotated data and accessible compute may be scarce. In this work, we present a comprehensive empirical study of open-source Small Language Model (SLM) performance after RLVR in low data regimes. Across three novel datasets covering number counting problems, graph reasoning, and spatial reasoning, we characterize how model performance scales with dataset size, diversity, and complexity. We demonstrate that (1) procedural datasets allow for fine-grained evaluation and training dataset development with controllable properties (size, diversity, and complexity), (2) under RLVR, models trained on lower complexity tasks can generalize to higher complexity tasks, and (3) training on mixed complexity datasets is associated with the greatest benefits in low data regimes, providing up to 5x sample efficiency versus training on easy tasks. These findings inspire future work on the development of data scaling laws for RLVR and the use of procedural data generators to further understand effective data development for efficient LLM fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: The study investigates the fine-tuning of small language models using reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) in low-data settings, focusing on how dataset size, diversity, and complexity impact performance.
AI.motivation: Motivated by the need to enhance language model reasoning in real-world low-data and low-compute scenarios, which are not addressed by scaling large data and compute resources.
AI.method: Empirical experiments on small language models across three datasets—number counting, graph reasoning, and spatial reasoning—which vary in size, diversity, and complexity. Procedural datasets with controllable properties are used for training and evaluation.
AI.result: It was found that procedural datasets enable fine-grained data control, lower-complexity tasks help models generalize to higher-complexity tasks, and mixed-complexity datasets are the most beneficial in low-data settings, offering up to 5x sample efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The study emphasizes the value of procedural data and mixed-complexity datasets for efficient fine-tuning of small language models, suggesting scaling laws and further data generator development to optimize RLVR in resource-constrained settings.

title: CDSA-Net:Collaborative Decoupling of Vascular Structure and Background for High-Fidelity Coronary Digital Subtraction Angiography
summary: Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) in coronary imaging is fundamentally challenged by physiological motion, forcing reliance on raw angiograms cluttered with anatomical noise. Existing deep learning methods often produced images with two critical clinically unacceptable flaws: persistent boundary artifacts and a loss of native tissue grayscale fidelity that undermined diagnostic confidence. We propose a novel framework termed as CDSA-Net that for the first time explicitly decouples and jointly optimizes vascular structure preservation and realistic background restoration. CDSA-Net introduces two core innovations: (i) A hierarchical geometric prior guidance (HGPG) mechanism, embedded in our coronary structure extraction network (CSENet). It synergistically combines integrated geometric prior (IGP) with gated spatial modulation (GSM) and centerline-aware topology (CAT) loss supervision, ensuring structural continuity. (ii) An adaptive noise module (ANM) within our coronary background restoration network (CBResNet). Unlike standard restoration, ANM uniquely models the stochastic nature of clinical X-ray noise, bridging the domain gap to enable seamless background intensity estimation and the complete elimination of boundary artifacts. The final subtraction is obtained by removing the restored background from the raw angiogram. Quantitatively, it significantly outperformed state-of-the-art methods in vascular intensity correlation and perceptual quality. A 25.6% improvement in morphology assessment efficiency and a 42.9% gain in hemodynamic evaluation speed set a new benchmark for utility in interventional cardiology, while maintaining diagnostic results consistent with raw angiograms. The project code is available at https://github.com/DrThink-ai/CDSA-Net.

AI.tldr: The paper presents CDSA-Net, a framework addressing motion and noise issues in coronary imaging by decoupling vascular structure preservation and background restoration, achieving improved diagnostic quality and efficiency.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address key limitations in coronary imaging using digital subtraction angiography (DSA), such as motion-induced artifacts and loss of grayscale fidelity, which undermine diagnostic confidence.
AI.method: The authors introduce CDSA-Net, which integrates a hierarchical geometric prior guidance (HGPG) for vascular structure continuity and an adaptive noise module (ANM) for realistic background restoration. This dual-network strategy ensures seamless subtraction of background from the raw angiogram.
AI.result: CDSA-Net outperformed state-of-the-art methods regarding vascular intensity correlation and perceptual quality. It achieved a 25.6% improvement in morphology assessment efficiency and a 42.9% gain in hemodynamic evaluation speed while maintaining diagnostic consistency.
AI.conclusion: CDSA-Net effectively overcomes the challenges in coronary imaging by ensuring structural and diagnostic reliability, setting a new benchmark for interventional cardiology.

title: The Geometric Canary: Predicting Steerability and Detecting Drift via Representational Stability
summary: Reliable deployment of language models requires two capabilities that appear distinct but share a common geometric foundation: predicting whether a model will accept targeted behavioral control, and detecting when its internal structure degrades. We show that geometric stability, the consistency of a representation's pairwise distance structure, addresses both. Supervised Shesha variants that measure task-aligned geometric stability predict linear steerability with near-perfect accuracy ($ρ= 0.89$-$0.97$) across 35-69 embedding models and three NLP tasks, capturing unique variance beyond class separability (partial $ρ= 0.62$-$0.76$). A critical dissociation emerges: unsupervised stability fails entirely for steering on real-world tasks ($ρ\approx 0.10$), revealing that task alignment is essential for controllability prediction. However, unsupervised stability excels at drift detection, measuring nearly $2\times$ greater geometric change than CKA during post-training alignment (up to $5.23\times$ in Llama) while providing earlier warning in 73\% of models and maintaining a $6\times$ lower false alarm rate than Procrustes. Together, supervised and unsupervised stability form complementary diagnostics for the LLM deployment lifecycle: one for pre-deployment controllability assessment, the other for post-deployment monitoring.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces geometric stability as a shared foundation for predicting steerability and detecting structural degradation in language models, offering complementary supervised and unsupervised methods.
AI.motivation: To reliably deploy language models, it is essential to predict whether a model responds to behavioral control and to detect degradation in its internal structure, both of which require a common diagnostic approach.
AI.method: The paper examines task-aligned geometric stability (supervised) for predicting controllability and unsupervised geometric stability for drift detection across embedding models and NLP tasks, using both novel metrics and comparative benchmarks.
AI.result: Supervised stability accurately predicts steerability (ρ = 0.89–0.97), outperforming class separability metrics, while unsupervised stability excels at detecting structural drift with 2–5x greater geometric sensitivity and lower false alarm rates.
AI.conclusion: Supervised and unsupervised geometric stability offer effective diagnostic tools throughout the lifecycle of LLMs, ensuring reliable deployment and monitoring systems for diverse NLP tasks.

title: MHSafeEval: Role-Aware Interaction-Level Evaluation of Mental Health Safety in Large Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly explored as scalable tools for mental health counseling, yet evaluating their safety remains challenging due to the interactional and context-dependent nature of clinical harm. Existing evaluation frameworks predominantly assess isolated responses using coarse-grained taxonomies or static datasets, limiting their ability to diagnose how harms emerge and accumulate over multi-turn counseling interactions. In this work, we introduce R-MHSafe, a role-aware mental health safety taxonomy that characterizes clinically significant harm in terms of the interactional roles an AI counselor adopts, including perpetrator, instigator, facilitator, or enabler, combined with clinically grounded harm categories. Then, we propose MHSafeEval, a closed-loop, agent-based evaluation framework that formulates safety assessment as trajectory-level discovery of harm through adversarial multi-turn interactions, guided by role-aware modeling. Using R-MHSafe and MHSafeEval, we conduct a large-scale evaluation across state-of-the-art LLMs. Our results reveal substantial role-dependent and cumulative safety failures that are systematically missed by existing static benchmarks, and show that our framework significantly improves failure-mode coverage and diagnostic granularity.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the limitations of static evaluation methods for large language models in mental health counseling by introducing R-MHSafe, a taxonomy for interaction roles, and MHSafeEval, a dynamic analysis tool.
AI.motivation: Existing methods fail to capture interactional, role-based, and cumulative harm over multi-turn conversations in mental health counseling provided by LLMs.
AI.method: The authors propose R-MHSafe to categorize AI counselor roles in harm dynamics, and MHSafeEval for adversarial role-aware multi-turn interaction evaluations.
AI.result: Large-scale evaluation using the new framework reveals significant role-dependent and cumulative safety failures in current LLMs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enhances harm detection coverage and granularity compared to static benchmarks, enabling better identification of safety issues.

title: Six Llamas: Comparative Religious Ethics Through LoRA-Adapted Language Models
comment: 51 pages, 14 figures. We present Six Llamas, a comparative study examining whether Llama-3.1-8B models fine-tuned on distinct religious corpora encode systematically different patterns of ethical reasoning. Five LoRA-adapted variants are constructed for Christianity, Islam, Judaism, Hinduism, and Buddhism. For theoretical background on the condensate comparative method, see arXiv:2603.07329
summary:
We present Six Llamas, a comparative study examining whether large language models fine-tuned on distinct religious corpora encode systematically different patterns of ethical reasoning. Six variants of Meta-Llama-3.1-8B are constructed: one unmodified control and five LoRA-adapted models trained exclusively on the sacred and theological texts of Christianity, Islam, Judaism, Hinduism, or Buddhism. All six models are probed with an identical battery of 17 standardized ethical prompts spanning moral dilemmas, game-theoretic scenarios, public policy questions, and moral-psychological self-assessments. To assess robustness and reproducibility, we implement a multi-temperature sampling design spanning ten temperature settings. We compute response consistency metrics, pairwise inter-model agreement rates, temperature sensitivity coefficients across four prompt domains, and run-to-run stability analyses.
  Findings show that LoRA-adapted models produce ethical reasoning patterns that are (a) systematically differentiated from the base model, (b) consistent with the moral logics of their training traditions, (c) structured along interpretable dimensions in moral-philosophical space, (d) core ethical positions remain stable across temperature variations for high-consensus dilemmas. The Trolley Problem achieves 100% consistency across all models and temperatures, while (e) tradition-specific divergence intensifies at higher temperatures in morally contested domains, and (f) the base model exhibits the highest overall response consistency (mean 88.3%), suggesting LoRA adaptation introduces both tradition-specific signal and increased sampling sensitivity.
  The study offers a proof-of-concept for the condensate comparative method using differentially trained language models as instruments for cultural and ethical analysis and identifies specific criteria for falsification and planned extensions.

AI.tldr: The study compares ethical reasoning in large language models fine-tuned on texts from five major religions, revealing differing moral logics and influences from their training traditions.
AI.motivation: To explore whether religiously fine-tuned large language models encode distinct ethical reasoning patterns consistent with their respective training traditions.
AI.method: Six Meta-Llama-3.1-8B models were studied: one control model and five models fine-tuned on religious texts via LoRA. Models were tested using 17 ethical prompts, across temperature settings. Metrics like response consistency, inter-model agreement, and stability were evaluated.
AI.result: LoRA-tuned models showed ethical reasoning aligned with their religious traditions. Core ethical answers were stable, but tradition-driven divergences surfaced under specific conditions.
AI.conclusion: LoRA adaptation imbues models with tradition-specific ethical reasoning but also increases sensitivity to sampling. The research provides a methodology for using fine-tuned language models for cultural and ethical studies.

title: DREAM: Dynamic Retinal Enhancement with Adaptive Multi-modal Fusion for Expert Precision Medical Report Generation
comment: Accepted at the IEEE Engineering in Medicine and Biology Society Annual International Conference (Proceedings of the 48th International Conference), 2026
summary: Automating medical reports for retinal images requires a sophisticated blend of visual pattern recognition and deep clinical knowledge. Current Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) often struggle in specialized medical fields where data is scarce, leading to models that overfit and miss subtle but critical pathologies. To address this, we introduce DREAM (Dynamic Retinal Enhancement with Adaptive Multi-modal Fusion), a novel framework for high-fidelity medical report generation that excels even with limited data. DREAM employs a unique two-stage fusion mechanism that intelligently integrates visual data with clinical keywords curated by ophthalmologists. First, the Abstractor module maps image and keyword features into a shared space, enhancing visual data with pathology-relevant insights. Next, the Adaptor performs adaptive multi-modal fusion, dynamically weighting the importance of each modality using learnable parameters to create a unified representation. To ensure the model's outputs are semantically grounded in clinical reality, a Contrastive Alignment module aligns these fused representations with ground-truth medical reports during training. By combining medical expertise with an efficient fusion strategy, DREAM sets a new state-of-the-art on the DeepEyeNet benchmark, achieving a BLEU-4 score of 0.241, and further demonstrates strong generalization to the ROCO dataset.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DREAM, a new framework for generating accurate medical reports for retinal images, addressing challenges in specialized medical fields with limited data by employing a two-stage fusion mechanism and contrastive alignment.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to overcome the limitations of current Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs), which often fail in specialized medical domains with scarce data, particularly in retinal image diagnostics.
AI.method: The DREAM framework utilizes a two-stage fusion mechanism: the Abstractor enhances visual data using clinical keyword mappings, while the Adaptor dynamically fuses modalities. A Contrastive Alignment module ensures fidelity with clinical ground truth during training.
AI.result: DREAM achieves state-of-the-art results on the DeepEyeNet benchmark with a BLEU-4 score of 0.241 and shows strong performance on the ROCO dataset.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that DREAM effectively integrates visual data with clinical knowledge to address data scarcity and model overfitting, setting a new standard for automated retinal medical reporting.

title: Modeling Higher-Order Brain Interactions via a Multi-View Information Bottleneck Framework for fMRI-based Psychiatric Diagnosis
summary: Resting-state functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) has emerged as a cornerstone for psychiatric diagnosis, yet most approaches rely on pairwise brain cortical or sub-cortical connectivities that overlooks higher-order interactions (HOIs) central to complex brain dynamics. While hypergraph methods encode HOIs through predefined hyperedges, their construction typically relies on heuristic similarity metrics and does not explicitly characterize whether interactions are synergy- or redundancy-dominated. In this paper, we introduce $O$-information, a signed measure that characterizes the informational nature of HOIs, and integrate third- and fourth-order $O$-information into a unified multi-view information bottleneck framework for fMRI-based psychiatric diagnosis. To enable scalable $O$-information estimation, we further develop two independent acceleration strategies: a Gaussian analytical approximation and a randomized matrix-based Rényi entropy estimator, achieving over a 30-fold computational speedup compared with conventional estimators. Our tri-view architecture systematically fuses pairwise, triadic, and tetradic brain interactions, capturing comprehensive brain connectivity while explicitly penalizing redundancy. Extensive evaluation across four benchmark datasets (REST-meta-MDD, ABIDE, UCLA, ADNI) demonstrates consistent improvements, outperforming 11 baseline methods including state-of-the-art graph neural network (GNN) and hypergraph based approaches. Moreover, our method reveals interpretable region-level synergy-redundancy patterns which are not explicitly characterized by conventional hypergraph formulations.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a novel $O$-information-based framework for higher-order brain interaction analysis in fMRI-based psychiatric diagnosis, showcasing both computational efficiency and diagnostic performance improvements.
AI.motivation: Current methods for fMRI-based psychiatric diagnosis often focus on pairwise brain connectivities and fail to capture higher-order interactions critical for understanding complex brain dynamics.
AI.method: The paper develops an $O$-information measure for higher-order interactions and incorporates it into a multi-view information bottleneck framework. Two acceleration strategies for computation are introduced: a Gaussian approximation and a randomized matrix-based Rényi entropy estimator.
AI.result: The proposed approach demonstrates over 30-fold computational speedup and consistent performance improvements across four benchmark psychiatric datasets, outperforming 11 baseline models.
AI.conclusion: This novel $O$-information-based framework provides a robust and interpretable approach for psychiatric diagnosis by capturing synergy-redundancy patterns in higher-order brain interactions.

title: Mira-Embeddings-V1: Domain-Adapted Semantic Reranking for Recruitment via LLM-Synthesized Data
summary: Candidate sourcing for recruiters is best viewed as a two-stage retrieval and reranking pipeline with recall as the primary objective under a limited review budget. An upstream production retriever first returns a candidate shortlist for each job description (JD), and our goal is to rerank that shortlist so that qualified candidates appear as high as possible. We present mira-embeddings-v1, a semantic reranking system for the recruitment domain that reshapes the embedding space with LLM-synthesized training data and corrects boundary confusions with a lightweight reranking head. Starting from real JDs, we build a five-stage prompt pipeline to generate diverse positive and hard negative samples that sculpt the semantic space from multiple angles. We then apply a two-round LoRA adaptation: JD--JD contrastive training followed by JD--CV triplet alignment on a heterogeneous text dataset. Importantly, these gains require no large-scale manually labeled industrial training pairs: a modest set of real JDs is expanded into supervision through LLM synthesis. Finally, a BoundaryHead MLP reranks the Top-K results to distinguish between roles that share the same title but differ in scope. On a local pool of 300 real JDs with candidates from an upstream production retriever, mira-embeddings-v1 improves Recall@50 from 68.89% (baseline) to 77.55% while lifting Precision@10 from 35.77% to 39.62%. On a supportive global pool over 44,138 candidates judged by a Qwen3-32B rubric, it achieves Recall@200 of 0.7047 versus 0.5969 for the baseline. These results show that LLM-synthesized supervision with boundary-aware reranking yields robust gains without a heavy cross-encoder.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces mira-embeddings-v1, a semantic reranking system tailored for recruitment, which reshapes embedding spaces using LLM-synthesized data and improves candidate ranking without extensive manual labeling.
AI.motivation: Recruiter workflows need efficient candidate sourcing under limited review budgets; optimizing ranking pipelines is critical to ensure qualified candidates appear at the top.
AI.method: The system uses LLM-generated data to train embeddings through a five-stage prompt pipeline, applies a two-round LoRA adaptation for alignment, and integrates a lightweight MLP head for reranking to reduce boundary confusions.
AI.result: Mira-embeddings-v1 significantly improves metrics like Recall@50 (68.89% to 77.55%) and Precision@10 (35.77% to 39.62%) on a local pool, and boosts Recall@200 (0.5969 to 0.7047) on a global pool.
AI.conclusion: LLM-driven synthetic supervision combined with boundary-friendly reranking demonstrates strong performance without relying on costly manual labeled data.

title: EmbodiedHead: Real-Time Listening and Speaking Avatar for Conversational Agents
summary: We present EmbodiedHead, a speech-driven talking-head framework that equips LLMs with real-time visual avatars for conversation. A practical embodied avatar must achieve real-time generation, unified listening-speaking behavior, and high rendered visual quality simultaneously. Our framework couples the first Rectified-Flow Diffusion Transformer (DiT) for this task with a differentiable renderer, enabling diverse, high-fidelity generation in as few as four sampling steps. Prior listening-speaking methods rely on dual-stream audio, introducing an interlocutor look-ahead dependency incompatible with causal user--LLM interaction. We instead adopt a single-stream interface with explicit per-frame listening-speaking state conditioning and a Streaming Audio Scheduler, suppressing spurious mouth motion during listening while enabling seamless turn-taking. A two-stage training scheme of coefficient-space pretraining and joint image-domain refinement further closes the gap between motion-level supervision and rendered quality. Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art visual quality and motion fidelity in both speaking and listening scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EmbodiedHead, a speech-driven talking-head framework for real-time LLM-based avatars, achieving seamless listening-speaking behavior and high visual quality using novel techniques.
AI.motivation: To develop a practical talking-head framework for LLMs, balancing real-time performance, unified conversational behavior, and high-quality visual rendering.
AI.method: The authors present a Rectified-Flow Diffusion Transformer coupled with a differentiable renderer for fast, high-fidelity generation. A single-stream interface with state conditioning ensures seamless turn-taking, supported by a Streaming Audio Scheduler and a two-stage training strategy.
AI.result: EmbodiedHead achieves state-of-the-art quality in visual rendering and motion fidelity during speaking and listening tasks.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully addresses previous limitations in speech-driven talking-head avatars, making it suitable for real-time, visually appealing conversational applications.

title: FlashFPS: Efficient Farthest Point Sampling for Large-Scale Point Clouds via Pruning and Caching
summary: Point-based Neural Networks (PNNs) have become a key approach for point cloud processing. However, a core operation in these models, Farthest Point Sampling (FPS), often introduces significant inference latency, especially for large-scale processing. Despite existing CUDA- and hardware-level optimizations, FPS remains a major bottleneck due to exhaustive computations across multiple network layers in PNNs, which hinders scalability. Through systematic analysis, we identify three substantial redundancies in FPS, including unnecessary full-cloud computations, redundant late-stage iterations, and predictable inter-layer outputs that make later FPS computations avoidable. To address these, we propose \textbf{\textit{FlashFPS}}, a hardware-agnostic, plug-and-play framework for FPS acceleration, composed of \textit{FPS-Prune} and \textit{FPS-Cache}. \textit{FPS-Prune} introduces candidate pruning and iteration pruning to reduce redundant computations in FPS while preserving sampling quality, and \textit{FPS-Cache} eliminates layer-wise redundancy via cache-and-reuse. Integrated into existing CUDA libraries and state-of-the-art PNN accelerators, \textit{FlashFPS} achieves 5.16$\times$ speedup over the standard CUDA baseline on GPU and 2.69$\times$ on PNN accelerators, with negligible accuracy loss, enabling efficient and scalable PNN inference. Codes are released at https://github.com/Yuzhe-Fu/FlashFPS.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FlashFPS, a framework to significantly accelerate Farthest Point Sampling (FPS) in Point-based Neural Networks (PNNs), addressing scalability bottlenecks while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: FPS is a performance bottleneck in PNNs due to redundancies and computational inefficiencies, hindering their scalability for large-scale point cloud processing.
AI.method: FlashFPS framework includes FPS-Prune, which minimizes redundant computations via candidate and iteration pruning, and FPS-Cache, which caches and reuses predictable layer-wise outputs to eliminate redundancy.
AI.result: FlashFPS provides a 5.16× speedup on GPUs and 2.69× on PNN accelerators compared to the standard CUDA baseline, with negligible accuracy loss.
AI.conclusion: FlashFPS is a scalable and efficient solution for accelerating FPS in PNNs, integrating seamlessly with existing systems while preserving sampling quality.

title: HiRAS: A Hierarchical Multi-Agent Framework for Paper-to-Code Generation and Execution
summary: Recent advances in large language models have highlighted their potential to automate computational research, particularly reproducing experimental results. However, existing approaches still use fixed sequential agent pipelines with weak global coordination, which limits their robustness and overall performance. In this work, we propose Hierarchical Research Agent System (HiRAS), a hierarchical multi-agent framework for end-to-end experiment reproduction that employs supervisory manager agents to coordinate specialised agents across fine-grained stages. We also identify limitations in the reference-free evaluation of the Paper2Code benchmark and introduce Paper2Code-Extra (P2C-Ex), a refined protocol that incorporates repository-level information and better aligns with the original reference-based metric. We conduct extensive evaluation, validating the effectiveness and robustness of our proposed methods, and observing improvements, including >10\% relative performance gain beyond the previous state-of-the-art using open-source backbone models and significantly reduced hallucination in evaluation. Our work is available on GitHub: https://github.com/KOU-199024/HiRAS.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces HiRAS, a hierarchical multi-agent framework enhancing computational experiment reproduction, alongside a refined benchmark protocol, P2C-Ex, to improve evaluation reliability. This combination significantly boosts performance and robustness compared to prior efforts.
AI.motivation: Large language models show potential for automating experiment reproduction, but current systems lack effective global coordination, reducing performance and robustness. This motivated the authors to design a hierarchical, better-coordinated agent system.
AI.method: The authors propose HiRAS, a multi-agent hierarchical framework with supervisory manager agents to coordinate specialized agents. They also enhance the Paper2Code benchmarking protocol by introducing P2C-Ex, which incorporates repository-level information.
AI.result: The proposed HiRAS framework demonstrated a >10% performance improvement over previous state-of-the-art methods using open-source models. It also showed reduced hallucination in evaluations.
AI.conclusion: HiRAS is a robust and effective system for computational experiment reproduction. Combined with the improved evaluation protocol P2C-Ex, it outperforms existing methods and sets a new benchmark standard for the Paper2Code task.

title: A Generalized Synthetic Control Method for Baseline Estimation in Demand Response Services
summary: Baseline estimation is critical to Demand Response (DR) settlement in electricity markets, yet existing machine learning methods remain limited in predictive performance, while methodologies from causal inference and counterfactual prediction are still underutilized in this domain. We introduce a Generalized Synthetic Control Method that builds on the classical Synthetic Control Method (SCM) from econometrics. While SCM provides a powerful framework for counterfactual estimation, classical SCM remains a static estimator: it fits the treated unit as a combination of contemporaneous donor units and therefore ignores predictable temporal structure in the residual error. We develop a generalized SCM framework that transforms baseline estimation into a dynamic counterfactual prediction problem by augmenting the donor representation with exogenous features, lagged treated load, and selected lagged donor signals. This enriched representation allows the estimator to capture autoregressive dependence, delayed donor-response patterns, and error-correction effects beyond the scope of standard SCM. The framework further accommodates nonlinear predictors when linear weighting is inadequate, with the greatest benefit arising in limited-data settings. Experiments on the Ausgrid smart-meter dataset show consistent improvements over classical SCM and strong benchmark methods, with the dominant performance gains driven by dynamic augmentation.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an enhanced Synthetic Control Method (SCM) for dynamic counterfactual prediction in electricity market baseline estimation, improving results through temporal structure incorporation and enriched features.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of existing machine learning techniques and underutilized causal inference methodologies in demand response settlement by refining counterfactual estimation methods.
AI.method: Developed a Generalized SCM framework that augments classical SCM with exogenous features, lagged signals, and dynamic elements to capture temporal structures and nonlinear patterns.
AI.result: Experiments on the Ausgrid dataset demonstrate consistent performance improvement over traditional SCM and benchmarks, particularly in limited-data scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic augmentation significantly enhances baseline estimation accuracy, proving effective for capturing complex dependencies and refining counterfactual prediction.

title: Cross-Modal Attention Analysis and Optimization in Vision-Language Models: A Study on Visual Reliability
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) achieve strong cross-modal performance, yet recent evidence suggests they over-rely on textual descriptions while under-utilizing visual evidence -- a phenomenon termed ``text shortcut learning.'' We propose an adversarial evaluation framework that quantifies this cross-modal dependency by measuring accuracy degradation (Drop) when semantically conflicting text is paired with unchanged images. Four adversarial strategies -- shape\_swap, color\_swap, position\_swap, and random\_text -- are applied to a controlled geometric-shapes dataset ($n{=}1{,}000$). We compare three configurations: Baseline CLIP (ViT-B/32), LoRA fine-tuning, and LoRA Optimized (integrating Hard Negative Mining, Label Smoothing, layer-wise learning rates, Cosine Restarts, curriculum learning, and data augmentation). The optimized model reduces average Drop from 27.5\% to 9.8\% (64.4\% relative improvement, $p{<}0.001$) while maintaining 97\% normal accuracy. Attention visualization and embedding-space analysis confirm that the optimized model attends more to visual features and achieves tighter cross-modal alignment.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses text shortcut learning in vision-language models by proposing an adversarial evaluation and optimizing techniques to reduce reliance on textual descriptions while boosting visual feature utilization.
AI.motivation: Recent evidence indicates vision-language models excessively rely on text rather than visual data, leading to inadequate performance in cross-modal settings.
AI.method: An adversarial evaluation framework with four strategies is applied to a geometric-shapes dataset, and various optimization techniques are introduced for LoRA fine-tuning to improve the models.
AI.result: The optimized model significantly reduces cross-modal dependency, improving average degradation from 27.5% to 9.8%, while retaining high accuracy at 97%.
AI.conclusion: Optimizations improve the balance between text and visual feature reliance, leading to better cross-modal alignment without sacrificing accuracy.

title: Tool Learning Needs Nothing More Than a Free 8B Language Model
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a prevalent paradigm for training tool calling agents, which typically requires online interactive environments. Existing approaches either rely on training data with ground truth annotations or require advanced commercial language models (LMs) to synthesize environments that keep fixed once created. In this work, we propose TRUSTEE, a data-free method training tool calling agents with dynamic environments fully simulated by free open-source LMs that can be as small as 8B, including task generation, user simulation, tool simulation and trajectory evaluation, paired with an adaptive curriculum learning mechanism that controls various aspects of the task difficulty dynamically during training. Our empirical results show that TRUSTEE brings consistent improvements across various domains and outperforms all the baselines which require extra external resources for training. These confirm that, with a sufficiently sophisticated design, even simulated environments with a local 8B LM as the backbone could set a strong baseline for tool learning, without expensive annotated data, realistic human interactions, executable tools or costly verifiable environments from human experts or commercial LMs. We hope our proposed paradigm could inspire future research on environment scaling with limited resources.

AI.tldr: TRUSTEE is a data-free RL method for training tool-calling agents using dynamically simulated environments with open-source language models.
AI.motivation: To address the high costs and resource dependencies of existing RL approaches for training tool-calling agents, such as reliance on annotated data, advanced language models, or interaction with realistic environments.
AI.method: TRUSTEE uses open-source language models and a dynamic environment simulation with adaptive curriculum learning, including task creation, user interaction, tool simulation, and trajectory evaluation.
AI.result: Empirical tests show TRUSTEE consistently outperforms baselines that utilize additional external resources, proving the efficacy of its design.
AI.conclusion: With a well-designed framework, even inexpensive and localized language models can establish strong baselines for tool learning, offering a scalable and resource-efficient approach.

title: Reverse Constitutional AI: A Framework for Controllable Toxic Data Generation via Probability-Clamped RLAIF
comment: Accepted to Findings of ACL 2026. 10 pages, 6 figures. Code and data available at https://github.com/ZeroLoss-Lab/R-CAI
summary: Ensuring the safety of large language models (LLMs) requires robust red teaming, yet the systematic synthesis of high-quality toxic data remains under-explored. We propose Reverse Constitutional AI (R-CAI), a framework for automated and controllable adversarial data generation that moves beyond isolated jailbreak prompts. By inverting a harmless constitution into a constitution of toxicity and iteratively refining model outputs through a critique--revision pipeline, R-CAI enables scalable synthesis of multi-dimensional adversarial data without human annotation. Optimizing solely for toxicity-related rewards, however, can lead to reward hacking and degraded semantic coherence. To address this challenge, we introduce probability clamping within reinforcement learning from AI feedback, which stabilizes adversarial optimization while preserving adversarial intent. Experiments demonstrate that R-CAI generates diverse, high-quality toxic data and that probability clamping substantially improves semantic coherence (15%) without sacrificing adversarial strength. Overall, R-CAI provides a fully automated framework for red teaming data generation and systematic safety evaluation of aligned language models.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Reverse Constitutional AI (R-CAI), a framework for generating adversarial toxic data automatically and effectively using reinforcement learning techniques, specifically for red teaming large language models.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the limited exploration in generating high-quality, adversarial toxic data systematically, aiming to enhance the safety and robustness of large language models through better red teaming methods.
AI.method: The method involves inverting a harmless 'constitution' into one of toxicity and applying an iterative critique-revision pipeline. Additionally, probability clamping in reinforcement learning is introduced to maintain semantic coherence while optimizing for toxicity.
AI.result: The proposed R-CAI method successfully generates diverse, high-quality toxic data. Probability clamping improves semantic coherence by 15% while maintaining the adversarial intent and strength.
AI.conclusion: R-CAI is a fully automated, scalable framework for generating adversarial data and evaluating the safety of language models, offering advancements in systematic red teaming techniques.

title: WorldDB: A Vector Graph-of-Worlds Memory Engine with Ontology-Aware Write-Time Reconciliation
summary: Persistent memory is the bottleneck separating stateless chatbots from long-running agentic systems. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) over flat vector stores fragments facts into chunks, loses cross-session identity, and has no first-class notion of supersession or contradiction. Recent bitemporal knowledge-graph systems (Graphiti, Memento, Hydra DB) add typed edges and valid-time metadata, but the graph itself remains flat: no recursive composition, no content-addressed invariants on nodes, and edge types carry no behavior beyond a label. We present WorldDB, a memory engine built on three commitments: (i) every node is a world -- a container with its own interior subgraph, ontology scope, and composed embedding, recursive to arbitrary depth; (ii) nodes are content-addressed and immutable, so any edit produces a new hash at the node and every ancestor, giving a Merkle-style audit trail for free; (iii) edges are write-time programs -- each edge type ships on_insert/on_delete/on_query_rewrite handlers (supersession closes validity, contradicts preserves both sides, same_as stages a merge proposal), so no raw append path exists. On LongMemEval-s (500 questions, ~115k-token conversational stacks), WorldDB with Claude Opus 4.7 as answerer achieves 96.40% overall / 97.11% task-averaged accuracy, a +5.61pp improvement over the previously reported Hydra DB state-of-the-art (90.79%) and +11.20pp over Supermemory (85.20%), with perfect single-session-assistant recall and robust performance on temporal reasoning (96.24%), knowledge update (98.72%), and preference synthesis (96.67%).
