Consequently, AI-generated formal proofs can exhibit semantic hallucination-passing the type checker yet failing to express the intended mathematics. We propose a human-in-the-loop approach in which human scientists and AI collaboratively produce formal proofs, with humans responsible for the semantic verification of propositions and definitions. To realize this approach, we develop Lean Atlas, a Lean 4 tool that visualizes the dependency graph of a Lean 4 project as an interactive web viewer, enabling human scientists to grasp the overall structure of a formalization efficiently. Its core feature, Lean Compass, is an algorithm that, given a selected theorem set, automatically extracts the project-specific nodes whose semantic correctness can affect those target statements, thereby reducing the candidate set for semantic review in large-scale formalizations. We further define *aligned Lean code* as formalization code that has undergone human semantic verification, and propose it as a quality standard for AI-generated formalizations. We evaluate the tool on six Lean 4 formalization projects with different structural characteristics; proof-heavy projects (PrimeNumberTheoremAnd, Carleson, Brownian Motion) achieved 94-99% average node reduction, a 6-theorem milestone subset of FLT achieved 59.8%, mixed PhysLib 69.0%, and definition-heavy XMSS 27.3%. Lean Atlas is available as open-source software at https://github.com/NyxFoundation/lean-atlas .

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Lean Atlas, a tool to tackle semantic hallucination in AI-generated formal proofs by visualizing the dependency graph of Lean 4 projects, aiding human semantic verification.
AI.motivation: AI-generated formal proofs may pass the type checker without accurately capturing intended mathematical meanings, posing a challenge for their reliability.
AI.method: The authors propose a human-in-the-loop system complemented by Lean Atlas, a Lean 4 tool that visualizes project dependencies and uses the Lean Compass algorithm to prioritize nodes for semantic review.
AI.result: On six Lean 4 formalization projects, Lean Atlas achieved 27.3%-99% average node reduction, streamlining human semantic verification across various project types.
AI.conclusion: Lean Atlas aids in addressing semantic issues in mathematical AI formalizations and establishes aligned Lean code as a quality benchmark for such integrations.

title: SegTTA: Training-Free Test-Time Augmentation for Zero-Shot Medical Imaging Segmentation
summary: Increasingly advanced data augmentation techniques have greatly aided clinical medical research, increasing data diversity and improving model generalization capabilities. Although most current basic models exhibit strong generalization abilities, image quality varies due to differences in equipment and operators. To address these challenges, we present SegTTA, a framework that improves medical image segmentation without model retraining by combining four augmentations (Gamma correction, Contrast enhancement, Gaussian blur, Gaussian noise) with weighted voting across multiple MedSAM2 checkpoints. Experiments demonstrate consistent improvements across three diverse datasets: healthy uterus segmentation, uterine myoma detection, and multi class hepatic structure segmentation. Ablation studies reveal that large organs benefit from intensity augmentations while small lesions require noise augmentations. The voting threshold controls the coverage precision trade off, enabling task specific optimization for different clinical requirements. Ultimately, on a multiclass hepatic vessel dataset, compared to MedSAM2 baselines, our method achieves an increase of 1.6 in mIoU and 1.9 in aIoU, along with a reduction of approximately 2.0 in HD95. Code will be available at https://github.com/AIGeeksGroup/SegTTA.

AI.tldr: SegTTA enhances medical image segmentation without model retraining by employing multiple augmentations and weighted voting, demonstrating improvements across diverse datasets.
AI.motivation: Addressing variability in medical image quality due to equipment and operators, and improving segmentation without retraining models.
AI.method: The SegTTA framework integrates Gamma correction, Contrast enhancement, Gaussian blur, and Gaussian noise augments with weighted voting applied across MedSAM2 checkpoints.
AI.result: Experiments show improved segmentation results, including increases in metrics like mIoU, aIoU, and reductions in HD95 across datasets such as uterine structures and hepatic vessel segmentation.
AI.conclusion: SegTTA effectively boosts segmentation performance and provides task-specific optimization capabilities, especially for clinical applications requiring precision and coverage balance.

title: Generalization Boundaries of Fine-Tuned Small Language Models for Graph Structural Inference
summary: Small language models fine-tuned for graph property estimation have demonstrated strong in-distribution performance, yet their generalization capabilities beyond training conditions remain poorly understood. In this work, we systematically investigate the boundaries of structural inference in fine-tuned small language models along two generalization axes - graph size and graph family distribution - and assess domain-learning capability on real-world graph benchmarks. Using a controlled experimental setup with three instruction-tuned models in the 3-4B parameter class and two graph serialization formats, we evaluate performance on graphs substantially larger than the training range and across held-out random graph families. Our results show that fine-tuned models maintain strong ordinal consistency across structurally distinct graph families and continue to rank graphs by structural properties on inputs substantially larger than those seen during training, with distinct architecture-specific degradation profiles. These findings delineate where fine-tuned small language models generalize reliably, providing empirical grounding for their use in graph-based reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper studies the generalization limits of small language models fine-tuned for graph property estimation. It highlights their ability to generalize in certain graph-related scenarios but also identifies where they falter.
AI.motivation: To understand how well fine-tuned small language models can generalize structural inference tasks beyond their training conditions, particularly for larger graphs and different graph distributions.
AI.method: The study uses three instruction-tuned models (3-4B parameters) and two graph serialization formats to systematically assess generalization capabilities. It focuses on testing graph size and family generalization axes and evaluates real-world benchmarks.
AI.result: The models show strong ordinal consistency across distinct graph families and perform well on larger graphs than seen during training. However, performance declines vary based on model architecture.
AI.conclusion: Small fine-tuned LMs generalize adequately for graph reasoning tasks in structured settings, but architectural nuances affect their reliability in generalizing beyond training conditions.

title: Decisive: Guiding User Decisions with Optimal Preference Elicitation from Unstructured Documents
summary: Decision-making is a cognitively intensive task that requires synthesizing relevant information from multiple unstructured sources, weighing competing factors, and incorporating subjective user preferences. Existing methods, including large language models and traditional decision-support systems, fall short: they often overwhelm users with information or fail to capture nuanced preferences accurately. We present Decisive, an interactive decision-making framework that combines document-grounded reasoning with Bayesian preference inference. Our approach grounds decisions in an objective option-scoring matrix extracted from source documents, while actively learning a user's latent preference vector through targeted elicitation. Users answer pairwise tradeoff questions adaptively selected to maximize information gain over the final decision. This process converges efficiently, minimizing user effort while ensuring recommendations remain transparent and personalized. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms both general-purpose LLMs and existing decision-making frameworks achieving up to 20% improvement in decision accuracy over strong baselines across domains.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Decisive, an interactive decision-making framework that enhances decision accuracy by combining document-based reasoning with Bayesian preference inference.
AI.motivation: Existing decision-making tools fail to synthesize relevant information effectively or accurately capture nuanced user preferences.
AI.method: The framework uses an objective scoring matrix from documents and employs adaptive questioning to infer user preferences through Bayesian methods.
AI.result: Experimental results show that Decisive outperforms current decision-making frameworks and general-purpose LLMs by improving decision accuracy by up to 20%.
AI.conclusion: Decisive ensures more transparent, personalized, and efficient decision-making, addressing limitations of traditional methods and LLMs.

title: Benchmarking Real-Time Question Answering via Executable Code Workflows
summary: Retrieving real-time information is a fundamental capability for search-integrated agents in real-world applications. However, existing benchmarks are predominantly static and therefore fail to capture the temporal dynamics of information and the continuously evolving nature of real-world knowledge. To address this limitation, we propose RT-QA, a dynamic evaluation framework that leverages executable code workflows to retrieve up-to-date answers at evaluation time. Specifically, we construct an agent-driven pipeline that autonomously generates code for web crawling and DOM-based answer extraction to produce real-time ground truth. To ensure robust evaluation over time, the pipeline further incorporates a self-repair mechanism to adapt to changes in web page structures. RT-QA spans 12 domains (e.g., Finance, Sports) with 320 Chinese questions categorized into three difficulty levels. Extensive evaluations of state-of-the-art models (e.g., GPT-5.2, GLM-4.7) reveal significant limitations in real-time adaptability: even the best models achieve only 46% accuracy. Our analysis highlights two primary failure modes: (1) Lazy Retrieval, where agents rely on search snippets instead of deeply scanning specific websites for information (20% of failures); and (2) Temporal Confusion, a cognitive error where agents retrieve a historical date (e.g., an event in 2024) and fail to re-anchor to the current time (2026) for subsequent reasoning. These findings suggest that future agents require not just better retrieval strategies, but robust temporal state management.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces RT-QA, a framework for real-time question answering that dynamically evaluates current information using code workflows for web crawling and DOM-based answer extraction.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks are static and fail to accommodate the dynamic nature of real-world knowledge, limiting the evaluation of search-integrated agents.
AI.method: RT-QA leverages an agent-driven pipeline that autonomously generates and executes code for live web crawling and DOM-based answer extraction, along with a self-repair mechanism for web page structural changes.
AI.result: Evaluations show state-of-the-art models perform poorly, with a best accuracy of just 46%, due to lazy retrieval (20% of failures) and temporal confusion errors.
AI.conclusion: Improved real-time retrieval and temporal state management are essential for the development of future search-integrated agents.

title: HSG: Hyperbolic Scene Graph
summary: Scene graph representations enable structured visual understanding by modeling objects and their relationships, and have been widely used for multiview and 3D scene reasoning. Existing methods such as MSG learn scene graph embeddings in Euclidean space using contrastive learning and attention based association. However, Euclidean geometry does not explicitly capture hierarchical entailment relationships between places and objects, limiting the structural consistency of learned representations. To address this, we propose Hyperbolic Scene Graph (HSG), which learns scene graph embeddings in hyperbolic space where hierarchical relationships are naturally encoded through geometric distance. Our results show that HSG improves hierarchical structure quality while maintaining strong retrieval performance. The largest gains are observed in graph level metrics: HSG achieves a PP IoU of 33.17 and the highest Graph IoU of 33.51, outperforming the best AoMSG variant (25.37) by 8.14, highlighting the effectiveness of hyperbolic representation learning for scene graph modeling. Code: https://github.com/AIGeeksGroup/HSG.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Hyperbolic Scene Graph (HSG) for learning scene graph embeddings in hyperbolic space, which enhances hierarchical structure while maintaining strong retrieval performance.
AI.motivation: Existing Euclidean-based methods struggle to capture hierarchical entailment relationships in scene graphs, limiting their structural consistency and representation quality.
AI.method: The authors propose HSG, which leverages hyperbolic geometry to naturally encode hierarchical relationships using geometric distance, providing a more structured embedding approach.
AI.result: HSG significantly improves hierarchical structure (Graph IoU of 33.51) and outperforms prior Euclidean-based methods like AoMSG by an 8.14-point margin.
AI.conclusion: Hyperbolic representation learning is highly effective for scene graph modeling, as demonstrated by improved hierarchical quality and performance metrics.

title: LoRaQ: Optimized Low Rank Approximation for 4-bit Quantization
summary: Post-training quantization (PTQ) is essential for deploying large diffusion transformers on resource-constrained hardware, but aggressive 4-bit quantization significantly degrades generative performance. Low-rank approximation methods have emerged as a promising solution by appending auxiliary linear branches to restore performance. However, current state-of-the-art approaches assume these branches must retain high precision (W16A16) and rely on heavy, data-dependent calibration for initialization. We challenge both limitations with LoRaQ (Low-Rank Approximated Quantization), a simple, data-free calibration approach that optimizes quantization error compensation. By overcoming the need for high-precision branches, LoRaQ enables the first fully sub-16 bit pipeline, allowing the low-rank branch itself to be quantized. We demonstrate that, at equal memory overhead, LoRaQ outperforms the state-of-the-art methods in their native implementations on Pixart-$Σ$ and SANA. We also analyze mixed-precision configurations, showing that setups such as W8A8, W6A6, and W4A8 for the low-rank branch, alongside a W4 main layer, yield superior results while maintaining a fully quantized architecture compatible with modern mixed-precision hardware.

AI.tldr: LoRaQ simplifies post-training quantization for large diffusion transformers, enabling fully sub-16 bit quantized architectures without data-intensive calibration, and achieves high performance on low-resource hardware.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of deploying large diffusion transformers on hardware with constrained resources while maintaining generative performance, particularly under low-bit quantization settings.
AI.method: The authors propose LoRaQ, a data-free calibration method that eliminates the requirement for high-precision branches. LoRaQ focuses on optimizing quantization error compensation and allows the low-rank branch to also be quantized, supporting fully sub-16 bit architectures.
AI.result: LoRaQ outperforms state-of-the-art methods on benchmarks like Pixart-$Σ$ and SANA at equal memory overhead, and achieves superior performance across various mixed-precision configurations.
AI.conclusion: LoRaQ enables efficient, fully quantized architectures suitable for modern hardware, effectively balancing memory efficiency and performance without reliance on high-precision components or data-dependent calibration.

title: TLoRA: Task-aware Low Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models
summary: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) has become a widely adopted parameter-efficient fine-tuning method for large language models, with its effectiveness largely influenced by the allocation of ranks and scaling factors, as well as initialization. Existing LoRA variants typically address only one of these factors, often at the cost of increased training complexity or reduced practical efficiency. In this work, we present Task-aware Low-Rank Adaptation (TLoRA), a unified framework that jointly optimizes initialization and resource allocation at the outset of training. TLoRA introduces a data-driven initialization strategy that aligns the LoRA $A$ matrix with task-relevant subspaces by performing singular value decomposition on the product of pre-trained weights and input activation covariance. After this, the $A$ matrix is frozen, and only the $B$ matrix is trained. Furthermore, TLoRA employs a sensitivity-based importance metric to adaptively allocate ranks and scaling factors across layers under a fixed parameter budget. We conduct extensive experiments that demonstrate TLoRA consistently performs excellently across various tasks, including natural language understanding, commonsense reasoning, math reasoning, code generation, and chat generation, while significantly reducing the number of trainable parameters.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Task-aware Low-Rank Adaptation (TLoRA), a parameter-efficient method optimizing rank allocation, scaling, and initialization to fine-tune large language models for diverse tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing LoRA methods focus on single optimization factors, causing inefficiency and complexity in training. The paper aims to create a unified solution for better fine-tuning adaptability.
AI.method: TLoRA applies a data-driven initialization via singular value decomposition for task-relevant alignment and adapts rank and scaling allocation using sensitivity-based metrics, freezing certain parameters.
AI.result: Experiments show TLoRA achieves strong performance on tasks like reasoning, code generation, and chat generation, all while reducing trainable parameters.
AI.conclusion: TLoRA offers a novel framework that enhances LoRA's efficiency and effectiveness, making it adaptable for various natural language processing tasks.

title: LiteSemRAG: Lightweight LLM-Free Semantic-Aware Graph Retrieval for Robust RAG
summary: Graph-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has shown great potential for improving multi-level reasoning and structured evidence aggregation. However, existing graph-based RAG frameworks heavily rely on exploiting large language models (LLMs) during indexing and querying, leading to high token consumption, computational cost and latency overhead. In this paper, we propose LiteSemRAG, a lightweight, fully LLM-free, semantic-aware graph retrieval framework. LiteSemRAG constructs a heterogeneous semantic graph by exploiting contextual token-level embeddings, explicitly separating surface lexical representations from context-dependent semantic meanings. To robustly model polysemy, we introduce a dynamic semantic node construction mechanism with chunk-level context aggregation and adaptive anomaly handling. At query stage, LiteSemRAG performs a two-step semantic-aware retrieval process that integrates co-occurrence graph weighting with an isolated semantic recovery mechanism, enabling balanced structural reasoning and semantic coverage. We evaluate LiteSemRAG on three benchmark datasets and experimental results show that LiteSemRAG achieves the best mean reciprocal rank (MRR@10) across all datasets and competitive or superior recall rate (Recall@10) compared to state-of-the-art LLM-based graph RAG systems. Meanwhile, LiteSemRAG consumes zero LLM tokens and achieves substantial efficiency improvements in both indexing and querying due to the elimination of LLM usage. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of LiteSemRAG, indicating that a strong semantic-aware graph retrieval framework can be achieved without relying on LLM-based approaches.

AI.tldr: LiteSemRAG is a novel graph-based retrieval framework focusing on lightweight and efficient retrieval without relying on large language models (LLMs).
AI.motivation: Existing graph-based RAG frameworks depend heavily on LLMs, leading to high costs, inefficiency, and latency issues, necessitating a more efficient alternative.
AI.method: The paper introduces LiteSemRAG, a semantic-aware graph retrieval system that constructs a heterogeneous semantic graph using contextual token-level embeddings, separates lexical and semantic meanings, handles polysemy with a dynamic node mechanism, and utilizes a two-step semantic-aware retrieval process.
AI.result: LiteSemRAG achieves the best mean reciprocal rank (MRR@10) and competitive recall rates on benchmark datasets while consuming zero LLM tokens and offering significant efficiency improvements.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that effective semantic-aware graph retrieval can be realized without LLMs, providing an efficient, lightweight alternative suitable for structured reasoning and evidence aggregation.

title: From Adaptation to Generalization: Adaptive Visual Prompting for Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Visual prompting has emerged as a powerful method for adapting pre-trained models to new domains without updating model parameters. However, existing prompting methods typically optimize a single prompt per domain and apply it uniformly to all inputs, limiting their ability to generalize under intra and inter-domain variability, which is especially critical in the medical field. To address this, we propose APEX, an Adaptive Prompt EXtraction framework that retrieves input-specific prompts from a learnable prompt memory. The memory stores diverse, domain-discriminative prompt representations and is queried via domain features extracted from the Fourier spectrum. To learn robust and discriminative domain features, we introduce a novel Low-Frequency Feature Contrastive (LFC) learning framework that clusters representations from the same domain while separating those from different domains. Extensive experiments on two medical segmentation tasks demonstrate that APEX significantly improves generalization across both seen and unseen domains. Furthermore, it complements any existing backbones and consistently enhances performance, confirming its effectiveness as a plug-and-play prompting solution in medical fields. The code is available at https://github.com/cetinkayaevren/apex/

AI.tldr: The paper introduces APEX, a novel adaptive visual prompting framework that generalizes across domains using learnable prompt memory and low-frequency feature contrastive learning, applied to medical segmentation.
AI.motivation: Traditional visual prompting is limited in generalizing across domains, especially in the medical field, where intra and inter-domain variability is critical.
AI.method: The proposed APEX framework adapts input-specific visual prompts using a learnable memory and a novel Low-Frequency Feature Contrastive (LFC) learning framework to retrieve and learn domain-discriminative prompts from Fourier spectrum features.
AI.result: Empirical results on medical segmentation tasks show that APEX significantly improves generalization across both seen and unseen domains and enhances performance when paired with any existing backbones.
AI.conclusion: APEX is an effective and plug-and-play solution for generalizing in medical fields, addressing the limitations of traditional visual prompting techniques.

title: An `Inverse' Experimental Framework to Estimate Market Efficiency
summary: Digital marketplaces processing billions of dollars annually represent critical infrastructure in sociotechnical ecosystems, yet their performance optimization lacks principled measurement frameworks that can inform algorithmic governance decisions regarding market efficiency and fairness from complex market data. By looking at orderbook data from double auction markets alone, because bids and asks do not represent true maximum willingnesses to buy and true minimum willingnesses to sell, there is little an economist can say about the market's actual performance in terms of allocative efficiency. We turn to experimental data to address this issue, `inverting' the standard induced value approach of double auction experiments. Our aim is to predict key market features relevant to market efficiency, particularly allocative efficiency, using orderbook data only -- specifically bids, asks and price realizations, but not the induced reservation values -- as early as possible. Since there is no established model of strategically optimal behavior in these markets, and because orderbook data is highly unstructured, non-stationary and non-linear, we propose quantile-based normalization techniques that help us build general predictive models. We develop and train several models, including linear regressions and gradient boosting trees, leveraging quantile-based input from the underlying supply-demand model. Our models can predict allocative efficiency with reasonable accuracy from the earliest bids and asks, and these predictions improve with additional realized price data. The performance of the prediction techniques varies by target and market type. Our framework holds significant potential for application to real-world market data, offering valuable insights into market efficiency and performance, even prior to any trade realizations.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a framework to predict market efficiency, particularly allocative efficiency, in digital double auction marketplaces using orderbook data. The authors propose models based on quantile normalization to tackle unstructured and complex market data.
AI.motivation: Current algorithms governing digital marketplaces lack measurement frameworks to understand and improve market efficiency and fairness. The performance of such markets is difficult to assess using existing orderbook data due to strategic behavior and lack of true reservation values.
AI.method: The authors invert the conventional induced value approach of double auction experiments. They develop predictive models, including linear regressions and gradient boosting trees, using quantile-normalized features from orderbook data to estimate allocative efficiency.
AI.result: The models predict allocative efficiency accurately, even with initial orderbook data, and demonstrate improved performance with the addition of price realizations. The effectiveness depends on specific market types and features.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides actionable insights into market efficiency and performance using only orderbook data. It has the potential to enhance the algorithmic governance of digital marketplaces, even before trades occur.

title: Depth Registers Unlock W4A4 on SwiGLU: A Reader/Generator Decomposition
summary: We study post-training W4A4 quantization in a controlled 300M-parameter SwiGLU decoder-only language model trained on 5B tokens of FineWeb-Edu, and ask which input-activation sites dominate the error. Naive round-to-nearest W4A4 collapses validation perplexity from FP16 23.6 to 1727. A simple residual-axis training-time intervention -- Depth Registers with a register-magnitude hinge loss (DR+sink) -- reduces this to 119 (about 14x) at matched FP16 PPL and matched zero-shot capacity, and composes with SmoothQuant to 39.9 PPL. The residual ~2 PPL gap to FP16 is the diagnostic core. We decompose W4A4 damage by input-activation site: the five trainable linears in a SwiGLU block split into residual-axis readers (qkv, w1, w3) and block-internal generators (o_proj, w2). Elementary norm arguments show residual-axis magnitude control bounds readers tightly but leaves w2's bilinear input bounded only by the trivial product of factor bounds; empirically, DR+sink collapses reader kurtosis while leaving generators essentially unchanged, and the reader-rescued W4A4 residue is flat at ~0.28 nats across three matched checkpoints with Delta-remove(w2) dominating. We present DR+sink as a training-time probe rather than a deployment proposal: a post-hoc alternative (Per-Linear QuaRot) nearly matches it on the reader axis. Full QuaRot -- adding online per-head value Hadamard plus online w2-input rotation -- does not close the gap either, directly testing the prediction that orthogonal rotation cannot bound the bilinear SwiGLU tail. Claims are specific to our 300M, 5B-token, single-seed setting, and our experiments do not isolate the partition from the hinge.

AI.tldr: This paper explores why post-training W4A4 quantization causes errors in decoder-only language models and evaluates interventions to mitigate these errors.
AI.motivation: To investigate the specific input-activation sites that dominate errors in post-training W4A4 quantization and propose methods to improve quantization performance, particularly for a 300M-parameter SwiGLU model.
AI.method: Examines the role of residual-axis readers and block-internal generators. Introduces the 'Depth Registers with a register-magnitude hinge loss (DR+sink)' intervention and evaluates its impact on quantization errors.
AI.result: Naive W4A4 quantization collapses validation perplexity severely, but DR+sink drastically reduces errors (from 1727 to 119 PPL), further composable with SmoothQuant to achieve 39.9 PPL. The main diagnostic gap (2 PPL) remains unexplained.
AI.conclusion: DR+sink effectively mitigates quantization issues for residual readers but does not fully address errors, particularly from block-internal generators. Orthogonal rotation alone cannot resolve issues tied to SwiGLU bilinear structures.

title: Training for Compositional Sensitivity Reduces Dense Retrieval Generalization
summary: Dense retrieval compresses texts into single embeddings ranked by cosine similarity. While efficient for recall, this interface is brittle for identity-level matching: minimal compositional edits (negation, role swaps) flip meaning yet retain high similarity. Motivated by geometric results for unit-sphere cosine spaces (Kang et al., 2025), we test this retrieval-composition tension in text-only retrieval. Across four dual-encoder backbones, adding structure-targeted negatives consistently reduces zero-shot NanoBEIR retrieval (8-9% mean nDCG@10 drop on small backbones; up to 40% on medium ones), while only partially improving pooled-space separation. Treating pooled cosine as a recall interface, we then benchmark verifiers scoring token--token cosine maps. MaxSim (late interaction) excels at reranking but fails to reject structural near-misses, whereas a small Transformer over similarity maps reliably separates near-misses under end-to-end training.

AI.tldr: Dense retrieval systems based on cosine similarity are efficient but struggle with identity-level changes due to compositional edits affecting meaning without altering embedding similarity.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address retrieval systems' limitations in handling compositional edits, driven by geometric properties of cosine spaces, as established in prior work (Kang et al., 2025).
AI.method: The authors test retrieval systems across four dual-encoder backbones, introduce structure-targeted negatives to the training process, and benchmark verifiers using token-token cosine maps.
AI.result: Structure-targeted negatives reduce mean nDCG@10 retrieval performance (up to 40%) while only partially improving pooled-space separation. A small Transformer over similarity maps outperforms MaxSim in rejecting structural near-misses under end-to-end training.
AI.conclusion: Enhancing retrieval systems requires moving beyond pooled cosine similarity by integrating verifiers capable of handling structural compositional edits reliably.

title: UniMesh: Unifying 3D Mesh Understanding and Generation
summary: Recent advances in 3D vision have led to specialized models for either 3D understanding (e.g., shape classification, segmentation, reconstruction) or 3D generation (e.g., synthesis, completion, and editing). However, these tasks are often tackled in isolation, resulting in fragmented architectures and representations that hinder knowledge transfer and holistic scene modeling. To address these challenges, we propose UniMesh, a unified framework that jointly learns 3D generation and understanding within a single architecture. First, we introduce a novel Mesh Head that acts as a cross model interface, bridging diffusion based image generation with implicit shape decoders. Second, we develop Chain of Mesh (CoM), a geometric instantiation of iterative reasoning that enables user driven semantic mesh editing through a closed loop latent, prompting, and re generation cycle. Third, we incorporate a self reflection mechanism based on an Actor Evaluator Self reflection triad to diagnose and correct failures in high level tasks like 3D captioning. Experimental results demonstrate that UniMesh not only achieves competitive performance on standard benchmarks but also unlocks novel capabilities in iterative editing and mutual enhancement between generation and understanding. Code: https://github.com/AIGeeksGroup/UniMesh. Website: https://aigeeksgroup.github.io/UniMesh.

AI.tldr: UniMesh integrates 3D generation and understanding in a unified architecture, overcoming the fragmented nature of existing models and enhancing capabilities like iterative editing and mutual task improvement.
AI.motivation: Current 3D vision models are specialized in either understanding or generation but lack a unified approach, limiting knowledge transfer and comprehensive scene modeling.
AI.method: The paper presents UniMesh, which utilizes a novel Mesh Head for bridging tasks, Chain of Mesh for iterative semantic editing, and a self-reflection mechanism to improve 3D captioning.
AI.result: UniMesh demonstrates competitive performance on benchmarks and introduces innovative abilities in iterative editing and cross-task enhancement.
AI.conclusion: By unifying 3D generation and understanding, UniMesh fosters a more holistic approach to 3D vision tasks and sets a foundation for versatile applications.

title: FreezeEmpath: Efficient Training for Empathetic Spoken Chatbots with Frozen LLMs
summary: Empathy is essential for fostering natural interactions in spoken dialogue systems, as it enables machines to recognize the emotional tone of human speech and deliver empathetic responses. Recent research has made significant progress in developing empathetic spoken chatbots based on large language models (LLMs). However, several challenges still exist when training such models, including reliance on costly empathetic speech instruction data and a lack of emotional expressiveness in the generated speech. Finetuning LLM with cross-modal empathetic instruction data may also lead to catastrophic forgetting and a degradation of its general capability. To address these challenges, we propose FreezeEmpath, an end-to-end empathetic spoken chatbot trained in a simple and efficient manner. The entire training process relies solely on existing speech instruction data and speech emotion recognition (SER) data, while keeping the LLM's parameters frozen. Experiments demonstrate that FreezeEmpath is able to generate emotionally expressive speech and outperforms other empathetic models in empathetic dialogue, SER, and SpokenQA tasks, demonstrating the effectiveness of our training strategy.

AI.tldr: FreezeEmpath is an empathetic spoken chatbot trained efficiently, overcoming data and emotional expressiveness challenges by keeping the LLM parameters frozen.
AI.motivation: To address training challenges in empathetic spoken AI, such as costly data requirements, lack of emotional expressiveness, and performance degradation when finetuning LLMs.
AI.method: FreezeEmpath trains an empathetic chatbot by relying only on existing speech instruction and emotion data, without updating LLM parameters, ensuring simplicity and efficiency.
AI.result: The model generates emotionally expressive speech and outperforms existing models in empathetic dialogue, emotion recognition, and spoken QA tasks.
AI.conclusion: FreezeEmpath is a successful approach to improving empathetic spoken systems while mitigating training complexity and maintaining overall system capabilities.

title: AgriIR: A Scalable Framework for Domain-Specific Knowledge Retrieval
summary: This paper introduces AgriIR, a configurable retrieval augmented generation (RAG) framework designed to deliver grounded, domain-specific answers while maintaining flexibility and low computational cost. Instead of relying on large, monolithic models, AgriIR decomposes the information access process into declarative modular stages -- query refinement, sub-query planning, retrieval, synthesis, and evaluation. This design allows practitioners to adapt the framework to new knowledge verticals without modifying the architecture. Our reference implementation targets Indian agricultural information access, integrating 1B-parameter language models with adaptive retrievers and domain-aware agent catalogues. The system enforces deterministic citation, integrates telemetry for transparency, and includes automated deployment assets to ensure auditable, reproducible operation. By emphasizing architectural design and modular control, AgriIR demonstrates that well-engineered pipelines can achieve domain-accurate, trustworthy retrieval even under constrained resources. We argue that this approach exemplifies ``AI for Agriculture'' by promoting accessibility, sustainability, and accountability in retrieval-augmented generation systems.

AI.tldr: AgriIR, a modular RAG framework, delivers domain-specific answers adaptively, targeting Indian agricultural information with transparency and efficiency.
AI.motivation: To provide trustworthy and adaptable retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) for Indian agricultural information access with low computational cost.
AI.method: Decomposes information access into modular stages—query refinement, sub-query planning, retrieval, synthesis, evaluation—integrating 1B-parameter language models, adaptive retrievers, and agent catalogues.
AI.result: AgriIR achieves domain accuracy and trustworthiness even under resource constraints, ensuring adaptability, transparency, and reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: AgriIR exemplifies sustainable AI for agriculture by emphasizing accessibility and modular control, highlighting its potential in specialized, resource-limited domains.

title: Dual-Anchoring: Addressing State Drift in Vision-Language Navigation
summary: Vision-Language Navigation(VLN) requires an agent to navigate through 3D environments by following natural language instructions. While recent Video Large Language Models(Video-LLMs) have largely advanced VLN, they remain highly susceptible to State Drift in long scenarios. In these cases, the agent's internal state drifts away from the true task execution state, leading to aimless wandering and failure to execute essential maneuvers in the instruction. We attribute this failure to two distinct cognitive deficits: Progress Drift, where the agent fails to distinguish completed sub-goals from remaining ones, and Memory Drift, where the agent's history representations degrade, making it lose track of visited landmarks. In this paper, we propose a Dual-Anchoring Framework that explicitly anchors the instruction progress and history representations. First, to address progress drift, we introduce Instruction Progress Anchoring, which supervises the agent to generate structured text tokens that delineate completed versus remaining sub-goals. Second, to mitigate memory drift, we propose Memory Landmark Anchoring, which utilizes a Landmark-Centric World Model to retrospectively predict object-centric embeddings extracted by the Segment Anything Model, compelling the agent to explicitly verify past observations and preserve distinct representations of visited landmarks. Facilitating this framework, we curate two extensive datasets: 3.6 million samples with explicit progress descriptions, and 937k grounded landmark data for retrospective verification. Extensive experiments in both simulation and real-world environments demonstrate the superiority of our method, achieving a 15.2% improvement in Success Rate and a remarkable 24.7% gain on long-horizon trajectories. To facilitate further research, we will release our code, data generation pipelines, and the collected datasets.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the challenges of State Drift in Vision-Language Navigation (VLN) and proposes a Dual-Anchoring Framework to mitigate Progress Drift and Memory Drift, achieving significant improvements in navigation success.
AI.motivation: Existing Video-Large Language Models for Vision-Language Navigation struggle with maintaining accurate state representation during long navigation scenarios, leading to task execution failures.
AI.method: The paper introduces a Dual-Anchoring Framework with two components: Instruction Progress Anchoring generates tokens to mark completed versus pending sub-goals, and Memory Landmark Anchoring uses a Landmark-Centric World Model to preserve representations of visited landmarks.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrated a 15.2% improvement in Success Rate and 24.7% gain on long-horizon trajectories for the proposed framework.
AI.conclusion: The Dual-Anchoring Framework significantly enhances VLN performance in both simulated and real-world environments, offering promising avenues for overcoming State Drift challenges.

title: Scalable Neighborhood-Based Multi-Agent Actor-Critic
summary: We propose MADDPG-K, a scalable extension to Multi-Agent Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (MADDPG) that addresses the computational limitations of centralized critic approaches. Centralized critics, which condition on the observations and actions of all agents, have demonstrated significant performance gains in cooperative and competitive multi-agent settings. However, their critic networks grow linearly in input size with the number of agents, making them increasingly expensive to train at scale. MADDPG-K mitigates this by restricting each agent's critic to the $k$ closest agents under a chosen metric which in our case is Euclidean distance. This ensures a constant-size critic input regardless of the total agent count. We analyze the complexity of this approach, showing that the quadratic cost it retains arises from cheap scalar distance computations rather than the expensive neural network matrix multiplications that bottleneck standard MADDPG. We validate our method empirically across cooperative and adversarial environments from the Multi-Particle Environment suite, demonstrating competitive or superior performance compared to MADDPG, faster convergence in cooperative settings, and better runtime scaling as the number of agents grows. Our code is available at https://github.com/TimGop/MADDPG-K .

AI.tldr: This paper introduces MADDPG-K, a scalable adaptation of MADDPG that uses smaller critics conditioned on nearby agents for improved training efficiency and performance.
AI.motivation: To address the computational inefficiency of centralized critic methods in multi-agent settings, particularly as the number of agents scales up.
AI.method: The authors limit each agent's critic to the k closest agents based on Euclidean distance, ensuring fixed input size. This reduces the cost by leveraging cheap scalar distance computations instead of expensive neural network operations.
AI.result: MADDPG-K demonstrates competitive or superior performance across cooperative and adversarial environments, with faster convergence in cooperative tasks and improved scalability as the number of agents increases.
AI.conclusion: MADDPG-K achieves efficient and scalable reinforcement learning for multi-agent environments by using limited inputs for critics without sacrificing performance.

title: MM-JudgeBias: A Benchmark for Evaluating Compositional Biases in MLLM-as-a-Judge
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have been increasingly used as automatic evaluators-a paradigm known as MLLM-as-a-Judge. However, their reliability and vulnerabilities to biases remain underexplored. We find that many MLLM judges fail to reliably integrate key visual or textual cues, yielding unreliable evaluations when evidence is missing or mismatched, and exhibiting instability under semantically irrelevant perturbations. To address this, we systematically define Compositional Bias in MLLM-as-a-Judge systems and introduce MM-JudgeBias, a benchmark for evaluating it. MM-JudgeBias introduces controlled perturbations across Query, Image, and Response, and evaluates model behavior via two complementary metrics: Bias-Deviation (BD) for sensitivity and Bias-Conformity (BC) for stability. Our dataset of over 1,800 curated and refined multimodal samples, drawn from 29 source benchmarks, enables a fine-grained diagnosis of nine bias types across diverse tasks and domains. Experiments on 26 state-of-the-art MLLMs reveal systematic modality neglect and asymmetric evaluation tendencies, underscoring the need for more reliable judges.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the reliability and biases of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) used as evaluators and introduces a benchmark (MM-JudgeBias) for assessing compositional bias in these systems.
AI.motivation: To address the unexplored reliability issues and vulnerability to biases in MLLMs when utilized as evaluators, particularly in their ability to effectively integrate multimodal inputs.
AI.method: The authors define Compositional Bias in these systems and propose MM-JudgeBias, a benchmark with controlled perturbations across queries, images, and responses. They evaluate model behaviors using metrics for sensitivity (Bias-Deviation) and stability (Bias-Conformity).
AI.result: Using a dataset of over 1,800 curated multimodal samples, experiments with 26 state-of-the-art MLLMs show systematic inadequacies, including modality neglect and asymmetric evaluation tendencies.
AI.conclusion: MLLMs as evaluators have significant limitations and compositional biases, emphasizing the need for improved model designs that are more reliable and less prone to bias.

title: Mapping Recent Shifts in Digital Art via Conference Discourse: AI, XR, the Metaverse, and Blockchain/NFTs (2021-2025)
comment: 16 pages, 3 figures, 3 tables, Submitted to DCAC
summary: This paper presents an analysis of five years (2021 - 2025) of conference discourse across six digital art conferences, aiming to trace thematic shifts associated with the rapid development of emerging technologies, namely artificial intelligence (AI), immersive technologies (including XR and the metaverse), and blockchain technologies and non-fungible tokens (NFTs). The results indicate a marked increase in AI-related contributions, while immersive technologies maintain a relatively stable share of the discourse, and blockchain- and NFT-based works remain marginal. Overall, whereas immersive technologies and blockchain-related topics exhibit relative stability, AI shows a significant rise after 2022, emerging as a dominant theme within digital art conference discourse.

AI.tldr: Analysis of six digital art conferences from 2021 to 2025 reveals a significant rise in AI-related discussions, while discourse on immersive technologies remains stable, and blockchain/NFT topics remain marginal.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to understand how thematic focuses at digital art conferences have shifted in response to developments in emerging technologies like AI, immersive tech, and blockchain/NFTs.
AI.method: The authors analyzed five years of conference discourse across six digital art conferences, focusing on thematic changes concerning AI, immersive technologies, and blockchain/NFTs.
AI.result: AI-related topics saw a sharp increase after 2022, becoming the dominant theme, while immersive technologies showed stable contributions and blockchain/NFT-based works remained marginal.
AI.conclusion: Digital art conferences are increasingly dominated by AI discussions, reflecting broader technological trends, while immersive and blockchain-related topics maintain stable or minor roles.

title: Unveiling Deepfakes: A Frequency-Aware Triple Branch Network for Deepfake Detection
summary: Advanced deepfake technologies are blurring the lines between real and fake, presenting both revolutionary opportunities and alarming threats. While it unlocks novel applications in fields like entertainment and education, its malicious use has sparked urgent ethical and societal concerns ranging from identity theft to the dissemination of misinformation. To tackle these challenges, feature analysis using frequency features has emergedas a promising direction for deepfake detection. However, oneaspect that has been overlooked so far is that existing methodstend to concentrate on one or a few specific frequency domains,which risks overfitting to particular artifacts and significantlyundermines their robustness when facing diverse forgery patterns. Another underexplored aspect we observe is that different features often attend to the same forged region, resulting in redundant feature representations and limiting the diversity of the extracted clues. This may undermine the ability of a model to capture complementary information across different facets, thereby compromising its generalization capability to diverse manipulations. In this paper, we seek to tackle these challenges from two aspects: (1) we propose a triple-branch network that jointly captures spatial and frequency features by learning from both original image and image reconstructed by different frequency channels, and (2) we mathematically derive feature decoupling and fusion losses grounded in the mutual information theory, which enhances the model to focus on task-relevant features across the original image and the image reconstructed by different frequency channels. Extensive experiments on six large-scale benchmark datasets demonstrate that our method consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance. Our code is released at https://github.com/injooker/Unveiling Deepfake.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the limitations of existing deepfake detection methods by introducing a triple-branch network and feature decoupling/fusion losses to improve robustness and generalization.
AI.motivation: Deepfake technologies pose ethical and societal concerns, including identity theft and misinformation. Existing detection methods focus on limited frequency domains and create redundant feature representations, reducing robustness and generalization.
AI.method: The paper introduces a triple-branch network to capture spatial and frequency features from original and frequency-reconstructed images. It also employs mathematically derived feature decoupling and fusion losses based on mutual information theory.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance across six large-scale deepfake benchmark datasets.
AI.conclusion: Innovative strategies like the triple-branch network and advanced loss functions significantly enhance the robustness and generalization of deepfake detection methods to tackle diverse forgery scenarios.

title: Attraction, Repulsion, and Friction: Introducing DMF, a Friction-Augmented Drifting Model
summary: Drifting Models [Deng et al., 2026] train a one-step generator by evolving samples under a kernel-based drift field, avoiding ODE integration at inference. The original analysis leaves two questions open. The drift-field iteration admits a locally repulsive regime in a two-particle surrogate, and vanishing of the drift ($V_{p,q}\equiv 0$) is not known to force the learned distribution $q$ to match the target $p$. We derive a contraction threshold for the surrogate and show that a linearly-scheduled friction coefficient gives a finite-horizon bound on the error trajectory. Under a Gaussian kernel we prove that the drift-field equilibrium is identifiable: vanishing of $V_{p,q}$ on any open set forces $q=p$, closing the converse of Proposition 3.1 of Deng et al. Our friction-augmented model, DMF (Drifting Model with Friction), matches or exceeds Optimal Flow Matching on FFHQ adult-to-child domain translation at 16x lower training compute.

AI.tldr: The paper revisits the Drifting Models approach and proposes a friction-augmented version (DMF) to address theoretical gaps, improving performance and reducing computational costs.
AI.motivation: To address unresolved questions about repulsive regimes in drift-field iterations and the problem of whether vanishing drift guarantees matching target and learned distributions.
AI.method: A contraction threshold for trajectories is derived, and a friction coefficient schedule is proposed. Identifiability of drift-field equilibrium under a Gaussian kernel is also proven.
AI.result: The derived theoretical results allow the introduction of the DMF model, which matches or surpasses Optimal Flow Matching in terms of translation performance at significantly lower computational demands.
AI.conclusion: Addressing the theoretical gaps strengthens the foundation for Drifting Models and showcases DMF's computational and performance advantages.

title: Beyond Reproduction: A Paired-Task Framework for Assessing LLM Comprehension and Creativity in Literary Translation
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for creative tasks such as literary translation. Yet translational creativity remains underexplored and is rarely evaluated at scale, while source-text comprehension is typically studied in isolation, despite the fact that, in professional translation, comprehension and creativity are tightly intertwined. We address these gaps with a paired-task framework applied to literary excerpts from 11 books. Task 1 assesses source-text comprehension, and Task 2 evaluates translational creativity through Units of Creative Potential (UCPs), such as metaphors and wordplay. Using a scalable evaluation setup that combines expert human annotations with UCP-based automatic scoring, we benchmark 23 models and four creativity-oriented prompts. Our findings show that strong comprehension does not translate into human-level creativity: models often produce literal or contextually inappropriate renderings, with particularly large gaps for the more distant English-Chinese language pair. Creativity-oriented prompts yield only modest gains, and only one model, Mistral-Large, comes close to human-level creativity (0.167 vs. 0.246). Across all model-prompt combinations, only three exceed a creativity score of 0.1, while the rest remain at or near zero.

AI.tldr: This paper benchmarks the translational creativity and comprehension of 23 large language models (LLMs) using a paired-task framework on literary excerpts, finding that strong comprehension does not equate to high creativity in translation.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to explore and evaluate the often-overlooked aspect of translational creativity in LLMs, while addressing the combined interplay of comprehension and creativity, crucial in professional translation but rarely studied jointly.
AI.method: A paired-task framework is employed: Task 1 measures source-text comprehension, and Task 2 evaluates creativity via Units of Creative Potential (UCPs). Models are assessed using a mix of expert human annotations and an automatic scoring system.
AI.result: Findings reveal that most LLMs struggle with translational creativity, frequently generating literal or inappropriate translations, especially for English-Chinese pairs. Creativity-oriented prompts provide limited improvement, and only one model, Mistral-Large, approaches human-level creativity.
AI.conclusion: Current models excel in comprehension but fall short in achieving human-level translational creativity, indicating a need for improved strategies to enhance their performance in creative translation tasks.

title: AutoVQA-G: Self-Improving Agentic Framework for Automated Visual Question Answering and Grounding Annotation
comment: Accepted at IEEE ICASSP 2026. 5 pages, 5 figures. Code available at https://github.com/rohnson1999/AutoVQA-G
summary: Manual annotation of high-quality visual question answering with grounding (VQA-G) datasets, which pair visual questions with evidential grounding, is crucial for advancing vision-language models (VLMs), but remains unscalable. Existing automated methods are often hindered by two key issues: (1) inconsistent data fidelity due to model hallucinations; (2) brittle verification mechanisms based on simple heuristics. To address these limitations, we introduce AutoVQA-G, a self-improving agentic framework for automated VQA-G annotation. AutoVQA-G employs an iterative refinement loop where a Consistency Evaluation module uses Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning for fine-grained visual verification. Based on this feedback, a memory-augmented Prompt Optimization agent analyzes critiques from failed samples to progressively refine generation prompts. Our experiments show that AutoVQA-G generates VQA-G datasets with superior visual grounding accuracy compared to leading multimodal LLMs, offering a promising approach for creating high-fidelity data to facilitate more robust VLM training and evaluation. Code: https://github.com/rohnson1999/AutoVQA-G

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AutoVQA-G, an automated framework to generate reliable VQA-G datasets, addressing fidelity and verification issues in existing methods.
AI.motivation: Manual creation of VQA-G datasets is scalable, and automated methods often grapple with hallucinations and verification challenges.
AI.method: AutoVQA-G leverages an iterative refinement process with Chain-of-Thought reasoning for visual verification and a memory-augmented Prompt Optimization agent for prompt refinement.
AI.result: AutoVQA-G outperforms leading multimodal LLMs in generating accurate visual grounding for VQA-G datasets.
AI.conclusion: AutoVQA-G presents an effective pathway to high-fidelity datasets, advancing VLM training and evaluation.

title: FSEVAL: Feature Selection Evaluation Toolbox and Dashboard
summary: Feature selection is a fundamental machine learning and data mining task, involved with discriminating redundant features from informative ones. It is an attempt to address the curse of dimensionality by removing the redundant features, while unlike dimensionality reduction methods, preserving explainability. Feature selection is conducted in both supervised and unsupervised settings, with different evaluation metrics employed to determine which feature selection algorithm is the best. In this paper, we propose FSEVAL, a feature selection evaluation toolbox accompanied with a visualization dashboard, with the goal to make it easy to comprehensively evaluate feature selection algorithms. FSEVAL aims to provide a standardized, unified, evaluation and visualization toolbox to help the researchers working in the field, conduct extensive and comprehensive evaluation of feature selection algorithms with ease.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FSEVAL, a toolbox for evaluating feature selection algorithms with visualization support.
AI.motivation: Feature selection is vital in addressing the curse of dimensionality while maintaining explainability. There is a lack of standardized tools for comprehensive evaluation of feature selection methods.
AI.method: The authors developed FSEVAL, a toolbox offering a unified platform for evaluating feature selection techniques, accompanied by a visualization dashboard.
AI.result: FSEVAL makes it easy for researchers to extensively evaluate feature selection algorithms, providing standardized and unified evaluation metrics.
AI.conclusion: The proposed FSEVAL toolbox simplifies and improves the process of evaluating feature selection methods, aiding researchers with its comprehensive and unified approach.

title: Copy-as-Decode: Grammar-Constrained Parallel Prefill for LLM Editing
comment: 31 pages, 8 figures, 25 tables (17-page main body plus appendix)
summary: LLMs edit text and code by autoregressively regenerating the full output, even when most tokens appear verbatim in the input. We study Copy-as-Decode, a decoding-layer mechanism that recasts edit generation as structured decoding over a two-primitive grammar: <copy lines="i-j"/> references an input line range, <gen>...</gen> emits new content. A token-level FSM guarantees syntactic validity, and a serving-layer primitive updates the KV cache for each copy span via a single parallel-prefill forward rather than $N$ autoregressive steps -- sharing the parallel-forward kernel of speculative decoding but with input tokens as the draft and program-enforced acceptance replacing probabilistic verification. We report an upper-bound analysis that requires no end-to-end training. (i) Kernel speedup: on Qwen2.5-{1.5B, 7B}, copying $N$ tokens via parallel prefill is $6.8\times$--$303\times$ faster than autoregressive ($N \in [8, 512]$, A100 80GB bf16). (ii) Copy ceiling: on ProbeEdit and HumanEvalPack-Fix (Py/JS), $74$--$98\%$ of gold tokens are reachable under the line-level primitive; composed with the empirical kernel over each corpus's span histogram this yields a closed-form wall-clock bound of $29.0\times / 3.4\times / 4.2\times$ ($13.0\times$ pooled). A token-level extension reaches $91$--$99\%$ coverage with $4.5\times$--$6.5\times$ floors. (iii) Pipeline losslessness: oracle programs round-trip through the deterministic resolver on all $482$ cases, localizing any downstream failure to span selection rather than the mechanism. A perturbation study shows pooled EM drops from $100\%$ to $15.48\%$ under off-by-one noise. A fine-tuning pilot on Qwen2.5-Coder-1.5B lifts HEvalFix-Py EM from $0/33$ (untrained) to $12$--$17\%$, a learnability signal, not a production selector. Batched-serving integration and multi-file coverage are scoped as follow-up.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Copy-as-Decode, a decoding mechanism for LLMs that employs structured grammar elements for generating edits more efficiently than autoregressive methods.
AI.motivation: Current text and code editing by LLMs is computationally expensive as they regenerate the full output auto-regressively, even when minimal changes are needed.
AI.method: The authors design a mechanism using structured decoding primitives (<copy> and <gen>) enforced by a finite state machine, reducing autoregressive overhead and leveraging parallel-forward kernels for faster token processing.
AI.result: Experiments show significant kernel speedup (up to 303x), high coverage in edit tasks (91-99% in extended cases), and pipeline losslessness with no mechanism-induced errors. Editing gains are demonstrated on various benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Copy-as-Decode has strong potential for efficiency improvements in LLM-based editing tasks and shows learnability when fine-tuned. Future work aims for broader real-world applicability in multi-file and batched serving contexts.

title: CSF: Black-box Fingerprinting via Compositional Semantics for Text-to-Image Models
summary: Text-to-image models are commercially valuable assets often distributed under restrictive licenses, but such licenses are enforceable only when violations can be detected. Existing methods require pre-deployment watermarking or internal model access, which are unavailable in commercial API deployments. We present Compositional Semantic Fingerprinting (CSF), the first black-box method for attributing fine-tuned text-to-image models to protected lineages using only query access. CSF treats models as semantic category generators and probes them with compositional underspecified prompts that remain rare under fine-tuning. This gives IP owners an asymmetric advantage: new prompt compositions can be generated after deployment, while attackers must anticipate and suppress a much broader space of fingerprints. Across 6 model families (FLUX, Kandinsky, SD1.5/2.1/3.0/XL) and 13 fine-tuned variants, our Bayesian attribution framework enables controlled-risk lineage decisions, with all variants satisfying the dominance criterion.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Compositional Semantic Fingerprinting (CSF), a black-box technique to attribute fine-tuned text-to-image models using only query access.
AI.motivation: Text-to-image models are commercially valuable but need effective methods to detect violations when licenses are restricted and internal model access is unavailable.
AI.method: CSF uses compositional under-specified prompts as semantic fingerprints, probing models in a black-box manner to detect fine-tuning adaptations without requiring pre-deployment watermarking or internal access.
AI.result: CSF shows effectiveness across 6 model families and 13 fine-tuned variants, providing controlled-risk lineage attribution and satisfying the dominance criterion.
AI.conclusion: CSF provides IP owners with a powerful post-deployment tool to enforce licensing, leveraging asymmetries in prompt generation to ensure robust attribution.

title: Coevolving Representations in Joint Image-Feature Diffusion
summary: Joint image-feature generative modeling has recently emerged as an effective strategy for improving diffusion training by coupling low-level VAE latents with high-level semantic features extracted from pre-trained visual encoders. However, existing approaches rely on a fixed representation space, constructed independently of the generative objective and kept unchanged during training. We argue that the representation space guiding diffusion should itself adapt to the generative task. To this end, we propose Coevolving Representation Diffusion (CoReDi), a framework in which the semantic representation space evolves during training by learning a lightweight linear projection jointly with the diffusion model. While naively optimizing this projection leads to degenerate solutions, we show that stable coevolution can be achieved through a combination of stop-gradient targets, normalization, and targeted regularization that prevents feature collapse. This formulation enables the semantic space to progressively specialize to the needs of image synthesis, improving its complementarity with image latents. We apply CoReDi to both VAE latent diffusion and pixel-space diffusion, demonstrating that adaptive semantic representations improve generative modeling across both settings. Experiments show that CoReDi achieves faster convergence and higher sample quality compared to joint diffusion models operating in fixed representation spaces.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CoReDi, a framework where semantic representation space adapts during diffusion model training, improving image synthesis and enabling faster convergence and better output quality.
AI.motivation: Current methods in joint image-feature generative modeling use fixed representation spaces, which do not adapt to generative objectives and limit performance.
AI.method: A coevolving framework (CoReDi) trains a lightweight linear projection jointly with the diffusion model, using techniques like stop-gradient targets, normalization, and regularization to ensure stability and prevent feature collapse.
AI.result: CoReDi outperforms fixed representation space approaches, achieving faster convergence and higher quality generative results across VAE latent diffusion and pixel-space diffusion.
AI.conclusion: Adapting the semantic representation space during diffusion training enhances complementarity with image latents and improves generative modeling performance.

title: Semantic-based Distributed Learning for Diverse and Discriminative Representations
summary: In large-scale distributed scenarios, increasingly complex tasks demand more intelligent collaboration across networks, requiring the joint extraction of structural representations from data samples. However, conventional task-specific approaches often result in nonstructural embeddings, leading to collapsed variability among data samples within the same class, particularly in classification tasks. To address this issue and fully leverage the intrinsic structure of data for downstream applications, we propose a novel distributed learning framework that ensures both diverse and discriminative representations. For independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) data, we reformulate and decouple the global optimization function by introducing constraints on representation variance. The update rules are then derived and simplified using a primal-dual approach. For non-i.i.d. data distributions, we tackle the problem by clustering and virtually replicating nodes, allowing model updates within each cluster using block coordinate descent. In both cases, the resulting optimal solutions are theoretically proven to maintain discriminative and diverse properties, with a guaranteed convergence for i.i.d. conditions. Additionally, semantic information from representations is shared among nodes, reducing the need for common neural network architectures. Finally, extensive simulations on MNIST, CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 confirm the effectiveness of the proposed algorithms in capturing global structural representations.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a distributed learning framework to ensure diverse and discriminative data representations for large-scale tasks, tested on MNIST, CIFAR-10, and CIFAR-100 datasets.
AI.motivation: To address limitations of task-specific approaches that produce nonstructural embeddings, resulting in collapsed variability in data representations.
AI.method: Used a reformulated global optimization function with variance constraints (for i.i.d. data) and clustering with virtual node replication (for non-i.i.d. data), employing primal-dual and block coordinate descent approaches.
AI.result: Achieved theoretically proven diverse and discriminative solutions, with convergence guaranteed for i.i.d. settings, and demonstrated effectiveness in simulations on standard datasets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework captures global structural representations, reduces reliance on common architectures, and supports better collaboration across distributed networks.

title: STaD: Scaffolded Task Design for Identifying Compositional Skill Gaps in LLMs
summary: Benchmarks are often used as a standard to understand LLM capabilities in different domains. However, aggregate benchmark scores provide limited insight into compositional skill gaps of LLMs and how to improve them. To make these weaknesses visible, we propose Scaffolded Task Design (STaD) framework. STaD generates controlled variations of benchmark tasks based on the concept of scaffolding, which introduces structured, incremental support in a step-by-step manner. Rather than inspecting failures individually, this approach enables systematic and scalable probing of model behavior by identifying the specific reasoning skill compositions they lack. Treating the LLM as a black box, our experiments on six models of varying sizes reveal multiple failure points in three reasoning benchmarks and highlight each model's unique and distinct skill gaps.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the Scaffolded Task Design (STaD) framework to systematically analyze compositional skill gaps in large language models across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Aggregate scores on benchmarks do not offer detailed insight into skill gaps or how to address them. A framework is needed to pinpoint specific reasoning deficits in LLMs.
AI.method: The authors propose STaD, a framework that creates controlled variations of benchmark tasks using structured scaffolding. This approach probes model reasoning skills under varied conditions.
AI.result: Experiments on six LLMs demonstrate STaD's effectiveness in diagnosing unique skill gaps, revealing distinct weaknesses across reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: STaD provides a scalable and systematic approach to expose compositional skill deficits in LLMs, offering valuable insights for improvement strategies.

title: Clinical Note Bloat Reduction for Efficient LLM Use
summary: Health systems are rapidly deploying large language models (LLMs) that use clinical notes for clinical decision support applications. However, modern documentation practices rely heavily on templates, copy--paste shortcuts, and auto-populated fields, producing extensive duplicated text (``note bloat'') that dilutes clinically meaningful signal and substantially increases the computational cost of LLM use. We introduce TRACE, a scalable preprocessing pipeline that removes note bloat by leveraging EHR attribution metadata to identify templated and copied content and applying frequency-based deduplication when metadata are unavailable. We evaluated TRACE across four real--world clinical cohorts spanning liver transplantation, obstetrics, and inpatient care (5.3 million notes) using blinded physician review and downstream modeling tasks. TRACE removed 47.3% of chart text while preserving performance for information extraction and clinical outcome prediction. At a large academic medical center, this reduction corresponds to an estimated $9.5 million annual decrease in LLM inference costs assuming one query per encounter. These findings show how underutilized EHR metadata can enable more scalable and cost-efficient deployment of LLM-based clinical systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TRACE, a pipeline to reduce duplicated text in clinical notes, thereby preserving clinical information while decreasing costs.
AI.motivation: Health systems face inefficiencies and high costs with LLMs due to 'note bloat' from modern documentation practices in electronic health records.
AI.method: The TRACE pipeline uses EHR attribution metadata to detect templated and copied content, and applies frequency-based deduplication when metadata is lacking.
AI.result: TRACE reduced 47.3% of clinical note text without compromising performance in clinical tasks, saving an estimated $9.5 million annually in LLM inference costs.
AI.conclusion: Utilizing EHR metadata through systems like TRACE can significantly enhance the scalability and cost-efficiency of LLM-based clinical tools.

title: Edit Fidelity Field: Semantics-Aware Region Isolation for Training-Free Scene Text Editing
summary: Scene text editing (STE) has achieved remarkable progress in accurately rendering target text through diffusion-based methods. However, we identify a critical yet overlooked problem: edit spillover -- when editing a target text region, existing methods inadvertently modify non-target regions, particularly neighboring text. Through systematic evaluation on 50 real-world scenes across four categories, we reveal that state-of-the-art diffusion editing models exhibit a spillover rate of 94%, meaning nearly all non-target text regions are altered during editing. To address this, we propose the Edit Fidelity Field (EFF), a semantics-aware continuous field that controls per-pixel editing fidelity. Unlike binary masks, EFF leverages OCR-detected text regions to construct a four-zone field: Edit Core (fully editable), Transition Zone (smooth decay), Protected Zone (non-target text, explicitly locked), and Background (strictly preserved). EFF operates as a training-free, model-agnostic post-processing module applicable to any diffusion-based STE method. We further propose per-region spillover quantification, a novel evaluation protocol that measures edit leakage at each non-target text region individually. Experiments demonstrate that EFF reduces spillover rate from 94% to 25% while improving non-target region preservation by +91.4 dB PSNR.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies the problem of edit spillover in scene text editing using diffusion methods and introduces a training-free post-processing module called Edit Fidelity Field (EFF) to reduce this effect and better preserve non-target regions.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to resolve the issue where existing diffusion-based text editing methods unintentionally modify non-target regions, which compromises the fidelity and usability of edited images.
AI.method: The authors propose the Edit Fidelity Field (EFF), a semantic-aware continuous field with zones that define editing boundaries, and introduce per-region spillover quantification for precise evaluation.
AI.result: EFF reduces the spillover rate from 94% to 25%, and improves the PSNR of preserving non-target text regions by +91.4 dB.
AI.conclusion: Integrating EFF significantly enhances the fidelity of scene text editing by reducing unintended alterations in non-target regions, and can be applied to any diffusion-based STE method.

title: Towards Disentangled Preference Optimization Dynamics Beyond Likelihood Displacement
summary:
Preference optimization is widely used to align large language models (LLMs) with human preferences. However, many margin-based objectives suppress the chosen response along with the rejected one, a phenomenon known as likelihood displacement, and no general mechanism currently prevents this across objectives.
  We bridge this gap by presenting a unified \emph{incentive-score decomposition} of preference optimization, revealing that diverse objectives share identical local update directions and differ only in their scalar weighting coefficients.
  Building on this decomposition, by analyzing the dynamics of the chosen/rejected likelihoods, we identify the \emph{disentanglement band} (DB), a simple, testable condition that characterizes when training can avoid likelihood displacement by realizing the preferred pathway: suppressing the loser while maintaining the winner, possibly after an initial transient.
  Leveraging the DB, we propose a plug-and-play \emph{reward calibration} (RC) that adaptively rebalances chosen versus rejected updates to satisfy the DB and mitigate likelihood displacement, without redesigning the base objective.
  Empirical results show that RC steers training toward more disentangled dynamics and often improves downstream performance across a range of objectives. Our code is available at https://github.com/IceyWuu/DisentangledPreferenceOptimization.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the problem of likelihood displacement in preference optimization for LLMs and introduces reward calibration (RC) to prevent it, improving training dynamics and downstream performance.
AI.motivation: Margin-based objectives for preference optimization in LLMs can lead to likelihood displacement, suppressing chosen responses alongside rejected ones, which can hinder proper alignment with human preferences.
AI.method: The authors propose the incentive-score decomposition to analyze commonalities in optimization objectives. They introduce the disentanglement band (DB), a testable condition that avoids likelihood displacement, and a reward calibration (RC) mechanism to optimize updates without modifying base objectives.
AI.result: Through empirical tests, the RC approach is shown to enhance training dynamics, reduce likelihood displacement, and improve model performance across diverse objectives.
