The framework uses four modules: an Explorer agent for vulnerability identification, a RAG engine retrieving relevant knowledge from curated databases including Google Project Zero reports and CWE entries, an Analyst agent for impact and exploitation assessment, and a Reporter agent for structured report generation. To ensure quality, RAVEN includes a task specific LLM Judge evaluating reports across structural integrity, ground truth alignment, code reasoning quality, and remediation quality. We evaluate RAVEN on 105 vulnerable code samples covering 15 CWE types from the NIST-SARD dataset. Results show an average quality score of 54.21%, supporting the effectiveness of our approach for automated vulnerability documentation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RAVEN, a framework combining LLMs and RAG for automatically generating vulnerability analysis reports, achieving promising results.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of automated systems for comprehensive vulnerability report documentation and analysis using LLM capabilities.
AI.method: RAVEN framework includes four modules: Explorer agent for vulnerability identification, RAG engine for retrieving knowledge, Analyst agent for impact/exploitation assessment, and Reporter agent for report generation. A task-specific LLM Judge evaluates report quality.
AI.result: RAVEN was tested on 105 vulnerable code samples spanning 15 CWE types, achieving an average quality score of 54.21%, demonstrating its effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: RAVEN shows potential in automating vulnerability documentation, paving the way for enhanced cybersecurity workflows.

title: Latent Fourier Transform
summary: We introduce the Latent Fourier Transform (LatentFT), a framework that provides novel frequency-domain controls for generative music models. LatentFT combines a diffusion autoencoder with a latent-space Fourier transform to separate musical patterns by timescale. By masking latents in the frequency domain during training, our method yields representations that can be manipulated coherently at inference. This allows us to generate musical variations and blends from reference examples while preserving characteristics at desired timescales, which are specified as frequencies in the latent space. LatentFT parallels the role of the equalizer in music production: while traditional equalizers operates on audible frequencies to shape timbre, LatentFT operates on latent-space frequencies to shape musical structure. Experiments and listening tests show that LatentFT improves condition adherence and quality compared to baselines. We also present a technique for hearing frequencies in the latent space in isolation, and show different musical attributes reside in different regions of the latent spectrum. Our results show how frequency-domain control in latent space provides an intuitive, continuous frequency axis for conditioning and blending, advancing us toward more interpretable and interactive generative music models.

AI.tldr: LatentFT introduces a framework using frequency-domain controls in latent space for generative music models to enhance musical variation, coherence, and interpretability.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the lack of intuitive control and interpretability in current generative music models, focusing on latent-space frequency manipulation.
AI.method: A diffusion autoencoder paired with latent-space Fourier transforms, allowing musical patterns to be controlled and manipulated via frequencies in latent space.
AI.result: LatentFT improves condition adherence and quality compared to baselines, and demonstrates distinct musical attributes residing within specific latent frequency spectrums.
AI.conclusion: LatentFT provides a novel approach to music generation by enabling intuitive frequency-domain control in latent space, akin to a latent equalizer for shaping musical structure.

title: First, Do No Harm (With LLMs): Mitigating Racial Bias via Agentic Workflows
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used in clinical settings, raising concerns about racial bias in both generated medical text and clinical reasoning. Existing studies have identified bias in medical LLMs, but many focus on single models and give less attention to mitigation. This study uses the EU AI Act as a governance lens to evaluate five widely used LLMs across two tasks, namely synthetic patient-case generation and differential diagnosis ranking. Using race-stratified epidemiological distributions in the United States and expert differential diagnosis lists as benchmarks, we apply structured prompt templates and a two-part evaluation design to examine implicit and explicit racial bias. All models deviated from observed racial distributions in the synthetic case generation task, with GPT-4.1 showing the smallest overall deviation. In the differential diagnosis task, DeepSeek V3 produced the strongest overall results across the reported metrics. When embedded in an agentic workflow, DeepSeek V3 showed an improvement of 0.0348 in mean p-value, 0.1166 in median p-value, and 0.0949 in mean difference relative to the standalone model, although improvement was not uniform across every metric. These findings support multi-metric bias evaluation for AI systems used in medical settings and suggest that retrieval-based agentic workflows may reduce some forms of explicit bias in benchmarked diagnostic tasks. Detailed prompt templates, experimental datasets, and code pipelines are available on our GitHub.

AI.tldr: The study evaluates racial bias in widely used medical LLMs for synthetic patient-case generation and differential diagnosis tasks, finding mitigation methods within specific workflows.
AI.motivation: Racial bias in the medical application of LLMs can lead to inequities in care and unintended harm, prompting analysis and mitigation.
AI.method: The paper assesses five LLMs using structured prompt templates and race-stratified benchmarks, examining implicit and explicit bias across synthetic case generation and diagnostic tasks.
AI.result: GPT-4.1 exhibited the least bias in case generation, while DeepSeek V3 produced the top performance in diagnostic tasks and improved bias metrics when part of an agentic workflow.
AI.conclusion: Multi-metric evaluations and retrieval-based agentic workflows are effective tools to identify and mitigate racial bias in medical LLM applications.

title: Committed SAE-Feature Traces for Audited-Session Substitution Detection in Hosted LLMs
summary: Hosted-LLM providers have a silent-substitution incentive: advertise a stronger model while serving cheaper replies. Probe-after-return schemes such as SVIP leave a parallel-serve side-channel, since a dishonest provider can route the verifier's probe to the advertised model while serving ordinary users from a substitute. We propose a commit-open protocol that closes this gap. Before any opening request, the provider commits via a Merkle tree to a per-position sparse-autoencoder (SAE) feature-trace sketch of its served output at a published probe layer. A verifier opens random positions, scores them against a public named-circuit probe library calibrated with cross-backend noise, and decides with a fixed-threshold joint-consistency z-score rule. We instantiate the protocol on three backbones -- Qwen3-1.7B, Gemma-2-2B, and a 4.5x scale-up to Gemma-2-9B with a 131k-feature SAE. Of 17 attackers spanning same-family lifts, cross-family substitutes, and rank-<=128 adaptive LoRA, all are rejected at a shared, scale-stable threshold; the same attackers all evade a matched SVIP-style parallel-serve baseline. A white-box end-to-end attack that backpropagates through the frozen SAE encoder does not close the margin, and a feature-forgery attacker that never runs M_hon is bounded in closed form by an intrinsic-dimension argument. Commitment adds <=2.1% to forward-only wall-clock at batch 32.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the issue of hosted language model (LLM) providers potentially advertising one model while secretly serving a cheaper model. It proposes a commit-open protocol using Merkle trees and sparse-autoencoder feature-trace sketches to verify model honesty.
AI.motivation: There is a risk that hosted LLM providers may dishonestly substitute advertised models with cheaper versions, undermining trust and reliability in model outputs.
AI.method: The proposed solution uses a commit-open protocol where the provider commits to a per-position feature-trace sketch in a Merkle tree. Verifiers assess random positions against a public probe library using a fixed-threshold z-score to detect dishonesty.
AI.result: The protocol was effective across various tested models (Qwen3-1.7B, Gemma-2-2B, Gemma-2-9B) in rejecting 17 types of attackers that evaded traditional detection methods like SVIP-style baselines. It also resisted advanced white-box and feature-forgery attacks.
AI.conclusion: The presented commit-open protocol is a robust, scalable, and cost-efficient solution to ensure the integrity of hosted LLM responses, addressing silent-substitution incentives without significant computational overhead.

title: Aether: Network Validation Using Agentic AI and Digital Twin
summary: Network change validation remains a critical yet predominantly manual, time-consuming, and error-prone process in modern network operations. While formal network verification has made substantial progress in proving correctness properties, it is typically applied in offline, pre-deployment settings and faces challenges in accommodating continuous changes and validating live production behavior. Current operational approaches typically involve scattered testing tools, resulting in partial coverage and errors that surface only after deployment. In this paper, we present Aether, a novel approach that integrates Generative Agentic AI with a multi-functional Network Digital Twin to automate and streamline network change validation workflows. It features an agentic architecture with five specialized Network Operations AI agents that collaboratively handle the change validation lifecycle from intent analysis to network verification and testing. Aether agents use a unified Network Digital Twin integrating modeling, simulation, and emulation to maintain a consistent, up-to-date network view for verification and testing. By orchestrating agent collaboration atop this digital twin, Aether enables automated, rapid network change validation while reducing manual effort, minimizing errors, and improving operational agility and cost-effectiveness. We evaluate Aether over synthetic network change scenarios covering main classes of network changes and on past incidents from a major ISP operational network, demonstrating promising results in error detection (100%), diagnostic coverage (92-96%), and speed (6-7 minutes) over traditional methods.

AI.tldr: Aether integrates AI and a Network Digital Twin for automated, accurate, and fast network change validation.
AI.motivation: Network change validation is cumbersome and error-prone with current manual and fragmented methods, demanding improved solutions for continuous and live network validation.
AI.method: Aether utilizes Generative Agentic AI and a unified Network Digital Twin, employing five specialized AI agents to manage the lifecycle of network change validation.
AI.result: Promising improvements include 100% error detection, 92-96% diagnostic coverage, and validation speeds of 6-7 minutes tested on various scenarios and incidents.
AI.conclusion: Aether enhances network change validation by integrating AI and simulation, reducing errors, manual workload, and improving operational agility and efficiency.

title: Evaluating Multi-Hop Reasoning in RAG Systems: A Comparison of LLM-Based Retriever Evaluation Strategies
comment: 15 Pages, Accepted for publication at the SynIRgy Workshop, ECIR 2026 (48th European Conference on Information Retrieval)
summary:
Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) enhances large language models (LLMs) with external knowledge to answer questions more accurately.
  However, research on evaluating RAG systems-particularly the retriever component-remains limited, as most existing work focuses on single-context retrieval rather than multi-hop queries, where individual contexts may appear irrelevant in isolation but are essential when combined. In this research, we use the HotPotQA, MuSiQue, and SQuAD datasets to simulate a RAG system and compare three LLM-as-judge evaluation strategies, including our proposed Context-Aware Retriever Evaluation (CARE). Our goal is to better understand how multi-hop reasoning can be most effectively evaluated in RAG systems.
  Experiments with LLMs from OpenAI, Meta, and Google demonstrate that CARE consistently outperforms existing methods for evaluating multi-hop reasoning in RAG systems. The performance gains are most pronounced in models with larger parameter counts and longer context windows, while single-hop queries show minimal sensitivity to context-aware evaluation. Overall, the results highlight the critical role of context-aware evaluation in improving the reliability and accuracy of retrieval-augmented generation systems, particularly in complex query scenarios. To ensure reproducibility, we provide the complete data of our experiments at https://github.com/lorenzbrehme/CARE.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new evaluation strategy for multi-hop reasoning in Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems using the Context-Aware Retriever Evaluation (CARE) method, which outperforms existing techniques.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation methods for RAG systems focus on single-context retrieval and fail to address the challenges of multi-hop queries where combined contexts are essential.
AI.method: The authors simulate a RAG system using datasets like HotPotQA, MuSiQue, and SQuAD, and compare CARE against other LLM-as-judge evaluation strategies.
AI.result: CARE consistently performed better than existing evaluation methods, particularly in models with larger parameters and longer context windows.
AI.conclusion: Context-aware evaluation, like CARE, is crucial for improving the accuracy and reliability of RAG systems when handling complex queries.

title: Dissecting AI Trading: Behavioral Finance and Market Bubbles
summary: We study how AI agents form expectations and trade in experimental asset markets. Using a simulated open-call auction populated by autonomous Large Language Model (LLM) agents, we document three main findings. First, AI agents exhibit classic behavioral patterns: a pronounced disposition effect and recency-weighted extrapolative beliefs. Second, these individual-level patterns aggregate into equilibrium dynamics that replicate classic experimental findings (Smith et al., 1988), including the predictive power of excess demand for future prices and the positive relationship between disagreement and trading volume. Third, by analyzing the agents' reasoning text through a twenty-mechanism scoring framework, we show that targeted prompt interventions causally amplify or suppress specific behavioral mechanisms, significantly altering the magnitude of market bubbles.

AI.tldr: This study explores how AI agents, based on Large Language Models (LLMs), form expectations and participate in experimental asset markets, replicating behavioral and market equilibrium tendencies.
AI.motivation: The study aims to understand whether AI agents, in the form of LLMs, can replicate human-like behavioral biases in financial market settings and how their interactions reproduce classic market behaviors.
AI.method: The researchers implemented a simulated open-call auction where AI agents based on LLMs traded assets and analyzed their behavior using a twenty-mechanism scoring framework.
AI.result: AI agents demonstrated behavioral tendencies like the disposition effect and recency-weighted beliefs, collectively leading to classic market dynamics, including bubbles. Prompt interventions successfully altered the agents' behaviors.
AI.conclusion: AI agents can effectively simulate both individual behavioral biases and aggregate market dynamics, offering insights into market behavior and potential manipulations via prompts.

title: Symbolic Synthesis for LTLf+ Obligations
summary: We study synthesis for obligation properties expressed in LTLfp, the extension of LTLf to infinite traces. Obligation properties are positive Boolean combinations of safety and guarantee (co-safety) properties and form the second level of the temporal hierarchy of Manna and Pnueli. Although obligation properties are expressed over infinite traces, they retain most of the simplicity of LTLf. In particular, we show that they admit a translation into symbolically represented deterministic weak automata (DWA) obtained directly from the symbolic deterministic finite automata (DFA) for the underlying LTLf properties on trace prefixes. DWA inherit many of the attractive algorithmic features of DFA, including Boolean closure and polynomial-time minimization. Moreover, we show that synthesis for LTLfp obligation properties is theoretically highly efficient - solvable in linear time once the DWA is constructed. We investigate several symbolic algorithms for solving DWA games that arise in the synthesis of obligation properties and evaluate their effectiveness experimentally. Overall, the results indicate that synthesis for LTLfp obligation properties can be performed with virtually the same effectiveness as LTLf synthesis.

AI.tldr: The paper explores synthesis for obligation properties in LTLfp, an extension of LTLf, and demonstrates its efficiency and simplicity for infinite traces through symbolic automata methods.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of synthesizing obligation properties, a blend of safety and guarantee properties, in the context of infinite traces with theoretical efficiency and algorithmic simplicity.
AI.method: The authors utilized symbolic deterministic weak automata derived from deterministic finite automata for synthesis, ensuring Boolean closure, polynomial-time minimization, and efficient algorithms for solving DWA games.
AI.result: Synthesis of LTLfp obligation properties was shown to be linear-time efficient at the theoretical level and experimentally effective, thanks to symbolic algorithms for DWA games.
AI.conclusion: Synthesis for obligation properties in LTLfp retains the simplicity and effectiveness of LTLf synthesis while being highly efficient even for infinite traces.
